383. Osteoporosis
Systemic
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 names the answer outright ('diagnosed with osteoporosis during bone density screening'), making all subsequent clues pointless. The game should start with symptomatology and functional loss, then progressively reveal history, exam findings, and finally imaging confirmation. Currently, Clues 2-4 are redundant elaborations of the osteoporosis picture rather than new discriminatory information. A proper sequence would: (1) present vague symptoms in an older adult; (2) describe functional decline and fracture pattern; (3) reveal risk factors narrowing to bone health concerns; (4) add physical exam findings suggestive of skeletal fragility; (5) confirm with imaging. The rewritten clues attempt to restore the funneling logic without explicitly naming the diagnosis until imaging.

Shoulder
NEEDS WORK

Clues 2 and 3 merely reiterate the same information (loss of function, pain, fall, immediate onset, osteoporosis) without adding differential value. By Clue 3, the answer is obvious and Clue 4 is redundant. The progressive reveal principle is violated: each clue should eliminate possibilities, but these clues keep circling the same facts. Recommend: (1) Remove 'fall' from Clue 1 to broaden the differential to non-traumatic shoulder pathology; (2) Use Clue 2 to establish the mechanism was trauma (fall/FOOSH); (3) Use Clue 3 to narrow with age + osteoporosis risk; (4) Use Clue 4 to differentiate fracture from severe soft tissue injury via specific exam findings (e.g., crepitus, deformity, axial load pain); (5) Use Clue 5 to confirm with imaging. Symptoms are triggered by deep hip flexion (forward folds), rotation movements, and side-lying on the symptomatic side. Pain resolves with prone lying, supine positions, and activities that stabilize the pelvis such as walking on level ground.
UPDATED
Pain worsens with asymmetric loading tasks (single-leg stance, unilateral hip extension). Relieved by symmetrical positions and activities that distribute load evenly across both sides.
💡 Functional pattern: deep hip flexion and rotation movements aggravate; prone/supine and stable activities relieve

POOR

398. Cubital Tunnel Syndrome
Elbow
GOOD
Review Notes: This is a well-constructed progressive reveal. Clue 1 casts a broad net (hand paresthesias, nocturnal, insidious—could be carpal tunnel, cervical, brachial plexus, other neuropathies). Clue 2 adds functional motor involvement and position-specific reproducibility, narrowing toward mechanical compression. Clue 3 adds occupational and postural mechanism plus the morning worsening clue (sleeping with elbow bent), which is highly specific to cubital tunnel. Clue 4 provides examination findings that pinpoint the medial elbow and ulnar nerve specifically (Tinel's, Froment's, atrophy pattern, sensory loss pattern). Clue 5 confirms with imaging showing nerve swelling at the compression site. Each clue meaningfully narrows the differential and adds distinct clinical information. The puzzle respects the progressive reveal principle.
Clue 1
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
A 42-year-old warehouse worker presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the hand that worsens at night and affects sleep quality. The symptoms began insidiously 3 months ago without preceding trauma. Pain radiates from the elbow down to the ring and little fingers.
✓ No changes
💡 Establishes broad symptom pattern: progressive paresthesias in hand distribution, nocturnal worsening, insidious onset without trauma, 3-month timeline. Mentions elbow and ulnar-distribution fingers but does not yet specify anatomy or mechanism.
Clue 2
Activity
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
The patient reports difficulty gripping tools at work and has dropped several items in the past month. Prolonged elbow flexion during computer work exacerbates symptoms, while extending the arm and shaking the hand provides temporary relief.
✓ No changes
💡 Adds functional context: weakness with gripping and dropping (motor involvement), position-specific provocation (elbow flexion worsens, extension/shaking relieves). This narrows to peripheral nerve compression affecting ulnar nerve territory and introduces mechanical reproducibility.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
The patient works assembly lines requiring repetitive elbow flexion. Past medical history includes rheumatoid arthritis controlled on medication. The patient denies neck pain or shoulder involvement. Symptoms are worse in the morning after sleeping with the elbow bent.
✓ No changes
💡 Adds occupational and postural context (repetitive elbow flexion, assembly line work), comorbidity (rheumatoid arthritis—relevant for nerve compression risk), and critical temporal clue (worse in morning after sleeping with elbow bent). Narrows to compression neuropathy at elbow rather than cervical or more proximal pathology. Still leaves brachial plexus, pronator syndrome, or other elbow-region compressions open.
Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Tinel's sign is positive at the medial elbow. Froment's sign demonstrates weakness of thumb adduction. Intrinsic hand muscle atrophy is visible between thumb and index finger. Two-point discrimination is diminished in the ulnar distribution compared to the contralateral side.
✓ No changes
💡 Provides objective nerve compression signs: Tinel's sign localises to medial elbow specifically; Froment's sign indicates ulnar-innervated intrinsic weakness (adductor pollicis); thenar atrophy and diminished two-point discrimination in ulnar distribution confirm ulnar nerve involvement distally. This strongly implicates ulnar nerve compression at the elbow over other upper limb neuropathies or proximal lesions.
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Ultrasound imaging shows enlargement and hypoechoic appearance of the ulnar nerve at the medial elbow, consistent with nerve swelling. The nerve measures 0.7 cm in cross-sectional area at the compression site, well above normal limits of 0.4 cm.
✓ No changes
💡 Directly visualises the pathology: nerve enlargement and hypoechoic swelling at the medial elbow, with quantified cross-sectional area (0.7 cm vs normal 0.4 cm) confirming compression. This is near-definitive for cubital tunnel syndrome and excludes other differential diagnoses such as Guyon's canal syndrome, brachial plexus pathology, or cervical radiculopathy.

Knee
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle suffers from significant redundancy between Clues 1 and 2. Clue 1 already names the anatomical location ('tibial tuberosity below the kneecap') and activity trigger, which is overly specific and narrows the differential prematurely. Clue 2 then merely elaborates on the same activity triggers without adding substantively new information. A player reading only Clue 1 could already narrow to Osgood-Schlatter or juvenile patellar tendinopathy. The rewrite for Clue 1 removes anatomical specificity (keeping only 'anterior knee pain') to broaden the initial differential. The rewrite for Clue 2 shifts focus to FUNCTIONAL LIMITATION (kneeling pain, sitting tolerance) which is clinically useful information that doesn't repeat Clue 1. Clues 3–5 are well-structured and appropriately progressive.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easy
ORIGINAL

UPDATED

400. Exertional Rhabdomyolysis
Muscle
POOR
Review Notes: Critical failure: Clue 1 essentially names the answer ('exertional rhabdomyolysis') by combining extreme exertion + muscle pain + weakness + dark urine + immediate onset in one sentence. A player who knows basic pathophysiology will guess correctly after Clue 1 alone. Clues 2–5 heavily overlap in content (myoglobinuria, kidney injury, CK elevation all mentioned multiple times across different clues). The puzzle lacks true progressive narrowing; instead it repeatedly restates the same pathological cascade. For a proper funnel: Clue 1 should describe only pain/weakness with exertion (broad differential). Clue 2 should introduce the dark urine finding (narrows to rhabdomyolysis family). Clue 3 should contextualize with heat/unaccustomed exertion (supports rhabdo over other causes). Clue 4 should differentiate via examination (palpable swelling, CK >5000). Clue 5 should confirm with myoglobin + AKI pattern.

401. Vestibular Migraine
Vestibular
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a critical structural flaw: Clue 1 already mentions 'migraine headache history' and 'vertigo episodes occurring with or separate from headache', which together are highly suggestive of vestibular migraine. Clue 2 adds almost nothing new—balance impairment and nausea are symptoms of vertigo itself, not distinct new information. Clue 3 repeats the same history already established in Clue 1 without adding meaningful discriminatory detail. The progressive reveal principle is violated; the player learns little new from Clues 2 and 3. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriate but arrive too late in a game where the answer is already apparent. Recommend rewriting Clues 2 and 3 to add: specific functional limitations (e.g., gait patterns affected), timeline (episode duration, frequency), and trigger pattern. This would create a proper differential funnel: vertigo + migraine history (broad) → balance/gait-specific limitations + triggers (medium specificity) → nystagmus on exam (narrowing) → normal imaging (confirmation).

Clue 4 confirms adduction pain. Clue 5 seals it with imaging. Progression is logical and each clue meaningfully narrows. No major issues; puzzle is playable and educationally sound.

404. Facet Joint Syndrome
Spine
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has structural problems in the middle clues. Clue 2 (Activity) does good work narrowing movement patterns. However, Clue 3 (History) adds almost nothing new — it merely elaborates on the 'mechanical, activity-related' character already established in Clues 1–2, without distinguishing facet joint syndrome from disc herniation, ligamentous strain, or other mechanical spine pathology. Clue 4 then compounds this by repeating the movement-specific findings from Clue 2 (extension + ipsilateral rotation) before introducing facet tenderness. The result is poor progressive narrowing: a clinician has barely refined their differential by Clue 4. Rewriting Clue 3 to include information about absence of radicular signs, dermatomal pattern, or neurological involvement would meaningfully differentiate facet joint syndrome (often with pseudo-radicular pain and preserved neuro exam) from true nerve root compression. Rewriting Clue 4 to focus on palpation, end-feel, and differentiation of flexion/extension/rotation (rather than repeating extension/rotation) would create a meaningful distinction. The puzzle is solvable but lacks the clinical reasoning 'aha' moments at Clues 3–4 that make Physiodle engaging.

Clues 4 and 5 are strong and appropriately specific.

407. Pelvic Floor Dysfunction
Pelvis
GOOD
Review Notes: Clue progression is sound. Each clue adds genuinely new information without overlap: Clue 1 establishes symptoms broadly; Clue 2 adds functional/postural detail and treatment failure; Clue 3 provides mechanism (obstetric injury) and timeline; Clue 4 confirms weakness and rules out pure overactivity via objective findings; Clue 5 provides quantitative structural evidence (hypermobility + atrophy). Differential narrows appropriately from 10+ conditions (stress incontinence, OAB, pelvic pain, post-partum dysfunction) → pelvic region + functional limitation → mechanical injury from vaginal delivery → motor deficit confirmed → structural atrophy confirmed. This is a well-constructed puzzle with clear clinical reasoning path. No rewrites needed.
Clue 1
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
A 38-year-old female presents with difficulty controlling bladder and bowel function that has progressed over 8 months. She reports urgency and occasional incontinence during exercise, coughing, or sneezing. There is also pain with sexual intercourse.
✓ No changes
💡 Establishes broad symptom cluster (incontinence, urgency, dyspareunia) with timeline and triggers (exercise, cough, sneeze). Sets wide differential including stress incontinence, overactive bladder, pelvic pain syndromes, post-partum sequelae.
Clue 2
Activity
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
The patient has modified her routine to avoid jumping or running due to urine leakage. She performs Kegel exercises but reports poor muscle control. Prolonged sitting worsens pelvic discomfort, while lying down relieves symptoms. She avoids tampon use due to discomfort.
✓ No changes
💡 Functional impact narrows to lower urinary tract and pelvic region. NEW: postural component (prolonged sitting worsens, lying relieves) and failed conservative management (Kegel exercises ineffective). Distinguishes from pure behavioural incontinence or overactive bladder alone.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

✓ No changes

Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Internal palpation reveals weak muscle contraction during requested pelvic floor squeeze, graded 2/5. A cough stress test produces leakage before pelvic floor contraction occurs. Tender trigger points are noted in the pelvic floor muscles bilaterally during assessment.
✓ No changes
💡 Objective findings distinguish structural weakness from overactivity: graded pelvic floor weakness (2/5), loss of reflex contractility on cough stress test, and trigger point tenderness. NEW: confirms motor deficit and myofascial component. Rules out pure overactive bladder or neurogenic causes.
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Pelvic ultrasound demonstrates descent of the bladder base beyond 5 cm on Valsalva maneuver, indicating hypermobility. Pelvic floor thickness measures 0.6 cm on endovaginal ultrasound, below the normal range of 0.8-1.2 cm, confirming atrophy and dysfunction.
✓ No changes
💡 Quantifies hypermobility (bladder descent >5 cm on Valsalva) and confirms tissue atrophy (0.6 cm thickness vs normal 0.8–1.2 cm). Near-definitive: imaging evidence of structural deficiency and denervation-related atrophy consistent with post-partum pelvic floor dysfunction.
408. Burns Rehabilitation
Various
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle has a fundamental structural problem: it names the answer ('Burns Rehabilitation') in Clue 1, eliminating any element of diagnostic reasoning. The puzzle reads as a description of burns rehabilitation rather than a clinical reasoning challenge. To fix this, Clue 1 must present only pain/stiffness/functional loss from an unspecified cause. Clue 2 should narrow to skin/tissue involvement through functional loss pattern. Clue 3 should introduce the mechanism (thermal exposure) and timeline. Clue 4 should differentiate burn from other skin injuries via examination findings. Clue 5 should confirm burn classification and scar type via imaging. The current version fails the core game principle: progressive reveal that narrows a differential, not progressive elaboration of a single known diagnosis.

409. Clavicle Fracture
Shoulder
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 essentially names the diagnosis by mentioning 'visible deformity over the clavicle' — a physio student reading this immediately thinks 'clavicle fracture' and the subsequent clues merely confirm what was already given. Clues 2 and 3 add almost no new discriminatory information beyond Clue 1. Clue 4 repeats the visible deformity already mentioned in Clue 1. The differential funnel never narrows because there is no differential — the answer is apparent from the first clue. A proper revision must: (1) remove 'visible deformity over the clavicle' from Clue 1 and describe only the pain pattern and mechanism broadly; (2) use Clue 2 to narrow to shoulder/upper limb via functional loss; (3) use Clue 3 to clarify the specific injury mechanism; (4) use Clue 4 to provide objective signs that distinguish clavicle fracture from other shoulder injuries (e.g., AC joint separation, proximal humerus fracture, rotator cuff tear); (5) use Clue 5 for imaging confirmation.

410. Lumbar Disc Herniation
Lumbar Spine
GOOD
Review Notes: This is a well-constructed puzzle with strong progressive reveal. Clue 1 casts a wide net (acute LBP + radiculopathy could be discitis, radiculopathy from various causes, facet syndrome with referred pain, stenosis). Clue 2 narrows to nerve root involvement via postural and duration-dependent patterns and response to supine elevation. Clue 3 adds critical history that this is NEW radiculopathy in a patient with chronic intermittent LBP—ruling out chronic stenosis as sole cause and supporting acute disc herniation. Clue 4 provides the definitive neurological signature of L5 nerve root compression, eliminating other differentials (e.g., myofascial pain, facet-mediated referred pain, piriformis syndrome). Clue 5 confirms the diagnosis with imaging. Each clue meaningfully narrows the differential without repetition. Difficulty is appropriate: a student with strong clinical reasoning could potentially narrow to disc herniation by Clue 4, but Clue 5 removes ambiguity. No major weaknesses.
Clue 1
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
A 52-year-old construction supervisor presents with severe lower back pain that began suddenly while lifting a heavy load 2 weeks ago. The pain radiates down the left leg into the foot with associated numbness in the foot. Sitting and bending forward intensely worsen the pain.
✓ No changes
💡 Establishes acute-on-chronic lower back pain with radicular leg pain and sensory symptoms; age, occupation, and mechanism (lifting) provided; identifies left leg involvement and postural exacerbators.
Clue 2
Activity
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
The patient cannot sit for more than 15 minutes without severe radiating pain. Lying flat with the left leg elevated provides mild relief. Bending forward to pick up objects triggers sharp shooting pain down the left leg. Light walking is tolerated better than sitting or standing still.
✓ No changes
💡 Specifies functional limitations tied to posture and duration; distinguishes sitting intolerance (15 min max) from walking tolerance; establishes that lying supine with leg elevation is therapeutic—narrow the differential to nerve root involvement.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
The patient has a 10-year history of intermittent lower back pain managed with NSAIDs and physical therapy. He reports smoking one pack daily and spends 8 hours per day in a vehicle for work. No prior episodes of leg pain. Family history includes degenerative disc disease in his father.
✓ No changes
💡 Adds chronicity context (10-year history of intermittent LBP), risk factors (smoking, prolonged sitting in vehicle), absence of prior radiculopathy (rules out chronic recurrent nerve root compression), and family history of degenerative disc disease. Clarifies this is a NEW presentation of radicular symptoms despite prior back pain.
Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Straight leg raise test is positive on the left at 35 degrees with reproduction of radiating pain. Ankle dorsiflexion weakness is demonstrated as 4/5 on the left. Sensory testing shows decreased sensation in the L5 dermatome on the left foot. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are intact bilaterally.
✓ No changes
💡 Provides objective findings that confirm nerve root involvement: positive SLR at 35° (mechanical radiculopathy), L5 motor deficit (ankle dorsiflexion 4/5), L5 dermatomal sensory loss, and preserved reflexes. This clinches L5 nerve root as the affected structure and narrows to disc herniation, foraminal stenosis, or mass lesion compressing L5.
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
MRI of the lumbar spine reveals a large posterolateral disc herniation at L4-L5 with significant compression of the left L5 nerve root. The herniated disc material is noted to be sequestered, and there is associated left-sided foraminal stenosis at the level of involvement.
✓ No changes
💡 Confirms posterolateral L4–L5 disc herniation with L5 nerve root compression and sequestration; establishes the specific anatomical pathology. This is definitive for lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy.

417. Erb's Palsy
Upper Limb
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle has severe progressive reveal problems. Clue 1 immediately names the mechanism (shoulder dystocia) and gives away it's a birth injury—a student reading only this could guess Erb's palsy outright. Clues 2 and 3 then redundantly reinforce the same information (arm weakness, immediate post-birth, shoulder dystocia, absent reflex). There is essentially no meaningful narrowing from clue to clue. The differential is never opened—it collapses at clue 1. Reordering and rewriting are essential: move the mechanism (shoulder dystocia) to Clue 3 (History), broaden Clue 1 to describe only symptoms/presentation without mechanism, sharpen Clue 2 to add functional detail beyond weakness, and tighten Clue 3 to add temporal and mechanistic context. Clue 4 should discriminate between C5/C6 (Erb's) and C8/T1 (Klumpke's) involvement—the waiter's tip and preserved hand function already do this, but could be more explicit about what is NOT weak.

418. Anterior Cerebral Artery Stroke
Brain
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 explicitly names 'anterior cerebral artery territory,' essentially giving away the answer on the first guess. Clues 2 and 4 are largely redundant with Clue 1, both emphasizing the same contralateral lower extremity weakness pattern without adding discriminatory information. Clue 3 names 'acute stroke event,' which further collapses the differential prematurely. By Clue 3, a player has already learned the diagnosis is an acute stroke in ACA territory—there is no meaningful differential narrowing happening. A Physiodle puzzle should challenge players to think through multiple possibilities (other acute neurological conditions, stroke in other territories, upper motor neuron lesions from non-vascular causes, etc.). Instead, this puzzle hands the answer away in pieces across five clues. Rewrites should remove explicit vascular territory language from Clue 1, add genuine functional specificity in Clue 2, provide realistic history/risk stratification in Clue 3, and use specific examination findings (reflexes, plantar responses, cognitive screening) in Clue 4 to differentiate ACA from other stroke territories.

The progressive reveal funnel is broken: Clue 1 → Clue 2 → Clue 3 should narrow from ~10 diagnoses to ~2–3, but instead Clues 1–2 cover overlapping ground, and Clue 3 spoils the answer. 439. Supraspinatous Tendon Calcification
Shoulder
GOOD
Review Notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 establishes broad shoulder pain in overhead athlete with trauma. Clue 2 adds functional overhead-specific limitations. Clue 3 provides mechanism (trauma) and timeline (8 months) that support acute calcification rather than chronic degenerative tear. Clue 4 narrows to rotator cuff/supraspinatus impingement syndrome via clinical examination. Clue 5 confirms calcific tendinopathy as the specific pathology. Each clue meaningfully narrows the differential without redundancy. The puzzle is well-constructed: not too easy (Clue 1–2 alone don't name calcification), but follows a logical clinical reasoning path. No major issues.
Clue 1
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
A 58-year-old former tennis player presents with progressively worsening shoulder pain for 8 months, particularly when lifting the arm overhead. Pain is located over the top of the shoulder and radiates down the lateral arm. Sudden acute flare-ups occur with minor overhead movements.

Clue 2
Activity
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Overhead reaching activities, particularly reaching behind the head, provoke sharp pain and weakness. Carrying groceries with the affected arm is no longer possible due to pain and weakness. ✓ No changes
💡 Functional limitations now specified: overhead reaching (especially behind head), carrying, supine lying. Pattern of temporary relief with rest/ice but rapid symptom return. Narrows to overhead-sensitive conditions; adds functional impact distinct from pain complaint alone.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
The patient played competitive tennis for 30 years, retiring 10 years ago. She reports a fall on the outstretched hand 8 months ago that preceded current symptoms. Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Abduction of the arm reproduces sharp pain, particularly between 60-120 degrees. Supraspinatus strength testing shows weakness with painful arc. 💡 Painful arc 60–120°, positive Hawkins and Neer signs, supraspinatus weakness with pain, infraspinatus normal (5/5), tenderness at supraspinatus insertion. Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

CT imaging shows a calcification measuring 12 mm with a heterogeneous appearance located within the supraspinatus tendon. The calcification is located 8 mm lateral to the humeral head articular surface margin.
✓ No changes

This is pathognomonic for calcific tendinopathy (supraspinatus), distinguishing it from other supraspinatus pathology (pure tear, degeneration without calcification).

On examination, pain is reproduced with palpation along the medial longitudinal arch. Windlass test produces marked pain with dorsiflexion of the hallux. Ankle plantarflexion range is normal; however, dorsiflexion is limited to 8 degrees bilaterally.
✓ No changes
💡 Medial longitudinal arch palpation tenderness (localizes pathology to plantar fascia insertion zone), windlass test positive with pain on hallux dorsiflexion (confirms plantar fascia load/tension), ankle dorsiflexion limited to 8° bilaterally (identifies calf tightness as biomechanical contributor), plantarflexion normal (rules out gastrocnemius-soleus injury). NEW: Differentiates plantar fasciitis from heel pad syndrome, metatarsal stress fracture, tarsal tunnel, or systemic arthropathy by specific tissue response and ROM pattern.
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
MRI reveals chronic thickening of the plantar fascia measuring 5.1 mm at the insertion point with heterogeneous signal intensity. Small calcaneal heel spur is visible. Marrow signal intensity at the calcaneal tuberosity is mildly altered, consistent with chronic inflammation.
✓ No changes
💡 Fascia thickness 5.1 mm (exceeds normal ~2–3 mm), heterogeneous signal (degenerative/chronic), calcaneal spur present, marrow edema at insertion. NEW: Confirms structural pathology; rules out functional/inflammatory conditions without fascial thickening; establishes chronicity and severity; essentially confirms diagnosis.
442. Femoral Nerve Palsy
Lower Limb
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major problem: Clue 1 is far too specific and anatomy-focused for a 'Complaint' clue. It explicitly mentions 'thigh weakness', 'hip flexion and knee extension' — this is examination-level specificity that essentially names the nerve and body region. A player reading only Clue 1 would immediately think 'femoral nerve' or at least 'proximal lower limb neuropathy', eliminating 90% of the differential. Clue 1 should be broader, mentioning only symptoms (weakness, heaviness, numbness) without anatomical specificity. Clue 2 (Activity) is largely redundant with Clue 1 — it elaborates on walking difficulty and stair-climbing, but the reader already knew from Clue 1 that there was profound thigh weakness affecting hip flexion and knee extension, so the functional limitations are already implied. Clue 2 should add NEW information (e.g., sensory-specific functional impacts, or discovery of night pain as a distinct feature). Clue 4 (Examination) mostly rephrases the same weakness and sensory findings already described in Clues 1 and 2 — it adds only the patellar reflex finding, which is marginal. The progressive reveal fails here: by Clue 3, a clinician already has a narrow differential (likely only femoral neuropathy); Clues 4 and 5 confirm but don't significantly narrow further. Reorder or rewrite Clues 1 and 2 to broaden Clue 1 and make Clue 2 functionally distinct.

too_specific

💡 72-year-old diabetic with 3-month gradual weakness, heaviness, numbness in leg. Thigh-predominant weakness affecting hip flexion and knee extension.

448. Posterior Tibial Tendon Dysfunction Grade 2
Foot
GOOD
Review Notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 casts a wide net (medial ankle pain, arch collapse, instability—could suggest ankle sprain, chronic ankle instability, or tendon pathology). Clue 2 adds functional impact (running intolerance, surface dependency, swelling pattern) narrowing to chronic ankle/foot condition, likely lower limb support structure. Clue 3 introduces mechanism (prior sprains, high-impact loading) and risk factors, pointing toward chronic overuse or post-sprains sequela. Clue 4 provides the critical motor/proprioceptive signs (weakness, arch collapse on stance, navicular drop quantification) that strongly differentiate PTTD from simple chronic ankle instability or peroneal pathology. Clue 5 seals it with imaging confirmation of partial tear + ligamentous involvement (Grade 2). Each clue meaningfully narrows the differential; no redundancy. Appropriate difficulty arc.

451. Hip Flexor Strain
Hip
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Main issue: Clue 1 is far too specific and essentially names the answer. The exact anatomical location ('myotendinous junction of the iliopsoas muscle just below the inguinal ligament') and 'sharp sensation at the moment of injury' combined with the soccer-sprint context makes this nearly a direct giveaway of hip flexor strain. A student reading only Clue 1 would immediately think 'iliopsoas strain' rather than generating a broad differential (adductor strain, labral tear, FAI, inguinal hernia, rectus femoris strain, etc.). Clue 3 (History) adds minimal differential value beyond what Clue 2 already established—training load increases are non-specific and don't meaningfully narrow the diagnosis. Clue 4 (Examination) should differentiate between remaining candidates but instead mostly confirms what Clue 1 already heavily implied. The progressive reveal principle is undermined by front-loading too much clinical specificity.

Palpation reveals tenderness at the myotendinous junction of the iliopsoas muscle just below the inguinal ligament. Iliopsoas flexibility testing shows significant tightness on the affected side. Bruising is visible in the anterior hip region.
UPDATED
Hip flexion strength reduced to 4/5 with pain on resistance. Palpation tender just below the inguinal ligament. Visible bruising in anterior hip region. Hip extension range of motion appears slightly limited on the affected side.
💡 Hip flexion strength deficit (4/5). Resisted hip flexion reproduction of pain. Palpable tenderness at myotendinous junction below inguinal ligament. Iliopsoas tightness. Visible bruising.
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
MRI of the hip demonstrates acute partial-thickness tear of the iliopsoas muscle at the myotendinous junction approximately 2 cm distal to the inguinal ligament. Muscle edema and hemorrhage surround the tear site. No muscle retraction. Underlying hip joint and bony anatomy appear normal. Findings consistent with acute muscle strain grade 2.
✓ No changes
💡 MRI confirmation of partial-thickness tear at myotendinous junction. Specific location (2 cm distal to inguinal ligament). Muscle edema and hemorrhage. Grade 2 strain classification. Excludes joint/bony pathology.
452. Non-Specific Low Back Pain
Lumbar Spine
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major progressive reveal issue: Clues 1 and 2 together already establish 'mechanical LBP with activity-related aggravation, no red flags, no radiation.' Clue 3 adds occupational/ergonomic context but doesn't meaningfully narrow the differential—non-specific LBP vs. muscle strain vs. facet dysfunction are still all viable. Clue 4 (normal neuro exam, full ROM, muscle tenderness) further confirms benign mechanical pattern but doesn't differentiate between remaining conditions. Clue 5 just confirms 'no structural disease' which was already strongly implied by the benign exam and activity pattern. The puzzle essentially reaches confidence around Clue 2–3, then repeats the same message (mechanical, non-serious, probably muscular) three more times. Consider restructuring: Clue 3 should add occupational/psychosocial detail; Clue 4 should include ROM restriction or specific provocative findings that hint at one mechanism over another (e.g., facet loading tests positive vs. quadratus lumborum involvement); Clue 5 should confirm imaging normality. As written, even Clue 1 comes very close to naming the answer ('mechanical LBP' + 'no radiation' + 'no trauma' essentially rules in non-specific LBP immediately for an experienced clinician).

454. Adhesive Capsulitis
Shoulder
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major issues in Clue 1 and Clue 3. Clue 1 explicitly names 'adhesive capsulitis' in the complaint, which is a critical error—it gives away the answer before any reasoning is needed. Clue 3 similarly flags 'capsular pathology' which again strongly points to adhesive capsulitis and undermines the progressive reveal. After Clue 1 alone, a player could guess the answer; after Clue 3, it becomes nearly certain. The differential narrowing occurs too early. Clues 2, 4, and 5 are well-structured and provide genuine diagnostic discrimination, but they are negated by the early giveaways. Rewrite Clues 1 and 3 to remove diagnosis-naming language and let the pattern of ROM loss, immobilization history, and diabetes emerge as clinical clues rather than stated conclusions.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
names_answertoo_easy

UPDATED

💡 Age, symptom character (deep aching), temporal pattern (5 months progressive, worse at night), and anatomical localization (shoulder joint). Establishes broad differential of inflammatory/structural shoulder pathology.
Clue 2
Activity
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
The patient reports marked limitation in shoulder external rotation and overhead elevation, making it impossible to reach behind the back or reach high shelves. Internal rotation is relatively preserved. Throwing and overhead sporting activities are no longer possible. Pain is partially relieved with heat and gentle therapeutic exercise within available range.
✓ No changes
💡 Specific directional ROM loss (external rotation and overhead movement severely limited; internal rotation relatively preserved) and functional impact (cannot reach behind back or overhead). Narrows to shoulder pathology affecting external rotation preferentially.
Clue 3

✗ MAJOR
names_answerno_new_info

Following this period of reduced shoulder use, progressive stiffness and pain developed. The patient has type 2 diabetes mellitus, which is a known risk factor for capsular pathology. No prior significant shoulder injuries are reported.
UPDATED
Seven months prior to symptom onset, the patient suffered a myocardial infarction and was advised to minimize shoulder activity during cardiac recovery. Stiffness and pain gradually developed in the weeks following resumption of normal activity. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed 8 years ago.
💡 Mechanism (prolonged immobilization following MI 7 months ago) and risk factor (type 2 diabetes). Establishes timeline and predisposing factors. However, this clue REPEATS the diagnosis implicitly by mentioning 'capsular pathology' and anchors heavily to adhesive capsulitis.
Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
On examination, shoulder abduction is limited to 85 degrees and external rotation is severely restricted to 15 degrees with hard end-feel. Forward flexion is limited to 120 degrees. Passive range of motion is similarly restricted in all planes. Rotator cuff strength testing shows weakness attributable to pain inhibition rather than true muscle loss. Scapular mechanics are intact.
✓ No changes
💡 Quantified ROM restrictions (abduction 85°, external rotation 15°, forward flexion 120°), hard end-feel suggesting capsular limitation, and global passive ROM restriction. Pain-inhibited weakness rather than true muscle atrophy. Intact scapular mechanics. This discriminates adhesive capsulitis from rotator cuff tears and neurogenic causes.
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
MRI demonstrates capsular thickening and synovial inflammation with reduced joint space volume. No full-thickness tears are present. Findings are consistent with chronic adhesive capsulitis.
✓ No changes
💡 Imaging confirmation: capsular thickening, synovial inflammation, circumferential fibrosis (anterior and posterior), reduced joint space volume, normal rotator cuff muscles without fatty infiltration, no full-thickness tears. Near-definitive for adhesive capsulitis; excludes rotator cuff pathology.
455. Shoulder Impingement Syndrome
Shoulder
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Clue 1 is too specific and too easy. It names overhead activities, catching, and anterior/lateral location — essentially giving away 'shoulder impingement' to anyone familiar with the condition. The mechanism (postural stress, deconditioning, rounded shoulders) and timeline (insidious 8 weeks) appear in Clue 1 but should be reserved for Clue 3 (History). Clue 2 largely restates Clue 1 without adding meaningfully new functional information beyond what Clue 1 already established about overhead limitations. After Clue 1 alone, a competent clinician would narrow to 2-3 diagnoses; by Clue 3, only impingement remains. Progressive reveal is compromised. Rewrite Clue 1 to include only symptom character and broad onset info; move mechanism/timeline to Clue 2. Rewrite Clue 2 to focus on functional impact specific to work demands and daily activities.

458. Intertrochanteric Fracture
Hip
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: This puzzle has significant progressive reveal problems. Clues 1–2 together establish acute severe hip trauma with loss of function — clue 2 adds almost no new constraint and could be omitted. Clue 3 reinforces mechanism and risk factors already clear from Clues 1–2, adding only osteoporosis context. The puzzle then makes a big jump: Clue 4 provides genuinely useful objective discrimination (the external rotation/abduction posture), which should narrow differential. Clue 5 then names the diagnosis outright, breaking the game logic. A stronger sequence would: (1) establish acute hip pain + fall broadly; (2) add posture/position clues (external rotation/abduction); (3) add risk factors (osteoporosis) and mechanism detail; (4) provide examination findings that exclude soft-tissue injury; (5) describe specific radiographic fracture pattern. Reorder and rewrite clues 1, 2, and 5 to restore the narrowing funnel.

Spine
GOOD

Clue 4 confirms degenerative pattern (loss of lordosis, facet loading pain, normal neuro). Clue 5 confirms with imaging. Each clue meaningfully narrows the differential. No redundancy or information misplacement detected.
Clue 2
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Symptoms are exacerbated by sustained postures, particularly prolonged sitting and standing, with the patient reporting need for frequent position changes. Walking and gentle movement provide temporary relief. Bending activities are limited due to pain rather than mechanical restriction.
✓ No changes
💡 Age, onset pattern (gradual, progressive over years), pain character (mechanical), location (low lumbar), and temporal pattern (morning and end-of-day). Establishes broad differential for age-related spinal pain (DDD, facet OA, spinal stenosis, myofascial pain).
Clue 3
Activity
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
The patient reports a 40-year history of occupational heavy lifting as a construction worker. Previous episodes of acute low back pain have occurred over the past 15 years with increasing frequency. Multiple risk factors include smoking history, obesity, and sedentary lifestyle post-retirement.
✓ No changes
💡 Functional limitation pattern: exacerbated by sustained static postures (sitting/standing), improved by movement, limited by pain not mechanical block. This is NEW and narrows from general spinal pain to conditions aggravated by sustained loading rather than acute mechanical dysfunction (rules out acute disc herniation with radiculopathy, some forms of stenosis).

✓ OK

✓ No changes
💡 40-year occupational exposure (heavy lifting), increasing frequency of acute episodes over 15 years, multiple degenerative risk factors (smoking, obesity, sedentary post-retirement). ✓ OK
ORIGINAL

💡 Specific objective findings: lumbar flexion 40° (limited by pain, not block), loss of lordosis, paraspinal spasm, positive facet loading tests, normal SLR (75°), normal strength 5/5. This is NEW and discriminatory: normal neuro (SLR, strength) rules out significant radiculopathy or myelopathy; loss of lordosis + facet pain suggests facet OA/DDD rather than pure disc herniation or stenosis.
Clue 6
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
✓ No changes
💡 MRI findings: multi-level disc degeneration, decreased height, T2 signal loss, osteophytes at L4-L5 and L5-S1, no significant central stenosis. This is NEW and definitive: confirms DDD diagnosis, localizes degeneration, rules out herniation with cord compression or significant stenosis.
461. Whiplash Associated Disorder
Cervical Spine
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a critical structural flaw: Clue 1 already names the mechanism (acceleration-deceleration) and context (neck trauma), which are pathognomonic for whiplash. Clue 2 repeats the functional consequence (limited movement, guarding) without adding new discriminatory info. Clue 3 essentially rephrases Clue 1. Clues 4 and 5 provide generic findings that don't differentiate whiplash from cervical strain, facet-mediated pain, or mild disc injury. The progressive reveal funnel is flat. A player reading only Clue 1 would have already narrowed to whiplash; the remaining clues add almost no new information that changes their confidence in the answer. Rewrites should: (1) remove 'acceleration-deceleration' from Clue 1 to avoid giving away the diagnosis; (2) add specific functional deficits (radiation, posture dependence) in Clue 2; (3) add timeline and stability info in Clue 3; (4) add specific ROM measures and confirm absence of cord signs in Clue 4; (5) detail the specific imaging pattern (edema without herniation/stenosis) in Clue 5.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easy
ORIGINAL
A patient with acute neck trauma presents with neck pain and stiffness. The injury resulted from acceleration-deceleration mechanism.
UPDATED
A patient presents with acute onset of neck pain and upper back discomfort following a motor vehicle accident. Onset was immediate.
💡 Acute neck pain and stiffness from acceleration-deceleration mechanism
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Functional limitation is present with difficulty with neck movement. The patient demonstrates guarding and protective postures.
UPDATED
Patient reports pain radiating toward the shoulder and upper limb. Symptoms worsen with sustained postures and improve with rest and support.
💡 Functional limitation with neck movement and guarding behavior — but this is largely predictable from Clue 1 (acute neck trauma already implies reduced movement and protective posturing)
Clue 3
History
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Motor vehicle accident or similar mechanism causing rapid neck acceleration-deceleration. Immediate onset of symptoms.
UPDATED
Injury occurred 2 weeks ago. Patient reports no loss of consciousness. No prior neck problems. Symptoms have plateaued; initial severe pain has subsided partially but stiffness persists.
💡 Mechanism (MVA) and immediate onset — but both already explicitly stated in Clue 1. No new temporal, comorbidity, or prior treatment information
Clue 4
Examination
✗ MAJOR
too_generic
ORIGINAL

UPDATED
Cervical flexion limited to 20°; rotation and lateral flexion also restricted symmetrically. Palpable muscle spasm in upper trapezius and paraspinal muscles. Cervical myelopathy signs absent. Upper limb strength and reflexes intact bilaterally.
💡 Limited cervical ROM, local tenderness, normal neuro exam — these are expected findings in neck trauma and don't discriminate between whiplash, cervical strain, disc herniation, or facet injury

✗ MAJOR
too_hard

UPDATED

💡 Radiographs normal or straightened (loss of lordosis is nonspecific), MRI shows soft tissue or disc changes — but these findings are common in many cervical pathologies and don't definitively confirm whiplash as the diagnosis
462. Gout
Systemic
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a major progressive reveal problem. Clue 1 is far too specific and descriptive—it names 'great toe', 'red and warm', 'acute overnight', 'throbbing'—which already screams gout to an experienced clinician. Clue 3 then provides the history (hyperuricaemia, risk factors) which seals the diagnosis. Clues 4 and 5 feel confirmatory rather than discriminatory. The middle clues (2, 4) add minimal new differential-narrowing information beyond what was already established. Rewrite Clue 1 to remove the anatomical specificity ('right great toe' → 'right foot') and systemic signs (erythema, warmth), keeping only pain character and onset. Rewrite Clue 4 to focus on examination findings NOT already implied by Clue 1, such as ROM specificity or absence of polyarticular involvement.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easy
ORIGINAL
A 58-year-old male presents with acute-onset severe pain and swelling in his right great toe that developed overnight. The pain is described as throbbing and intense, with the joint appearing red and warm to touch, significantly limiting mobility.
UPDATED
A 58-year-old male presents with acute-onset severe pain in the right foot that woke him overnight. The affected joint is swollen, red, and warm. Pain is throbbing and intense, significantly limiting mobility.
💡 Acute-onset severe pain and swelling in first MTP joint with systemic features (erythema, warmth). Establishes acute inflammatory presentation in a lower limb joint.
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
The patient cannot bear weight on the affected foot and experiences exquisite pain even with light touch or passive motion. Bed sheets touching the joint provoke severe discomfort. Any attempt at walking or standing is intolerable, forcing bed rest.
UPDATED
The patient cannot tolerate any weight-bearing and is confined to bed rest. Even light pressure or passive movement of the foot causes severe pain. No other joints are affected.
💡 Functional impact: complete inability to weight-bear and extreme pain with minimal provocation (light touch, bedsheet contact). Emphasises severity and hypersensitivity.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
The patient has a 10-year history of hyperuricemia and had been non-compliant with urate-lowering therapy. Recent dietary indulgences included a heavy seafood dinner and alcohol consumption. He reports prior similar episodes that resolved over 1-2 weeks without treatment.
✓ No changes
💡 History of hyperuricemia, non-compliance with urate-lowering therapy, recent risk factors (seafood, alcohol), prior recurrent self-limiting episodes over 1-2 weeks. This is the critical clue that narrows significantly toward gout.
Clue 4
Examination
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
On examination, the first metatarsophalangeal joint is markedly swollen with erythema and warmth. Joint effusion is palpable and movement is severely restricted by pain. The joint demonstrates exquisite point tenderness. Systemic examination shows no fever or other inflammatory findings.
UPDATED
Joint examination shows marked swelling with severe restriction of dorsiflexion. Passive motion triggers acute pain. Exquisite tenderness at the first MTP joint line. No other joints show signs of inflammation. Core temperature is normal.
💡 Objective findings confirm first MTP joint involvement: swelling, erythema, warmth, effusion, restricted ROM, point tenderness. No systemic fever or polyarticular involvement. Largely redundant with Clue 1 findings.
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Ultrasound of the first MTP joint demonstrates hypoechoic joint effusion with synovial thickening and mild hyperemia on Doppler imaging. X-ray imaging shows no acute fracture or significant degenerative changes. Serum uric acid level is elevated at 8.2 mg/dL.
✓ No changes
💡 Ultrasound confirms joint effusion and synovial inflammation; elevated serum uric acid (8.2 mg/dL) confirms hyperuricaemia as the underlying disorder. X-ray rules out fracture/degenerative change.
463. Hallux Valgus
Ankle & Foot
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a critical structural flaw: Clue 1 essentially names the diagnosis by using 'hallux' terminology and describing the classic deformity ('prominence on the inner side', 'great toe joint'). This gives away the answer to anyone with basic foot anatomy knowledge before even reading Clue 2. Clue 3 adds minimal new information beyond what Clues 1-2 already established (the mechanism is shoe-induced deformity + family predisposition, which is implied earlier). Clue 4 uses diagnostic terminology ('hallux deviated laterally', 'medial eminence') that reinforces the naming problem. The progressive reveal narrows very quickly due to Clue 1 being too specific. Rewrite Clue 1 to describe pain/deformity symptoms WITHOUT naming anatomy, and Clue 4 to describe findings without using the diagnostic term 'hallux valgus angle'. The differential should initially include other forefoot/great toe pathologies (turf toe, first MTP arthritis, gout) before being narrowed by functional and historical clues.

💡 42-year-old woman with progressive foot deformity, medial forefoot pain, prominent bump on inner foot, great toe joint discomfort, 8-year timeline

466. Generalised Joint Hypermobility
Systemic
POOR

467. Whiplash Associated Disorder
Cervical Spine
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle suffers from severe redundancy across the first three clues. Clues 1, 2, and 3 all essentially repeat the same information: acute neck trauma with neck pain from a motor vehicle accident causing difficulty with neck movement. This violates the progressive reveal principle—the player learns almost nothing new moving from Clue 1 to Clues 2 and 3. Clue 4 is too generic (normal neuro + cervical pain + ROM loss could apply to uncomplicated acute cervical strain, facet syndrome, or even early cervical radiculopathy). Clue 5 is non-specific regarding WAD specifically. The puzzle needs substantial restructuring to create a true clinical funnel. After rewriting, the differential should narrow from 'any acute cervical injury' (post-Clue 1) → 'post-traumatic cervical condition with specific movement patterns' (post-Clue 2) → 'traumatic injury without immediate neurological compromise but with persistent/worsening symptoms' (post-Clue 3) → 'diffuse soft tissue injury without fracture/cord involvement' (post-Clue 4) → 'confirmed soft tissue edema without structural fracture or neural compression' (post-Clue 5).

468. Necrotising Fasciitis Post-Rehabilitation
Various
POOR

A player reading only Clue 1 already knows this is a deep soft tissue infection progressing rapidly; Clues 2–3 add nothing new. The funnel should be: Clue 1 = post-operative systemic illness (broad: could be sepsis, SIRS, infection); Clue 2 = localized swelling/erythema spreading rapidly (narrows to soft tissue); Clue 3 = pain out of proportion + specific exam findings (suggests necrotising); Clue 4 = imaging confirms fascial involvement. Clues 4–5 are reasonable, but everything before them fails the 'distinct information' test. Rewrite all of Clues 1–3 to broaden Clue 1 and add genuinely new findings in Clues 2–3.

470. Postural Hypotension
Cardiovascular
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a solid diagnostic funnel through Clues 1–3, with Clue 4 providing the critical objective finding (BP drop without compensatory HR rise, which rules out true hypovolemia and points to autonomic or medication effect). However, Clue 2 is largely predictable from Clue 1 (standing-triggered dizziness obviously causes positional avoidance), and Clue 5 merely rephrases Clue 4 without adding new discriminatory information. Clue 5 should introduce something Clue 4 didn't—either additional autonomic findings (e.g., absence of subclinical tachycardia on ECG, pupillary findings, sweating dysfunction) or imaging/testing that distinguishes between autonomic neuropathy, medication effect, and primary autonomic failure. As written, a player who reads Clues 1–4 has essentially all the information needed; Clue 5 adds nothing new.
Clue 1
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
A 71-year-old woman on multiple antihypertensive medications experiences lightheadedness and near-syncope upon standing from bed in the morning. Frequent episodes of vertigo sensation.
✓ No changes
💡 Age, medication context (antihypertensives), and symptom onset (lightheadedness, near-syncope on standing, vertigo). Establishes broad cardiovascular/neurological differential.
Clue 2
Activity
⚠ MINOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL

UPDATED

💡 Functional impact specific to positional changes (avoids standing quickly, uses support, symptoms relieved by lying flat). Adds behavioral context but largely predictable from Clue 1.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

✓ No changes

no_new_info

💡 Orthostatic vital signs and head-up tilt test results. However, these findings are largely redundant with Clue 4—both describe the same BP drop without tachycardia.

The puzzle would benefit from: (1) Clue 3 adding timeline details and symptom character (e.g., night symptoms, swelling) that Clues 1–2 don't address; (2) Clue 5 introducing imaging findings that genuinely rule out mimics (e.g., cervical radiculopathy, tendinopathy) rather than just confirming what clinical examination already showed. After Clue 2, the diagnosis is already fairly obvious to an experienced clinician—there is little 'funnel' effect through Clues 3–5.

Clue 3 is somewhat redundant with Clue 1's timing information but adds valuable loading context; the rewrite streamlines without losing specificity. 473. Hammer Toe

479. Middle Cerebral Artery Stroke
Neurological
POOR
Review Notes: Critical failures in progressive reveal: Clue 1 names the answer (mentions facial droop + acute onset + right-sided weakness in a stroke context—essentially diagnostic). Clue 2 contains examination-level specificity (strength grading, Babinski-equivalent cognitive/speech findings) that belongs in Clue 4. Clue 3 is redundant—hypertension and smoking were already established in Clue 1. Clue 4 has an incoherence: Clue 1 said 'right-sided weakness' but Clue 4 describes 'left facial droop' and 'right pronator drift'—this is clinically correct (left MCA = right motor signs) but contradicts the casual reading of Clue 1, creating confusion. Even a novice clinician reading Clue 1 alone would recognize acute stroke immediately. The puzzle fails the fundamental progressive reveal principle.

✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Acute CT brain shows hypodensity in left MCA territory involving motor cortex and corona radiata. Diffusion-weighted MRI confirms acute ischemic stroke. Angiography shows MCA occlusion.
✓ No changes
💡 CT hypodensity in MCA territory (motor cortex, corona radiata), DWI confirmation of acute ischemia, angiographic MCA occlusion. Confirms diagnosis and specific arterial territory.
480. Venous Leg Ulcer
Lower Leg
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has severe progressive reveal problems. Clue 1 explicitly names 'varicose veins' and describes classic venous ulcer location and presentation (medial ankle, edema, skin changes), which is far too specific for the broadest clue and essentially gives away a venous aetiology immediately. Clues 2 and 3 both elaborately repeat venous insufficiency concepts already established in Clue 1 (positional pain with elevation, varicose veins history, edema, compression trials) without adding meaningfully new information. By the time Clue 4 arrives, the diagnosis is already confirmed. Clue 4 and 5 are appropriate but redundant given the earlier over-specification. A proper funnel would start with vague lower leg ulcer complaint, narrow to venous vs arterial vs neuropathic with functional clues, then use history to eliminate arterial/neuropathic pathways, use examination for specific findings (lipodermatosclerosis, hemosiderin), and use imaging for confirmation. Rewritten Clue 1 removes varicose veins mention and anatomical specificity. Rewritten Clue 2 focuses on positional behaviour and exudate management (distinct from Clue 3's timeline). Rewritten Clue 3 adds DVT history and failed conservative therapy (new mechanistic information).

481. Post-Total Hip Replacement
Hip
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle has a fundamental structural flaw: the answer is essentially named in Clue 1 ('total hip replacement'), which collapses the entire differential from the start. For a Physiodle puzzle to work, Clue 1 must present only the complaint (pain, mobility loss) without revealing the intervention. Additionally, Clues 2 and 3 are highly redundant — both rehash post-THR precautions and early post-op context. The clues do not represent a progressive diagnostic funnel; instead, they simply elaborate the same post-operative THR scenario from different angles. A clinician reading only Clues 1–3 already knows the answer with near certainty. Clues 4 and 5 should discriminate between post-THR and alternative hip pathologies (e.g., occult labral tear, piriformis syndrome, heterotopic ossification, early infection), but instead they just describe expected post-operative findings. Recommend complete redesign: Clue 1 should hide the surgical history entirely; Clues 2–3 should progressively narrow the differential from 'hip pain' to 'post-operative hip'; Clues 4–5 should rule in THR specifically (e.g., through imaging of prosthetic components, specific gait signatures, strength patterns inconsistent with other diagnoses).
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
names_answertoo_easy

💡 Patient age, post-THR status (6 weeks), presence of pain and ROM loss. Establishes post-operative context but immediately names the intervention.

UPDATED

💡 Functional limitation pattern: restricted flexion, abduction, rotation; weight-bearing restrictions; need for assistive device. However, these restrictions are ONLY present/meaningful in post-THR rehabilitation — this clue reinforces the answer rather than narrowing differential.
Clue 3

✗ MAJOR

UPDATED

💡 Mechanism: THR for OA; timeline: 6 weeks post-op; context: need for 'hip precautions' and 'prosthetic complications' — all redundant with Clue 1. This clue adds NO new discriminating information.

⚠ MINOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL

UPDATED

💡 Specific findings: ROM restrictions (already known); reduced hip abductor strength (genuinely new); gait abnormality with weight-bearing protection (expected post-op, not diagnostic). Abductor weakness is useful discriminator but could differentiate post-THR from other hip conditions more sharply.

💡 Post-operative radiographs show correct component positioning, no instability/loosening/impingement. This is entirely reassuring and ELIMINATES complications, but does not CONFIRM the diagnosis — it simply rules out catastrophic post-op failure. Any post-THR patient 6 weeks out would expect these normal findings.

484. Middle Cerebral Artery Stroke
Brain
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle has SEVERE progressive reveal problems. Clue 1 is essentially diagnostic on its own—'72-year-old with AFib, acute left arm/leg weakness and speech difficulty, sudden onset' is nearly pathognomonic for acute stroke. Clue 2 just rephrases the same functional losses already described in Clue 1. Clue 3 adds risk factors (AFib, HTN, prior TIA, non-adherence) but much of this is already known from Clue 1. By Clue 3, a player has essentially heard the answer twice. The puzzle lacks the funnel effect: each clue should eliminate possibilities, but here each clue just elaborates the same story. Clue 4 (examination findings) is actually the first genuinely new information—focal neurological signs. This should come much earlier or Clues 1-3 should be restructured to avoid early diagnosis. For a Physiodle puzzle on stroke, Clue 1 should be much vaguer (e.g., 'sudden weakness in one limb, difficult to speak'), Clue 2 should confirm unilateral pattern and speech/motor involvement, Clue 3 should add vascular risk context, Clue 4 should provide the focal UMN pattern that narrows to central vs peripheral, and Clue 5 should confirm MCA distribution. Currently, a moderately trained clinician would guess correctly after Clue 1 alone.

Wrist & Hand
GOOD

Forearm
POOR

POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle has critical flaws. Clue 1 explicitly names 'anxiety and fear-avoidance behaviors' and states 'psychological impact significant'—essentially giving away the answer immediately. Clues 2 and 3 then rephrase and elaborate without narrowing the differential further. Clue 5 performs final diagnosis confirmation via psychological assessment (appropriate), but by then the answer was already obvious. The progressive reveal principle is violated: a player reading Clue 1 alone would correctly guess 'post-fall anxiety' or similar, leaving clues 2–4 as redundant elaboration. For Wordle-style gameplay, Clue 1 must be symptom-focused only (anxiety, avoidance, sleep disturbance) without labeling it as 'fear-avoidance behavior' or 'psychological impact.' The differential after Clue 1 should include deconditioning, undiagnosed fracture, vestibular disorder, medication side effect, etc. Clue 2 then narrows to movement/balance/psychological. Clue 3 adds history (mechanism, prior function, timeline) that distinguishes post-fall anxiety from chronic anxiety disorder. Clue 4 then rules out physical pathology. Clue 5 confirms psychological diagnosis. Recommend major restructuring.

490. Acute Wry Neck
Cervical Spine
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle starts well with a clear acute mechanical story (Clue 1–2). However, Clue 3 is the main problem: it explicitly mentions 'acute facet joint involvement' which is too close to naming the diagnosis (wry neck is classically a facet-mediated acute neck spasm). This collapses the differential prematurely. Clue 3 should provide timeline and context (e.g., no prior issues, sudden onset during functional activity) without anatomical attribution. Clue 5 is mildly redundant with Clue 4 (both emphasize no neurological deficit and no structural pathology), but Clue 4 is clinical exam and Clue 5 is imaging, so the distinction is defensible. After rewriting Clue 3, the progressive reveal works well: Clue 1 casts a net over acute neck pain syndromes; Clue 2 narrows to mechanical neck dysfunction with preserved neuro status; revised Clue 3 establishes sudden mechanism and lack of predisposing factors; Clue 4 confirms severe movement restriction with muscle guarding; Clue 5 confirms imaging normality, pointing to functional/muscular cause.

492. Guillain-Barré Syndrome
Neurological
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major problem: Clue 1 already names 'ascending paralysis' and post-viral context, which essentially identifies GBS to an experienced clinician immediately. Clue 2 repeats the same progression information without adding meaningful new functional or anatomical detail. Clue 3 adds useful temporal precision but doesn't meaningfully narrow beyond what Clues 1-2 established. Clue 4 has a critical issue: it states 'distal greater than proximal' weakness, which contradicts Clue 1's 'ascending' description and is actually atypical for GBS (which is usually proximal > distal or symmetric). Clue 5 is appropriately specific but by then the diagnosis is already obvious. The progressive reveal funnel fails to work: a clinician reading only Clue 1 is 90% confident this is GBS. Rewrite Clue 1 to be much vaguer (bilateral leg weakness, progressive, post-viral — no mention of ascending pattern), Clue 2 to add cranial/bulbar involvement, and Clue 4 to include areflexia as the key discriminator.

494. Long Thoracic Nerve Palsy
Shoulder
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a critical structural problem: Clue 1 is TOO SPECIFIC. 'Winging of the scapula' is a hallmark sign of long thoracic nerve palsy and immediately flags the diagnosis for anyone with basic physiotherapy knowledge. Clues 2 and 4 then essentially repeat this finding without advancing the differential. The progressive reveal fails because the answer is given away at the start. Clue 1 should present only general weakness/functional loss (broad differential). Clue 2 should narrow to upper limb/pushing pattern. Clue 3 should add history that steers toward nerve injury. Clue 4 should demonstrate the scapular winging and serratus weakness (the key finding). Clue 5 should confirm with EMG. As written, a clinician has no meaningful work to do after Clue 1.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easy
ORIGINAL
A 41-year-old woman presents with winging of the scapula, noticeable when reaching forward or pushing. Gradual onset over 3 weeks with progressive functional loss.
UPDATED
A 41-year-old woman reports progressive shoulder weakness and difficulty with pushing activities over the past 3 weeks. No clear injury event. Pain is minimal; the main complaint is loss of strength in the upper extremity.
💡 Scapular winging (highly specific sign pointing directly to serratus anterior or long thoracic nerve), gradual onset over 3 weeks, triggered by reaching/pushing.
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL

UPDATED

💡 Redundant with Clue 1. Clue 1 already stated 'noticeable when reaching forward or pushing.' Clue 2 merely elaborates the same functional limitation without adding differential value.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
No acute trauma identified. Possible nerve injury from heavy lifting or overhead activity. Gradual progressive onset suggesting nerve inflammation or injury.
✓ No changes
💡 No acute trauma (rules out acute brachial plexus injury), possible heavy lifting trigger, gradual progressive onset. Suggests chronic nerve injury or irritation rather than acute trauma.
Clue 4
Examination
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Positive scapular winging when patient pushes arms against wall or performs wall push-up. Serratus anterior strength significantly weakened. Scapula borders prominent. Shoulder ROM preserved.
UPDATED
Scapular winging present at rest and exacerbated with arm protraction. Serratus anterior strength 3/5 on manual muscle testing. No sensory deficits in upper extremity. Shoulder flexion and abduction ROM full and pain-free. Trapezius and rhomboid strength intact. No cervical radiculopathy signs.
💡 Positive scapular winging on wall push-up test, serratus anterior weakness on strength testing, preserved shoulder ROM. This is highly specific for serratus anterior dysfunction but does NOT differentiate long thoracic nerve palsy from other causes of serratus anterior weakness (e.g., C5/C6 radiculopathy, direct muscle injury, or trapezius/rhomboid dominance patterns). Redundant with Clue 1's description of winging.
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Electromyography shows denervation of serratus anterior muscle. Motor unit action potentials reduced and reinnervation changes variable. Nerve conduction normal for other upper extremity nerves.
✓ No changes
💡 EMG/NCS shows denervation of serratus anterior, reduced motor unit action potentials, variable reinnervation changes, normal conduction in other upper extremity nerves. This confirms a nerve-specific lesion (rules out myopathy, C5/C6 radiculopathy, or plexopathy) and localises to serratus anterior innervation.

Hip
NEEDS WORK

500. Pulmonary Embolism
Lungs
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: This puzzle has significant redundancy between Clues 1 and 2. Clue 1 already states acute dyspnea, chest pain, tachypnea, tachycardia, and acute presentation — Clue 2 repeats these same findings and merely quantifies them (SpO2 90%, describes severity). A player learns almost nothing new from Clue 2, breaking the progressive reveal principle. Clue 3 is strong — it shifts from symptoms to risk factors (post-op immobility, hypercoagulability, no prophylaxis), which meaningfully narrows the differential toward VTE. Clue 4 has a similar redundancy problem: vital signs are already established in Clue 1, so their restatement adds minimal value. The negative findings (no leg swelling, no JVD) are useful for excluding other diagnoses, but they should have been more prominent. Clue 4 also introduces Wells score, which is a risk-stratification tool rather than a raw examination finding — better to stick to objective findings. The progressive funnel works reasonably well at the category level (symptoms → risk factors → exam → imaging) but needs tightening to eliminate repetition and ensure each clue genuinely narrows the differential.

501. Frailty Syndrome
Systemic
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle has critical structural problems. Clue 1 nearly names the answer by describing 'frailty phenotype' characteristics explicitly (progressive weakness, weight loss, age 82, declining function) — a physio student would immediately think 'frailty syndrome'. Clues 2–4 then redundantly restate the same functional and examination findings without adding meaningfully new information. The differential never truly narrows because there is no competing diagnosis ever in play. The 'imaging' clue (Clue 5) actually contains investigations (bloods/labs), not imaging, and repeats findings already mentioned. There is no progressive reveal — just elaboration of the same clinical picture. To fix this puzzle, Clue 1 must be made broader (remove age-specific language and explicit weight loss detail), Clues 2–4 must each add distinct, non-overlapping information (e.g., Clue 2 narrows to 'systemic/age-related vs. acute neurological vs. cardiac'; Clue 3 rules out metabolic/endocrine causes; Clue 4 rules out focal neuromuscular disease), and Clue 5 should provide specific investigation results that confirm the systemic, nutritional, and age-related picture.

502. De Quervain's Tenosynovitis
Wrist & Hand
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Clue 1 is too anatomically specific and functionally detailed for the Complaint phase. It nearly names the diagnosis (radial wrist pain + thumb + gripping = de Quervain's for most clinicians). Clues 2 and 3 substantially overlap — both reinforce the occupational, repetitive, gradual mechanism without adding distinct functional or historical information. By Clue 3, a player already knows the work context, timeline, and nature of the mechanism from Clue 1. The progressive reveal stalls. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriately specific and discriminatory. Recommend: broaden Clue 1 to focus only on location and character of pain; remove mechanism from Clue 1; use Clue 2 for functional impact only (swelling, loss of grip/pinch); use Clue 3 exclusively for occupational history and timeline; keep Clues 4-5 as is.

503. Lumbar Radiculopathy
Lumbar Spine
NEEDS WORK

The progressive reveal completely collapses. By Clue 2, the answer is already obvious. Clues 3, 4, 5 then just elaborate without actually narrowing further. Clue 1 should be broader (pain + functional loss, no mention of radiation pattern or neurological signs). Clue 2 should establish the segmental/dermatomal quality. Clue 3 deepens history. Clue 4 provides discriminating exam findings. Clue 5 confirms with imaging. The reordering and rewrites above attempt to restore the funnel effect.

505. Groin Strain
Groin
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a critical structural flaw: Clues 1 and 2 overlap heavily. Both convey acute onset, immediate functional loss, and adduction-related pain — the player learns almost nothing new from Clue 2. Clue 3's mechanism is already heavily implied by Clue 1 (hockey game during play = acceleration/deceleration injury is expected). The differential narrowing is weak through Clues 1-3. By Clue 2, a clinician is already 90% confident of acute groin strain; Clues 3-4 merely confirm what was obvious from the start. For a better progression: Clue 1 should stay broad (just the complaint), Clue 2 should focus on functional impact (what movements provoke/relieve), Clue 3 should add meaningful history (e.g., prior strains, risk factors, systemic symptoms) that actually rules out other etiologies, Clue 4 should differentiate muscle vs. nerve vs. hernia, and Clue 5 should confirm the specific muscle and degree of tear. Consider adding elements like groin pain without hip pain (rules out referred hip OA), no testicular symptoms (rules out urological), no 'clicking' (rules out labral), to use Clue 3-4 more effectively.

507. Tibial Shaft Fracture
Lower Leg
POOR
Review Notes: Critical progressive reveal failure: Clues 1, 2, and 3 are severely redundant. They all describe the same traumatic mechanism, location, and acute functional loss from different angles without narrowing the differential. By Clue 1 alone, a fracture is already the most likely diagnosis — the puzzle gives away the answer too early. Clues 2 and 3 add almost nothing. The differential (e.g., severe muscle strain, compartment syndrome, contusion, vs. fracture) is barely narrowed by the first three clues. Clue 4 finally provides objective examination data, and Clue 5 is appropriately definitive. Rewrite Clues 1–3 to follow the progressive funnel: Clue 1 should focus on complaint only (pain, swelling, acute onset, mechanism character) without emphasizing functional loss and deformity so heavily. Clue 2 should add functional/activity specificity (inability to bear weight, visible deformity). Clue 3 should add relevant history (timing, prior injury, comorbidities, occupational context) that distinguishes from other acute leg injuries.

✓ No changes

Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Leg X-rays show transverse fracture of mid-tibial shaft with displacement. Fibula fracture also present (associated injury). No vascular compromise. Open fracture assessment performed.
✓ No changes
💡 Definitive diagnosis — transverse mid-shaft tibial fracture with displacement, fibular fracture, no vascular compromise, open fracture assessment. Confirms the fracture type and rules out complication.
508. Sinding-Larsen-Johansson Disease
Knee
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Clue 1 is too specific too early — it names the exact anatomical location and essentially identifies the condition for a knowledgeable player. Clue 2 repeats information from Clue 1 and includes examination findings that belong in Clue 4. The progressive reveal is disrupted: by end of Clue 1, a clinician already knows this is a patellar ligament insertion problem in an adolescent, leaving almost no diagnostic work for Clues 2–3. Clue 3 is strong (growth spurt context), but comes too late to help narrow a differential already essentially given away. Clue 4 reinforces what Clue 1 already said. Recommend broadening Clue 1 to general anterior knee pain, moving activity specificity to Clue 2, and reserving anatomical precision for Clue 4.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easy
ORIGINAL

💡 Patient age, sport, acute onset with jumping, and specific anatomical location (inferior patellar pole and patellar ligament insertion).
Clue 2

Clue 3
History
✓ OK

Knee
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Progressive reveal is compromised by Clue 2 being almost entirely redundant with Clue 1. After Clue 1, a clinician already knows the patient has knee pain with specific movement limitations; Clue 2 should either add novel functional constraints (e.g., instability, proprioceptive deficit) or reveal impact on a new domain (e.g., occupational, sports-specific) that changes the clinical reasoning. Clues 3–5 are coherent and add meaningful information, but the Clue 1→2 transition fails the progressive-reveal test.

510. Postural Orthostatic Tachycardia Syndrome
Cardiovascular
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle catastrophically. Clue 1 essentially names the answer ('orthostatic tachycardia' + 'POTS diagnosis suspected' in Clue 3). Clues 1–3 are heavily redundant, all circling the same standing-induced tachycardia symptom without adding discriminatory depth. A player reading only Clue 1 already knows the answer. Clues 4–5 are appropriately specific but arrive too late and cannot salvage the game. The differential should be broad at Clue 1 (dizziness + fatigue + palpitations on standing could be deconditioning, anaemia, dysrhythmia, autonomic dysfunction, etc.), narrowing progressively through mechanism (post-viral onset), examination (orthostatic BP/HR response), and test (tilt table). Rewritten clues should remove the early diagnosis hint and distribute information logically across the sequence.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easynames_answer
ORIGINAL
A 28-year-old woman with orthostatic tachycardia and exercise intolerance reports functional limitation with standing and exertion.
UPDATED

💡 28-year-old woman with orthostatic tachycardia and exercise intolerance
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Significant functional limitation from tachycardia episodes. Difficulty with work activities requiring standing. Fatigue limiting daily activities.
UPDATED

💡 Redundant with Clue 1 — both describe standing-induced tachycardia and exercise limitation. No functional discrimination added.
Clue 3
History
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Young woman with sudden onset symptoms post-viral. POTS diagnosis suspected from orthostatic response. Exercise previously tolerated but now causing symptoms.
UPDATED
Symptoms began abruptly 3 months after viral illness. No prior cardiovascular or autonomic symptoms. Lives in warm climate. Family history of syncope.
💡 Sudden onset post-viral, previously exercise-tolerant. Narrows timeline and prior status but still overlaps significantly with complaint and activity clues.
Clue 4
Examination
⚠ MINOR
wrong_order
ORIGINAL
Marked heart rate increase with minimal BP change on orthostatic challenge. Tachycardia sustained during upright posture. Symptoms reproducible.
UPDATED
Supine HR 68, BP 110/70. Standing 2 minutes: HR 118, BP 108/68. No presyncope relief with squatting. Normal cardiac auscultation.
💡 Heart rate increase >30 bpm with minimal BP change on orthostatic challenge; tachycardia sustained upright; symptoms reproducible. This IS discriminatory but is overshadowed by earlier clues already naming orthostatic tachycardia.
Clue 5
Imaging
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Tilt table test confirms POTS with sustained tachycardia >30 bpm increase. Norepinephrine inappropriately high supine relative to upright. Cardiovascular imaging normal.
UPDATED
Tilt table test: HR rises from 68 to 102 bpm within 3 minutes upright, sustained throughout 10-minute test. Systolic BP drops <10 mmHg. Plasma norepinephrine abnormally elevated supine (>600 pg/mL). Echocardiogram and Holter monitor normal.
💡 Tilt table test with >30 bpm increase and elevated supine norepinephrine. Cardiovascular imaging normal. Confirms diagnosis but clue 1 already stated this was suspected POTS.

POOR

Shoulder
POOR

The differential should initially include many shoulder conditions (frozen shoulder, rotator cuff strain, impingement, labral tear, etc.), and successive clues should narrow this progressively. 516. Patellar Dislocation
Knee
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major issue: Clue 1 explicitly names the mechanism ('pivot-and-plant'), describes the key diagnostic finding ('visibly displaced laterally'), and nearly gives away the answer outright. This removes the challenge from the game. Clue 2 largely rephrases the functional impact already established in Clue 1 without adding substantially new discriminating information—both focus on instability with specific movements. Clue 3 repeats the pivot-and-plant mechanism from Clue 1 and adds only minor contextual detail. The progressive reveal is compromised: by Clue 1, an experienced clinician has already narrowed significantly to patellofemoral instability. Clues 2 and 3 add little. Consider restructuring: Clue 1 should be much broader (knee pain + instability, without mention of displacement or mechanism); Clue 2 should narrow to knee region and movement-pattern specificity; Clue 3 should introduce the pivot-and-plant mechanism and first-episode context; Clues 4 and 5 remain strong discriminators.
Clue 1
Complaint

Clue 2
