517. Intertrochanteric Fracture
Hip
GOOD
Review Notes: Strong progressive reveal. Clue 1 appropriately establishes acute trauma in elderly patient without naming body region explicitly. Clue 2 narrows to severe structural injury. Clue 3 provides fragility context and comorbidity; minor redundancy with 'declining mobility' already mentioned in Clue 2, but Osteoporosis diagnosis is new and relevant. Clue 4 provides pathognomonic physical signs that differentiate fracture type. Clue 5 confirms with imaging. Each clue meaningfully narrows differential. Not too easy (Clue 1 alone doesn't give answer), not too hard (Clue 4 should narrow severely). Minor rewrite suggested for Clue 3 to emphasize osteoporosis as primary risk factor rather than merely mentioning warfarin management.
Clue 1
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
An 82-year-old woman living independently presents to the emergency department following a fall from standing height at home, reporting severe right hip and groin pain with inability to weight-bear.
✓ No changes

Clue 2
Activity
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
She was unable to stand or take any steps after the fall and required an ambulance. Prior to the fall she was ambulatory with a walking stick indoors, and her mobility had been declining over the preceding months.
✓ No changes
💡 Adds functional context: complete loss of mobility post-fall (vs. pre-fall declining ambulatory status), required emergency transport. Indicates severe structural injury rather than soft tissue only. Narrows toward fracture/dislocation.
Clue 3
History
⚠ MINOR
minor_redundancy

She has a history of osteoporosis and takes calcium and vitamin D supplementation. She has experienced two previous falls without fracture in the last year. Of note, she is on warfarin for atrial fibrillation, which will require management prior to surgical intervention.
UPDATED
History of osteoporosis with declining bone density despite supplementation. Two previous falls in the last year without fracture, but this fall occurred from standing height only. On warfarin for atrial fibrillation, creating perioperative management complexity.

Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
The right lower limb is shortened and externally rotated at rest. There is marked tenderness over the greater trochanteric and proximal thigh region. Any attempt at active hip movement produces severe pain, and a positive log-roll test is noted with axial loading.
✓ No changes
💡 Classic hip fracture triad: limb shortening, external rotation, severe pain on any movement. Log-roll test positive (axial loading pain = hip pathology, not lumbar). This powerfully discriminates fracture from dislocation (which would typically show internal rotation and flexion deformity) and soft tissue injury. Localizes pathology to proximal femur/hip joint.
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Pelvic AP X-ray demonstrates a fracture line below the femoral neck in the region between the greater and lesser trochanters. The fracture is classified as an unstable 4-part intertrochanteric pattern, and orthopaedic referral for dynamic hip screw fixation is arranged.
✓ No changes
💡 Definitively confirms fracture location (intertrochanteric, not femoral neck/subcapital or subtrochanteric). Specifies fracture pattern (unstable 4-part) and treatment pathway (dynamic hip screw). Confirms diagnosis entirely.
518. Polymyalgia Rheumatica
Systemic
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle is fundamentally broken. Clue 1 names the diagnosis outright ('diagnosed polymyalgia rheumatica'), eliminating any mystery or clinical reasoning. Clues 2–4 then redundantly describe the same well-controlled clinical picture without adding discriminatory information. The entire game collapses on Clue 1. A player guesses correctly immediately; subsequent clues teach nothing new. For a valid Physiodle puzzle, Clue 1 must present symptoms only (bilateral shoulder/hip aching, morning stiffness) without naming the condition, and subsequent clues must progressively differentiate PMR from other causes of polyarticular pain in older adults (e.g., rheumatoid arthritis, seronegative spondyloarthropathy, malignancy, infection). This puzzle requires complete restructuring.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR

UPDATED
A 72-year-old man reports persistent morning stiffness and aching in the shoulders and hips over the past 6 months. Symptoms gradually worsened before seeking care.
💡 Names the diagnosis directly ('diagnosed polymyalgia rheumatica'); establishes age, sex, and current treatment. This is the answer itself.
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Mild residual morning stiffness but significantly improved from baseline. Functional improvement with shoulders and hips. Activity tolerance improved.
UPDATED
Unable to overhead reach or comb hair due to shoulder pain and stiffness. Hip stiffness limits rising from chair and walking distance. Symptoms worst in early morning.
💡 Redundant with Clue 1. Clue 1 already stated 'Symptoms well-controlled' and implied functional status. This clue merely rephrases the same information (mild stiffness, improved function) without narrowing differential.
Clue 3
History
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
PMR diagnosed 6 months prior with good corticosteroid response. Maintained on stable low-dose prednisone. Regular follow-up for ESR trending. No systemic complications.
UPDATED
Recent-onset bilateral shoulder and hip pain in a patient with elevated inflammatory markers. Minimal prior joint disease history. Rapid symptom progression over 2–3 months before presentation.
💡 Adds timeline (6 months) and treatment response (corticosteroid benefit). However, timeline and diagnosis were already stated in Clue 1. The only marginally new detail is 'good corticosteroid response,' but this is generic to PMR and doesn't differentiate from other inflammatory arthritides.

no_new_infotoo_generic
ORIGINAL

UPDATED

💡 Reports ROM 'largely restored' and 'minor discomfort'—but these are indistinguishable from Clue 2, which already described 'functional improvement' and 'mild residual stiffness.' No new discriminatory findings (e.g., specific ROM values, asymmetry, neurological deficits, palpation findings).
Clue 5
Imaging
⚠ MINOR
no_new_infowrong_order

💡 Adds ESR value (28 mm/hr) and exclusion of giant cell arteritis. The ESR is a lab finding (appropriate) but is already implied by 'Inflammatory markers improved' in Clue 3. The exclusion of GCA is clinically useful but comes very late and should have been signposted earlier in history/examination.

Clues 2-4 mostly repeat and rephrase information (immobility, sacral location, erythema, non-blanchable) without adding discriminating details. For a physio student, Clue 1 alone allows immediate confident diagnosis. 523. Patellar Dislocation
Knee
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: This puzzle has a critical progressive reveal failure. Clue 1 explicitly states 'patella was visibly displaced laterally before spontaneous reduction' — this essentially names the answer immediately and violates the broadest-net principle of Clue 1. Clue 2 provides only functional elaboration of what was already stated (giving way, apprehension). Clue 3 is appropriate but retrospectively confirms what Clue 1 already revealed. Clues 4 and 5 add objective detail but feel confirmatory rather than discriminatory at that point. A player reading only Clue 1 would guess the answer almost immediately. The puzzle should start much broader (acute knee pain/instability without mentioning displacement), then narrow through activity (pivoting/deceleration), then history (acute pivot mechanism), then examination findings (apprehension, medial restraint loss, abnormal tracking), and finally imaging (MPFL tear + osteochondral findings). Rewrite recommended for Clues 1, 2, 4, and 5 to establish proper differential narrowing.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easynames_answer
ORIGINAL

UPDATED

💡 Acute knee instability, pain, giving way, and visible lateral patellar displacement with spontaneous reduction
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL

UPDATED

💡 Functional limitation: symptoms triggered by deceleration, pivoting, directional changes; apprehension with weight-bearing and lateral movement
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Acute patellar dislocation occurred during athletic activity with a pivot-and-plant mechanism. Underlying anatomical predisposition such as increased Q-angle or ligamentous laxity may be present. This is the initial episode of dislocation.
✓ No changes

Clue 4
Examination
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL

UPDATED
Apprehension test reproduces fear of redislocation. Lachman and anterior drawer tests are normal, ruling out ACL injury. Patellar tracking is abnormal with lateral glide preference. Moderate joint effusion present. VMO strength noticeably diminished compared to VL.
💡 Positive apprehension test; medial restraint insufficiency; reduced quadriceps strength; effusion/swelling present
Clue 5
Imaging
✗ MAJOR
no_new_infonames_answer
ORIGINAL

UPDATED

💡 Patella reduced; possible osteochondral findings; MPFL disruption on MRI; confirms acute patellar dislocation

525. Sjögren's Syndrome
Whole Body
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The main problem is Clue 1 gives away the diagnosis too early by explicitly stating 'xerophthalmia' (a medical term for dry eye syndrome) and naming both cardinal symptoms (dry eyes AND dry mouth) in the opening. Clue 2 then largely repeats Clue 1 without adding new discriminatory information — it just elaborates on the eye dryness already mentioned. After Clues 1+2, a player has essentially heard 'dry eyes and dry mouth' twice and already suspects Sjögren syndrome. The differential should still be broader at Clue 1 (dry eyes and mouth could be medication side effects, sarcoidosis, lymphoma, radiotherapy sequelae, other autoimmune conditions). Clue 3 rescues the sequence by adding systemic features (fatigue, arthralgia) that start narrowing meaningfully. Clues 4-5 are well-positioned and specific.

💡 Redundant with Clue 1. Rephrases the eye dryness complaint and adds functional impact (reading, computer work) but doesn't narrow differential meaningfully. The reduced tear production is a restatement, not new clinical data.
Clue 3

✓ OK

NEEDS WORK

The progression doesn't narrow the differential effectively: after Clue 1 alone, a clinician has already suspected lateral epicondylitis strongly. Clues 2 and 3 just reinforce the same occupational mechanism without adding discriminating information. The puzzle needs Clue 1 rewritten to be broader (e.g., 'lateral elbow pain, onset 6 weeks, work-related'), Clue 2 to add functional detail that's distinct (e.g., day-to-day functional loss, night symptoms), and Clue 3 to add history that rules out mimics (e.g., no systemic features, no prior trauma, no cervical radiculopathy features). Clues 4 and 5 are well-positioned and specific enough to distinguish lateral epicondylitis from other elbow conditions.

527. Elbow Dislocation
Elbow
POOR
Review Notes: Critical failure in progressive reveal. Clues 1, 2, and 3 all communicate essentially the same information: acute high-force trauma to elbow with severe pain, deformity, swelling, and emergency presentation. By Clue 2, the player already knows mechanism, onset, severity, and functional loss—nothing new is learned in Clue 3. The funnel collapses: the first clue is already so specific (blow to elbow, deformity, emergency indicated) that a student reads it and immediately thinks 'dislocation or fracture-dislocation'. Clues 2 and 3 waste opportunities to narrow the differential. Clue 4 finally adds discriminating exam findings (crepitus, neurovascular signs, fixed deformity), but by then the diagnosis is obvious. A well-designed version would use Clues 1–2 to establish acute severe arm pain and immobility without specifying mechanism; Clue 3 to introduce the high-force traumatic mechanism; Clue 4 to provide exam signs (deformity, crepitus, neurovascular concern) that point to dislocation over pure fracture; and Clue 5 to confirm with imaging. Rewrites provided for Clues 1–3 to restore information hierarchy.

530. Wrist Sprain
Wrist & Hand
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: This puzzle has poor progressive reveal. Clues 1–3 are heavily redundant; they repeat the FOOSH mechanism and acute presentation without adding meaningfully distinct information. By Clue 3, a player has learned almost nothing new. Clues 4–5 are highly specific (scapholunate ligament, MRI findings) but by then the differential has collapsed. The main issue is front-loading: Clue 1 includes mechanism, functional loss, and onset character — information typically spread across Clues 1–3. Clue 5 names the ligament involved, which is nearly synonymous with the diagnosis. A better structure would: Clue 1 = broad pain/swelling after trauma; Clue 2 = functional pattern (radial/ulnar deviation pain); Clue 3 = timeline + sport context; Clue 4 = scaphoid tenderness + ligamentous findings; Clue 5 = imaging confirmation without naming the ligament.

NEEDS WORK

The progressive reveal is collapsed; a player could reasonably guess the answer after Clue 1 or Clue 2. Review Notes: This puzzle is fundamentally broken: Clue 1 literally names the diagnosis (lumbar spinal fusion L4-L5) and states the pathology, making it unsolvable as Wordle. The subsequent clues merely elaborate on an already-known answer rather than narrowing a differential. There is no clinical reasoning required. For a proper Physiodle puzzle, Clue 1 should present only symptoms (e.g., low back pain, stiffness post-surgery) without naming the surgical procedure or diagnosis. The clues should then progressively narrow through functional impact, surgical/procedural history, examination findings, and imaging—revealing that the patient has had spinal fusion. The current structure violates the core game mechanic of progressive deduction.

538. Occupational Asthma
Respiratory System
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a fatal flaw: Clue 2 essentially names the answer by explicitly linking symptoms to chemical fumes/dust in manufacturing with weekend improvement—this is occupational asthma by definition. Clue 3 then compounds the problem by naming the specific causative agents (isocyanates, epoxy resins) and ventilation trigger. By Clue 2, a clinician should only have 2-3 candidates in their differential; by end of Clue 3, occupational asthma is the only reasonable answer. The progressive reveal funnel collapses too early. Rewrite Clue 2 to remove chemical specificity and workplace attribution, focusing instead on the pattern of functional limitation (work/weekend variability, respiratory symptoms with exertion/triggers). Rewrite Clue 3 to provide history context without naming the specific agents—occupational exposure history, sensitization timeline, and workplace environmental change are sufficient.

✓ OK

541. Brachial Plexus Injury
Shoulder & Neck
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major issues with progressive reveal. Clue 1 contains mechanism detail ('traction injury during shoulder depression') that telegraphs brachial plexus injury to experienced clinicians immediately. Clue 2 names 'Erb's palsy pattern' indirectly through specific sensory distribution (lateral shoulder, thumb/index = upper trunk) and motor pattern (abduction, external rotation, elbow flexion = C5-C6), which is essentially the answer. Clue 3 explicitly names 'Erb's palsy pattern' and 'upper trunk injury pattern', eliminating any diagnostic challenge. By Clue 2, differential is already collapsed to one diagnosis. A rewritten puzzle should: (1) Clue 1: present as non-specific traumatic shoulder injury with weakness/pain only; (2) Clue 2: reveal upper limb motor/sensory pattern without naming trunk levels; (3) Clue 3: add timeline and edema/hemorrhage findings; (4) Clue 4: provide reflex/atrophy pattern distinguishing upper from lower trunk; (5) Clue 5: confirm with imaging. Current puzzle reveals answer by Clue 2.

GOOD

545. Biceps Tendinopathy
Shoulder
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle's progressive reveal is significantly compromised by Clue 2, which explicitly mentions 'resisted elbow flexion and supination'—the exact maneuver used in Speed's and Yergason tests. Combined with the anterior shoulder location from Clue 1, this essentially diagnoses biceps tendinopathy immediately. Clue 3 then compounds this by using the word 'tendinopathy' outright. After Clues 1–2, a player should have multiple diagnoses on the differential (rotator cuff pathology, acromioclavicular arthritis, subacromial impingement, glenohumeral instability); Clue 3 should narrow this to biceps tendinopathy as one of 2–3 remaining options. By naming the problem prematurely, the clues fail to create a meaningful diagnostic funnel. Clue 4 becomes redundant because the answer is already obvious. Rewrite Clues 2 and 3 to remove the giveaways and preserve diagnostic uncertainty through Clue 4.

💡 Functional limitations with overhead activities and throwing; exacerbated by resisted elbow flexion and supination; improved with rest/ice; nocturnal pain. Should narrow to shoulder/upper limb but this clue also names the exact movement that provokes the biceps (resisted elbow flexion + supination), which is essentially diagnostic.

names_answer

✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Ultrasound demonstrates biceps tendon thickening (5mm) with hypoechoic signal and increased vascularity on Doppler imaging consistent with tendinopathy. Subacromial space narrowed suggesting associated subacromial impingement. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration of proximal biceps tendon without full thickness tear.
✓ No changes
💡 Speed's test and Yergason test reproduce pain; palpable tenderness over bicipital groove; negative apprehension test; rotator cuff weakness 4+/5. This is appropriate specificity for examination clue and meaningfully differentiates biceps pathology from rotator cuff tear, glenohumeral instability, or acromioclavicular joint dysfunction.
Clue 6
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
✓ No changes
💡 Ultrasound: biceps tendon thickening (5mm), hypoechoic signal, increased vascularity; subacromial space narrowed. MRI: intrasubstance degeneration of proximal biceps tendon without full-thickness tear. Confirms biceps tendinopathy and rules out complete tear or other structural pathology. Appropriate definitive information for final clue.

NEEDS WORK

The clue sequence lacks true narrowing: by Clue 2, a skilled clinician has essentially arrived at the answer already. The progressive reveal fails because the first two clues are too specific and the diagnosis is then explicitly stated. Rewrites should remove the answer name from Clue 3, make Clue 1 more general (e.g., remove 'facial droop' language if possible), and ensure Clue 4/5 genuinely exclude mimics rather than just elaborating on CN VII dysfunction.

Knee
POOR

Clue 2 should describe a specific functional pattern (e.g., inability to decelerate, loss of rotational control on cuts) that is distinct from the raw symptom report in Clue 1. History

Shoulder
NEEDS WORK

554. Peripheral Neuropathy
Peripheral Nerves
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle struggles most in Clues 1 and 2. Clue 1 is too explicit — it already names 'neuropathy' by mentioning diabetes + progressive numbness + tingling + burning pain in feet for a known diabetic. A physio student reading only Clue 1 would narrow immediately to diabetic neuropathy, defeating the progressive reveal. Clue 2 then largely rephrases the same distal, symmetric, nocturnal pattern without adding functionally distinct information — it elaborates rather than narrows. Clues 3–5 are strong: History adds metabolic context, Examination provides discriminating signs, and Imaging confirms. 556. Proximal Hamstring Tendinopathy
Thigh
GOOD
Review Notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 establishes broad context (buttock pain, runner, training load). Clue 2 adds functional pattern and sitting intolerance (ischial load specificity). Clue 3 provides mechanism (training overload) and biomechanical factors (weak glutes, tight hip flexors). Clue 4 contains the key discriminator (Puranen-Orava test, isometric pain, hip ROM asymmetry) that confidently differentiates proximal hamstring from nearby conditions. Clue 5 seals it with imaging. Differential narrows logically at each step. No redundancy, no premature revelation, no gaps.
Clue 2
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

✓ No changes
💡 42-year-old recreational runner, 8-week buttock pain worse over 5km runs, localized to lower buttock near ischial tuberosity, recent training volume/intensity increase. This establishes age, activity context, onset timeline, symptom location, and aggravating factor (distance/intensity).

✓ OK

✓ No changes

✓ OK

✓ OK

This definitively confirms proximal hamstring tendinopathy.
557. Heart Failure
Heart
POOR
Review Notes: CRITICAL ISSUE: This puzzle is essentially a clinical vignette for heart failure from Clue 1 onwards. The diagnosis is too obvious too early. Clue 1 explicitly names 'myocardial infarction 3 years ago' and describes classic HF symptoms (orthopnea, fatigue, dyspnea on exertion), which any clinician recognizes as heart failure immediately. Clues 2–5 then over-elaborate the same story with clinical sign redundancy (orthopnea mentioned again as 'can't lie flat'; swelling mentioned as 'ankle edema' then 'peripheral pitting edema'; crackles described in both Clue 2 and Clue 4). The differential is never genuinely open. For Physiodle to work, Clue 1 must be MUCH broader and not telegraph 'post-MI patient with HF symptoms.' Consider reframing Clue 1 to describe only dyspnea and fatigue without MI history, then reveal cardiac risk factors in Clue 3. Clue 2 should focus on the orthopnea/swelling functional impact WITHOUT listing vital signs or crackles. The examination and imaging clues are solid but come too late to have diagnostic impact.

💡 Progressive dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, functional limitation (can't climb stairs). Sets the broad picture of cardiopulmonary or systemic illness.

558. Proximal Humerus Fracture
Shoulder
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Primary issue: Clue 1 is too specific and essentially names the diagnosis. A 75-year-old osteoporotic woman with FOOSH fall, immediate severe pain, deformity, and swelling almost certainly has a proximal humerus fracture — the differential is already collapsed before Clue 2. A skilled clinician would guess 'proximal humerus fracture' on Clue 1 alone. Clue 2 adds functional language but doesn't meaningfully narrow further since the mechanism and presentation already point to fracture. The puzzle needs Clue 1 rewritten to be more non-specific: present only the complaint (shoulder pain, inability to move) without the mechanism, age, osteoporosis history, or deformity. Reserve mechanism and risk factors for Clue 3. This would force the player to work through differential diagnoses (rotator cuff tear, dislocation, contusion, fracture) before imaging confirms. Clues 3–5 are well-sequenced and appropriately specific.

559. Central Sensitisation Syndrome
Systemic
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Progressive reveal structure is compromised by Clue 1 being too specific and Clue 2 being nearly diagnostic. Clue 1 mentions 'pain disproportionate to findings' which is a hallmark red flag for central sensitisation—this should come later. Clue 2 explicitly names 'allodynia' and lists sleep/cognitive symptoms with stress/weather triggers, which collectively narrow the differential dramatically after only 2 clues. By Clue 3, the diagnosis is essentially given away. Clue 4 adds the tender points exam which feels redundant with the allodynia and widespread pain already established. Rewrite Clue 1 to be purely symptomatic (pain distribution, quality, duration) without comparative language about findings. Rewrite Clue 2 to focus only on allodynia and sleep without cognitive symptoms—reserve those for Clue 3. This will preserve a proper differential funnel and allow genuine diagnostic reasoning to occur across the sequence.

560. Post-Amputation Rehabilitation
Limbs
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 names the answer directly by stating 'transtibial amputation' and explicitly labeling the patient as 'in rehabilitation phase with prosthetic fitting planned.' A player reading only Clue 1 knows the diagnosis with certainty. Clues 2–3 then redundantly elaborate on information already given (phantom pain, stump pain, diabetes complications, sepsis all mentioned in Clue 1). Clue 4 finally provides genuinely new examination data, but it arrives far too late — the game is already won. The puzzle needs complete restructuring: Clue 1 should withhold the amputation diagnosis entirely and present only broad functional complaint language; Clues 2–3 should progressively narrow toward lower-limb loss without naming it; Clue 4 should clinch the lower-limb amputation diagnosis; Clue 5 should confirm prosthetic readiness. In its current form, this is a single-clue puzzle disguised as a five-clue puzzle.

561. Tuberosity Avulsion Fracture
Knee
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Main issues: (1) Clue 2 contains examination-level specificity (palpation over tibial tubercle, palpable quadriceps, effusion assessment) that belongs in Clue 4, creating overlap and violating progressive reveal. (2) Clue 4 repeats much of what Clue 2 already established (tibial tubercle tenderness, extension weakness, swelling) without adding substantially new discriminatory information. The 'palpable but non-contractile' quadriceps detail and effusion are already implied. (3) Recommend moving palpatory findings and objective assessment details from Clue 2 to Clue 4, and tightening Clue 2 to focus purely on movement limitation and functional loss. This creates clearer separation: Clue 2 = what movement is lost; Clue 4 = why (exam findings that prove extensor mechanism failure). Progressive reveal would then flow more naturally from symptom → function → history → objective mechanism of failure → imaging confirmation.

562. Ischiofemoral Impingement
Hip
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Progressive reveal is generally sound through Clue 4, which provides highly specific examination findings (ischiofemoral stretch test, IR asymmetry, infrapiriformis tenderness) that strongly narrow to ischiofemoral impingement. However, Clue 4 is so specific that Clue 5 becomes confirmatory rather than revelatory — by that point, a skilled clinician should already be confident in the diagnosis. The imaging findings in Clue 5 are important for ruling out mimics (FAI, labral pathology) but the rewrite suggested above should make this distinction more prominent rather than just elaborating measurements. Consider whether a subtle alternative diagnosis (e.g., deep gluteal syndrome, hamstring origin syndrome) might still be on the differential after Clue 4 before Clue 5 lands — if not, the clues may be too easy overall despite good structure.

✓ No changes
💡 Positive resisted adduction, ischiofemoral stretch test (highly specific), asymmetric IR limitation (25 vs 45°), deep infrapiriformis tenderness, hamstring tenderness, antalgic gait. These are specific clinical findings that differentiate ischiofemoral impingement from other deep hip conditions (e.g., FAI, labral pathology, pure sciatica).
Clue 6
Imaging
✗ MAJOR
no_new_infotoo_easy
ORIGINAL
✓ No changes
💡 REDUNDANT with Clue 4 and Clue 3. The MRI findings (narrowed ischiofemoral space 18mm, QF edema, hamstring tendinopathy) are the anatomical confirmation, but the examination findings already named 'ischiofemoral stretch test' and described the specific regional tenderness pattern. The imaging adds quantitative measurements and rules out FAI/labral pathology, which is confirmatory but the diagnosis is essentially already given by Clue 4's examination findings.
563. Developmental Coordination Disorder
Systemic
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The main weakness is Clue 2, which largely rephrases Clue 1 without adding substantive new information. Clue 1 already establishes poor pencil grip (fine motor), poor cycling/balance (gross motor), and clumsiness. Clue 2 then elaborates on writing, drawing, buttoning (fine motor) and running, jumping, falling (gross motor) — but the player learns nothing diagnostically new; they're just seeing the same motor deficit described twice. Clue 2 should instead pivot to functional impact in a specific domain not yet covered (e.g., self-care, play, sports-specific skills) or to social/emotional consequences (e.g., social withdrawal, frustration, school refusal). The progressive reveal is thus weak at the 1→2 transition. Clues 3, 4, and 5 work well — they progressively narrow from history/rule-outs to objective findings to formal assessment. After a rewrite of Clue 2, this puzzle would flow much better.
Clue 2
Complaint

ORIGINAL
Struggles with fine motor tasks including writing, drawing, and buttoning clothes. Poor gross motor coordination with difficulty running smoothly and jumping. Falls frequently and appears clumsy compared to peers. Writing illegible with poor letter formation and spacing.

💡 Functional impact: fine motor (writing, drawing, buttoning) and gross motor (running, jumping, frequent falls). But these are largely implied by Clue 1 already — pencil grip difficulty obviously involves writing/drawing; poor balance obviously means falling.

564. Subtrochanteric Fracture
Hip
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a critical weakness at Clue 1: it essentially announces the answer by naming 'deformity with external rotation' and stating the mechanism as a fall in an osteoporotic patient on corticosteroids. Any competent clinician reading only Clue 1 would immediately think 'hip fracture' — the differential is not meaningfully broad. Clue 2 then adds minimal new discriminatory information beyond restating the regional pain and movement loss. By Clue 3, the answer is almost certain. Clues 4 and 5 are well-structured and genuinely specific, but they arrive too late to matter for gameplay. Rewrite Clue 1 to present only the mechanism and acute symptoms without the deformity descriptor or osteoporosis context; save the risk factors for Clue 3. This forces the player to reason through a broader differential (other causes of acute hip pain: dislocation, muscle tear, referred pain from spine) before the history narrows it. Clue 2 is then redundant with Clue 1 as currently written; suggest rephrasing for functional/postural detail rather than pain rephrasing.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easy

UPDATED
A 62-year-old woman on long-term corticosteroid therapy for rheumatoid arthritis falls from standing height onto her left side. Acute severe left hip pain with inability to bear weight. Left leg held in external rotation.
💡 Establishes acute severe hip pain with inability to bear weight, external rotation deformity, and risk factor (corticosteroid use, osteoporosis). Narrows to hip pathology.
Clue 2

UPDATED

💡 Describes functional loss (transfer inability, pain with movement) and swelling in upper thigh region. Confirms hip region involvement.
Clue 3

✓ OK

Clue 4

✓ OK

565. Cervical Spondylosis
Cervical Spine
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle severely. Clues 1–3 essentially repeat the same information (progressive neurological symptoms, cervical degeneration, age, chronic course). The differential is already crushed by clue 1 alone, leaving almost no room for clinical reasoning. Clue 1 uses near-diagnostic language ('progressive upper extremity symptoms,' 'neurological symptoms,' 'functional limitation') that only fits a handful of conditions, and clue 3 explicitly names 'cervical degenerative disc disease' and 'spinal cord compression.' Clues 2 and 3 add almost no new information—they elaborate on what's already stated. Clue 4 is also generic (could describe many upper motor neuron lesions) and doesn't meaningfully discriminate myelopathy from, say, motor neurone disease. Only clues 4–5 together approach adequate specificity. A rewrite must broaden clue 1 dramatically, ensure clue 2 adds functional detail not implied by clue 1, clue 3 adds timeline/mechanism not yet mentioned, clue 4 discriminates with specific neuro findings, and clue 5 seals with imaging. This puzzle currently plays more like a trivia question than a diagnostic reasoning game.
Clue 1

💡 Age (60), chronic neck pain, progressive upper extremity symptoms, stiffness, neurological progression

💡 Exacerbation with prolonged sitting and neck extension; functional limitations with sustained positioning
Clue 3
History
✗ MAJOR
no_new_infonames_answer
ORIGINAL
Long-standing cervical degenerative disc disease has progressed over decades. Chronic neck strain and age-related joint degeneration have contributed. Spinal cord compression may be developing gradually.
UPDATED
Patient reports morning stiffness lasting 45 minutes. No prior trauma or neck injury. No imaging performed previously. Symptoms gradually worsened despite two courses of physiotherapy (6 months apart). Intermittent episodes of grip weakness and difficulty with fine motor tasks (buttoning, writing).
💡 Redundant: 'long-standing cervical degenerative disc disease' and 'age-related joint degeneration' already heavily implied by clue 1 (60-year-old, chronic, progressive). 'Spinal cord compression may be developing' repeats clue 1's neurological progression.

✗ MAJOR

Positive Lhermitte sign (electric shock down spine with neck flexion). Lower limb weakness present (hip flexion 4/5 bilaterally). Gait ataxic.

Clue 5

566. Vestibular Neuritis
Vestibular
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a solid funnel structure through Clue 3, but Clue 3 itself is largely redundant—it restates information already embedded in Clues 1 and 2 (viral prodrome, sudden onset, absence of hearing loss) without adding genuinely new discriminatory details. Clue 5 also feels somewhat repetitive: after Clue 4's definitive examination findings (positive HIT, caloric asymmetry), the imaging clue mostly confirms what is already strongly suspected rather than providing a critical new discriminator. The puzzle would be stronger if Clue 3 added distinct historical or demographic context (e.g., recurrence pattern, systemic illness, medication history, or occupational factors), and if Clue 5 provided imaging that actively ruled out a close mimicker (e.g., 'internal auditory canal patent; no evidence of labyrinthitis or meningitis involvement'). Overall coherence is good, but progressive narrowing could be tighter.

567. Mallet Finger
Wrist & Hand
NEEDS WORK

Clue 1 already names 'hyperflexion injury' with explicit DIP loss of extension and distal phalanx pain — this is nearly pathognomonic for mallet finger and gives away the answer prematurely. Clue 2 essentially rephrases the same findings (DIP flexion, inability to extend) without meaningful new functional information. Clue 4 contains an error (FDS is not tested in mallet finger assessment) and again repeats ROM and neurovascular findings. The differential narrowing happens in Clues 1–2, leaving Clues 3–4 feeling redundant. Clue 3 is appropriate and adds timeline context. Clue 5 is appropriately specific but arrives after the diagnosis is already obvious. Rewrite should make Clue 1 broader (acute finger injury with loss of extension, minimal detail on exact mechanism or joint), allow Clue 2 to establish the isolated DIP loss of active extension, let Clue 3 narrow mechanism and timeline, use Clue 4 for detailed exam and ROM quantification, and reserve Clue 5 for the avulsion fracture confirmation.

570. Jersey Finger
Wrist & Hand
POOR
Review Notes: The puzzle has a critical failure: Clue 1 essentially names the answer ('jersey finger' is the colloquial term for FDP avulsion from traction injury; 'ring finger caught in jersey' + 'loss of finger flexion' + 'swelling over proximal forearm' is almost pathognomonic). Clue 2 then explicitly states 'flexor digitorum profundus avulsion' — naming the diagnosis outright. After reading Clue 1 alone, an experienced clinician would confidently guess the answer before seeing Clues 2-5. The progressive reveal principle is completely violated. Clues 3-5 are well-sequenced and appropriate in content, but Clues 1-2 must be rewritten to remove the obvious mechanism and the anatomical diagnosis name. Clue 1 should describe a functional complaint (sudden inability to flex) without mentioning how it happened or which forearm region is swelling. 571. Cervical Radiculopathy
Cervical Spine
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major problem: Clues 1, 2, and 3 all explicitly name or strongly imply 'nerve root compression' and 'dermatome pattern' — the core diagnostic feature. By Clue 2, any physio student knows this is radiculopathy. Clues 1 and 2 are too specific too early and eliminate the differential narrowing process. Clue 1 should describe only symptoms (pain, numbness, weakness) without specifying radiation pattern or mechanism. Clue 2 should add the functional/activity detail that reveals the radicular distribution pattern. Clue 3 should provide history that distinguishes cervical radiculopathy from other causes of arm pain (trauma, spondylosis history, progressive course). The puzzle collapses the differential far too early.

572. Cervical Spine Surgery
Neck
POOR
Review Notes: FUNDAMENTAL FAILURE: Clue 1 completely names the answer — it states the exact diagnosis ('anterior cervical discectomy and fusion at C5-6 for cervical myelopathy'). This destroys the game. Clue 2 adds functional color but is secondary to what was already disclosed. Clue 3 is almost entirely redundant with Clue 1, merely restating pre-operative findings and surgical rationale without new discriminatory information. Even if Clue 1 were rewritten to be vague (e.g., 'patient reports stiffness and arm numbness 6 weeks after elective neck surgery'), Clues 2 and 3 still fail the progressive reveal principle — they don't narrow a differential, they elaborate on a single known case. The puzzle conflates 'case detail progression' (which is narrative elaboration) with 'diagnostic narrowing' (which is what Physiodle requires). For this to work: Clue 1 must describe only symptoms (stiffness, numbness, hoarseness) without mentioning surgery. Clue 2 must reveal the surgical context. Clue 3 must differentiate between post-op complications (e.g., nerve injury, infection, implant failure vs. normal recovery). Clues 4–5 should confirm the specific post-operative status. As written, this puzzle is unplayable — there is no diagnostic reasoning required.

574. Hearing Loss
Ear
POOR
Review Notes: CRITICAL ISSUES: (1) Clue 1 essentially names the answer ('hearing loss' + 'hearing difficulty' + high-frequency pattern + age 68 = age-related sensorineural hearing loss). A physio student guesses this on attempt 1. (2) Clue 2 adds almost no new diagnostic information beyond Clue 1 — it elaborates functional impact but doesn't narrow differential further. (3) MAJOR INCOHERENCE: Clue 4 suggests air-bone gaps and mixed/conductive loss; Clue 5 then contradicts this with pure sensorineural findings and normal tympanometry. This breaks the clinical story and confuses players. (4) Clue 4 includes cerumen removal (a procedure/action) rather than pure examination findings. The puzzle needs significant restructuring: make Clue 1 much broader (remove 'hearing' terminology if possible, keep only age/progressive symptoms), ensure Clue 4 and 5 tell a coherent story, and ensure audiometric findings actually discriminate sensorineural from conductive (or mixed).

579. Quadriceps Tendinopathy
Thigh
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major issue: Clue 1 is far too specific and essentially gives away the diagnosis. It names the exact location (superior patellar pole), the sport, the mechanism, and even describes swelling at the tendon insertion — all strong indicators of quadriceps tendinopathy. A experienced clinician would have very few differential diagnoses after Clue 1 alone. Clue 2 largely repeats information already established in Clue 1 (jumping aggravates, rest helps, explosive movements problematic) without adding meaningful new dimension — it elaborates mechanically but doesn't narrow the differential further. Clue 3 compounds this by again discussing jumping, training volume, and prior tendon history all already implied in Clues 1-2. By Clue 3, a clinician should have reached high confidence; instead, the puzzle is still layering foundational information. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriately specific and do differentiate quadriceps from patellar tendon pathology. Rewrite Clue 1 to remove anatomical location specificity and swelling description. Rewrite Clue 2 to focus on functional impact beyond what Clue 1 already establishes (e.g., recovery timeline, activity tolerance ceiling, daily function impact). This will create a genuine funnel effect.
Clue 1
Complaint

580. Serratus Anterior Injury
Shoulder
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has critical issues in Clues 1 and 2. Clue 1 explicitly names 'scapular winging' which is pathognomonic for long thoracic nerve/serratus anterior injury — this gives away the answer before players have made any guesses. Clue 2 doubles down by describing the exact clinical test (scapular winging on push-up) that confirms the diagnosis. Clue 3 makes it worse by literally saying 'long thoracic nerve injury suspected'. By the time a player reads Clues 1–3, there is essentially no diagnostic uncertainty remaining. The puzzle is solved before any guesses are made. Clues 4 and 5 are well-written and specific, but they're providing confirmatory detail to a diagnosis that was already obvious. The progressive reveal funnel has collapsed: instead of gradually narrowing from 10+ conditions (Clue 1) → 5–6 (Clue 2) → 2–3 (Clue 3) → 1 (Clue 4) → confirmed (Clue 5), this puzzle goes from essentially 1 diagnosis at Clue 1. Rewrite Clues 1–3 to remove anatomy-specific and pathology-specific language, and to present a functional picture that could plausibly fit multiple shoulder/scapular conditions.

583. Radial Nerve Palsy
Upper Limb
NEEDS WORK

As written, this puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle.

Mild endplate changes and osteophyte formation at multiple levels. No acute findings. Imaging findings do not explain severity of reported pain or functional limitation.
💡 MRI findings: degenerative changes, disc bulging without cord compression, facet arthrosis, hyperostosis, possible vascular insufficiency
587. Peroneal Tendinopathy
Ankle & Foot
GOOD
Review Notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 is appropriately broad (insidious lateral ankle pain in runner — could be ligament, tendon, sinus tarsi, OS peroneum). Clue 2 narrows functionally and mechanically (inversion activities, training volume). Clue 3 is the turning point: prior sprain + subluxation history + overload mechanism crystallizes the differential around peroneal pathology specifically. Clue 4 examination findings (peroneal groove tenderness, resisted eversion pain, swelling) directly test peroneal function and eliminate other lateral ankle candidates. Clue 5 imaging seals the diagnosis with tendon-specific pathology. Each clue adds genuinely new, narrowing information. No redundancy. Appropriate anatomical specificity at each stage.
Clue 1
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

ORIGINAL

History
✓ OK

590. Meralgia Paraesthetica
Thigh
GOOD
Review Notes: Excellent progressive reveal. Clue 1 casts wide net (lateral thigh pain could be L4 radiculopathy, hip pathology, TFL syndrome, etc.). Clue 3 adds key history (occupational compression, obesity, gradual onset) that strongly suggests compression neuropathy over trauma or muscle injury. Clue 4 provides the critical discriminator: LFCN-specific sensory findings with normal motor exam and localized inguinal tenderness—this essentially confirms meralgia paraesthetica and rules out L4 radiculopathy (which would have motor involvement and different pain pattern). Clue 5 provides definitive electrophysiologic and imaging confirmation. Clinician would be highly confident after Clue 4 and certain after Clue 5. Each clue meaningfully narrows the differential and adds distinct information appropriate to its position in the sequence.
Clue 1

✓ OK

Clue 3

591. Sarcopenia
Systemic
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a structural problem: Clue 1 and Clue 2 are nearly interchangeable. Both describe the same functional losses (weakness, reduced ambulation, chair rise difficulty). Clue 2 adds grip strength numbers and gait velocity but doesn't eliminate alternative diagnoses — a 76-year-old with progressive weakness could still be deconditioning, myopathy, neurological disease, or metabolic dysfunction. The differential should narrow meaningfully after Clue 2; instead, the player learns only that the weakness is quantified and symmetrical, which is still compatible with many diagnoses. Clue 3 (History) is where true narrowing occurs (malnutrition + inactivity + age = sarcopenia more likely). Clue 4 and Clue 5 have minor redundancy with Clue 2 (grip strength and gait speed repeated) but are largely appropriate. Recommend rewriting Clue 2 to focus on asymmetry, sensory status, and pattern of weakness (e.g., proximal vs. distal) to actively differentiate from myopathy or neuropathy. Clue 5 could be tightened by showing a DEXA value clearly below cutoff and clarifying that MRI excludes myositis or dystrophy.
Clue 1
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
A 76-year-old woman presents with progressive weakness and functional decline over 2 years. Difficulty rising from chair without armrest support. Walking slower and shorter distances than previously. Recent fall from trip on uneven pavement.
✓ No changes
💡 Age, progressive weakness and functional decline over 2 years, specific functional losses (chair rise, ambulation distance reduction), recent fall context.
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Reduced grip strength (12 kg versus 22 kg in contralateral hand). Difficulty with stairs and walking long distances. Loss of independence with heavy household tasks. Slow gait velocity (0.7 m/sec versus normal 1.4 m/sec). Difficulty getting out of bed and toilet independently.
UPDATED
Reduced grip strength (12 kg, versus 22 kg in right hand). Symmetrical weakness affecting both upper and lower extremities. Difficulty with isolated fine motor tasks and sustained gripping. No focal weakness or sensory loss noted. Weakness not attributable to pain or joint limitation.
💡 Redundant with Clue 1. Rephrases the same functional limitations (chair rise, stairs, walking) and adds grip strength measurement, but doesn't narrow the differential beyond what progressive weakness + functional decline already suggested. A 76-year-old with progressive weakness and reduced ambulation could be sarcopenia, deconditioning, neurological disease, metabolic disorder, myopathy, etc. — this clue doesn't eliminate any of those.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Age 76 with sedentary lifestyle and reduced physical activity. Inadequate protein intake (40g daily versus recommended 1.0-1.2 g/kg body weight). Loss of appetite with reduced food intake. Multiple comorbidities including osteoarthritis and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease limiting activity.

This does narrow the differential — the combination of age + inactivity + malnutrition + comorbidities now makes sarcopenia, deconditioning, or cachetic state more likely than neurological disease or primary myopathy. Good narrowing here.
Clue 4
Examination
⚠ MINOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Visible muscle wasting in upper extremities and thighs. Reduced grip strength 12 kg (abnormal cutoff <16 kg for women). Slow gait speed 0.7 m/sec (abnormal <1.0 m/sec). Reduced lower extremity strength particularly hip flexors and knee extensors (4/5). Difficulty rising from chair in <12 seconds (requires 18 seconds).
UPDATED
Visible muscle wasting in bilateral upper extremities and thighs. Hip flexor and knee extensor strength 4/5 bilaterally; shoulder and elbow strength 4+/5. Tight hip flexors, otherwise no contractures. Normal tone. No tremor, fasciculations, or sensory loss. Mild difficulty with single-leg stance.
💡 Visible muscle wasting (structural finding), quantified grip strength cutoff interpretation, gait velocity with cutoff, lower extremity strength testing (4/5, hip flexors and knee extensors specified), Timed Up and Go proxy (18-second chair rise). This is appropriate specificity for Clue 4 — it provides objective tests that distinguish sarcopenia from pure deconditioning or comorbidity-driven decline. However, there is modest overlap with Clue 2 (grip strength and gait speed mentioned in both). The NEW information here is the visible wasting, strength grading, and muscle-specific weakness pattern.
Clue 5
Imaging
⚠ MINOR
ORIGINAL
DEXA scan confirms low fat-free mass index (5.8 kg/m2, cutoff <5.75). MRI thigh shows reduced muscle mass with increased intramuscular fat infiltration. UPDATED
DEXA scan: fat-free mass index 5.2 kg/m² (below sarcopenia cutoff <5.75). MRI thigh: marked reduction in muscle cross-sectional area with diffuse intramuscular fat infiltration; no focal muscle edema or signal change to suggest inflammatory myopathy. Prealbumin 17 mg/dL (low, <20). Albumin 3.1 g/dL (low). CRP 2.1 mg/L (normal). TSH, creatine kinase normal. Vitamin D 18 ng/mL (deficient).
💡 DEXA fat-free mass index (5.8 kg/m², just above cutoff 5.75 — tight diagnostic margin), MRI shows reduced muscle mass with intramuscular fat infiltration (structural confirmation of sarcopenia), protein markers low-normal (prealbumin 18, albumin 3.2 — supporting nutritional component), CRP mildly elevated (inflammatory state), vitamin D insufficient. This is appropriately specific and confirmatory. However, the DEXA cutoff of 5.8 versus 5.75 is extremely borderline and slightly undermines the diagnosis — the clue would be stronger if imaging more clearly separated sarcopenia from other diagnoses (e.g., myopathy, cachexia, steroid-induced myopathy would show different MRI patterns or lab profiles).
592. Post-Traumatic Stress Disorder - Physical Rehabilitation
General
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle has critical structural problems. The diagnosis is named explicitly or heavily implied in nearly every clue ('PTSD', 'psychiatric evaluation confirms PTSD diagnosis', 'trauma-related avoidance', 'startle response'). The progressive reveal principle is violated — the answer is essentially confirmed by Clue 2. Additionally, Clues 1–3 all establish the same core story (post-MVA with trauma symptoms) without meaningful differential narrowing. Clue 4 adds physical findings that are non-specific to PTSD and could belong to simple post-whiplash syndrome. Clue 5 is redundant: psychiatric diagnosis should not be 'imaging' in a physio game — this is a physiotherapy rehabilitation scenario, not a psychiatry diagnosis game. The puzzle conflates psychiatric diagnosis (PTSD) with physiotherapy management of post-traumatic pain and movement dysfunction. For a physio game, the answer should be a *movement/physical diagnosis* (e.g., 'Chronic Post-Traumatic Whiplash Syndrome with Central Sensitization' or 'Post-Traumatic Pain Syndrome with Movement Avoidance'), not a psychiatric condition. Rewrite needed at all levels to remove diagnostic giveaways, establish genuine differential (whiplash vs. other post-MVA presentations), and focus on physical rehabilitation reasoning.

595. Post-Cardiac Surgery Rehabilitation
Heart
POOR
Review Notes: CRITICAL ISSUES: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 literally names the answer ('post-CABG presents for cardiac rehabilitation') and includes specific post-operative details (sternotomy, CABG x3, grafts, EF numbers). A player reading Clue 1 knows the diagnosis immediately. Clues 2-4 are largely redundant elaborations of the same clinical picture already established in Clue 1, adding no meaningful differential narrowing. There is NO differential diagnosis here — the puzzle reads like a single case presentation split into five sections rather than a progressive clinical reasoning funnel. For Physiodle to work, Clue 1 should describe ONLY the symptoms (dyspnea, chest discomfort, fatigue, exercise limitation) WITHOUT mentioning cardiac surgery, CABG, or post-operative status. The post-operative context should emerge in Clue 3 (History), which would then narrow the differential from broad cardiopulmonary/constitutional conditions to specifically post-surgical recovery. As written, this is not a puzzle — it's a case report. It requires fundamental restructuring to function as a Wordle-style game.

Clue 3 adds little new mechanism detail beyond Clue 1's description of the traumatic event. By Clue 4, the diagnosis is already obvious from Clues 1–2. The progressive reveal funnel is collapsed; there is no meaningful narrowing. Rewrite Clue 1 to be broader (complaint + demographics only, no deformity descriptor or joint naming), Clue 2 to focus purely on functional loss from pain/restriction, Clue 3 to add timeline/comorbidity context, and Clue 4 to differentiate between anterior vs. posterior dislocation and rule out other shoulder injuries.

The progressive reveal lacks the expected funnel effect—by Clue 1, an experienced clinician is already thinking hamstring strain rather than a broad differential of 10+ thigh pathologies. Clues 3, 4, and 5 are appropriate in detail level but their discriminatory power is diminished by the early giveaway. Consider broadening Clue 1 to remove sport-specific context and anatomical region specificity, and refocusing Clue 2 to add genuinely new functional or historical detail.

604. Trigger Finger
Wrist & Hand
GOOD
Review Notes: Overall, this is a well-constructed puzzle with strong progressive narrowing through Clue 4. The examination findings (nodule at A1 pulley, reproducible triggering, relief with passive extension) are genuinely discriminatory and separate trigger finger from other hand pathologies. Clue 5 is the weakest link—after the classic examination findings in Clue 4, ultrasound imaging mainly confirms what palpation already revealed. Rewrite suggests framing imaging around what imaging ADDS (exclusion of joint disease, fluid localisation) rather than what it repeats. The puzzle is not 'too easy' because Clue 1 alone doesn't give away trigger finger (catching + locking + clicking at MCP could also suggest joint dysfunction or early osteoarthritis), and Clue 2 adds meaningful functional constraints. This is a solid, playable puzzle.
Clue 1
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
A 48-year-old woman presents with catching and locking sensation in the right ring finger at the MCP joint. Clicking noise with finger flexion and extension. Worsens in morning.
✓ No changes
💡 Establishes symptom profile (catching, locking, clicking) and temporal pattern (morning worse). Age and gender provided. Hand region identified but not yet narrowed to specific diagnosis.
Clue 2
Activity
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

✓ No changes
💡 Narrows functional impact to fine motor tasks and grip activities. NEW distinction: pain with full extension is a specific movement limitation not explicitly stated in Clue 1. Clarifies that dexterity (not strength) is the primary functional loss.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Gradual onset over 3 months. No acute trauma. Repetitive gripping activities in workplace (packing work). Symptoms progressive with worsening locking episodes.
✓ No changes
💡 Adds critical timeline (3 months gradual) and mechanism (repetitive gripping/packing work). Eliminates acute trauma and inflammatory arthropathy. Workplace ergonomics narrows differential toward overuse conditions. Progressive worsening with locking episodes is consistent with trigger finger but would also fit tenosynovitis or early osteoarthritis.
Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

✓ No changes
💡 Highly specific findings: (1) Nodular thickening over A1 pulley—this is pathognomonic for trigger finger and rules out most other hand conditions; (2) Triggering reproduced and relieved by passive extension—classic mechanical finding; (3) Swelling at pulley level—confirms inflammatory component. This clue is VERY strong and almost confirms the diagnosis. A clinician reading clues 1-4 would be >90% confident.

no_new_info

💡 Confirms nodule on ultrasound (echoes Clue 4 palpation finding but visualises it—marginal new info). Synovial fluid and MRI confirmation are descriptive rather than discriminatory at this stage. After Clue 4, imaging adds little diagnostically; the examination findings were already near-definitive.
605. Hamstring Strain
Thigh
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a fundamental progressive reveal problem: Clues 1 and 2 are both highly specific to hamstring strain, making the differential far too narrow too early. Clue 1 names 'posterior thigh pain during sprint' with 'abrupt onset during acceleration' — this is essentially pathognomonic for acute hamstring strain in a runner and eliminates most other thigh conditions immediately. Clue 2 adds visible swelling and ecchymosis but doesn't materially narrow further (already implied by acute traumatic mechanism in Clue 1). Clue 3 is appropriate. Clue 4 has some redundancy with Clue 2 (ecchymosis mentioned again; swelling/warmth already expected from Clue 2). By Clue 4, the answer is obvious. Clue 1 should be rewritten to remove sports-specific language ('sprinter', 'acceleration phase') and keep only the core symptom (acute posterior thigh pain). This would expand the differential to include other acute posterior thigh pathology (gluteal strain, sciatic nerve irritation, discogenic referred pain, DVT). Clues 2 and 4 overlap significantly and should be differentiated — Clue 2 focuses on functional limitation and gross signs, Clue 4 should focus on discriminatory clinical tests that distinguish hamstring strain from other posterior thigh conditions.

609. Ankylosing Spondylitis
Spine
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a significant progressive reveal problem. Clue 3 is largely redundant with Clues 1 and 2 — it rephrases the same clinical narrative (age, onset pattern, morning stiffness character, no trauma) without adding discriminatory new information. A rewritten Clue 3 should focus on what excludes other spondyloarthropathies or mechanical spine conditions (e.g., absence of preceding infection/GI symptoms, HLA-B27 status as a potential finding, or other systemic features). Clue 5 is also problematic because it explicitly names the pathology (bilateral sacroiliitis + syndesmophytes + HLA-B27 + elevated inflammatory markers = ankylosing spondylitis). While confirmatory imaging is appropriate for Clue 5, it should be less diagnostic in tone. The puzzle works clinically but fails the progressive reveal test: a player guessing after Clue 2 has essentially the same information as after Clue 3, and after Clue 5 they have no diagnostic uncertainty remaining.

610. Atelectasis
Lungs
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle has severe problems with progressive reveal. Clue 1 is far too specific—it essentially names the condition (post-op dyspnea + hypoxemia + fever on POD3 after abdominal surgery screams atelectasis to any clinician). The differential at this point is not broad; it's narrowed to 2-3 diagnoses immediately. Clues 2 and 3 then add almost nothing new—they simply rephrase or elaborate on the same information (pain limiting breathing, shallow breathing, immobility, secretion retention). Clue 4 provides physical exam findings that confirm what was already stated in Clue 1 (reduced breath sounds at bases, fever, hypoxemia). By Clue 5, there is no meaningful narrowing left to do. The puzzle should start much broader (generic dyspnea in a post-op patient), progressively reveal the timing/context/mechanism, then home in on the specific regional findings and imaging. As written, a player has little incentive to think through a differential—the answer is heavily implied from the start.

611. Degenerative Disc Disease
Spine
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle suffers from poor progressive reveal. Clues 1 and 2 are heavily overlapping (both emphasize mechanical pain worse with activity/sitting, relief with rest), providing minimal new discriminatory information between them. Clue 2 should shift focus entirely to functional limitations and how the patient compensates, rather than restating the pain pattern. Clue 3 appropriately adds occupational/lifestyle risk factors but these don't narrow the differential much—many mechanical LBP conditions share these risks. Clue 4 provides good objective findings but focuses on what is NOT present (no radiculopathy, normal neuro) rather than what IS present that distinguishes DDD specifically. By Clue 4, a clinician is still thinking 'non-radicular mechanical LBP' not specifically 'DDD.' Only Clue 5 (imaging) confirms DDD. The funnel is too wide until the final clue. Consider Clue 2 rewrite to describe specific movement patterns and compensations; consider Clue 4 to include findings that differentiate multilevel degenerative disc disease from, say, primary facet syndrome (e.g., worse with flexion/loading patterns, better with extension or unloading).

612. Overtraining Syndrome
Systemic
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a fatal flaw in Clue 2: it merely restates Clue 1 (elevated resting HR, fatigue, infections, delayed recovery) without adding functional or activity-based discrimination. The progression should shift from 'what is the patient experiencing' (Clue 1) to 'what can't they do and why' (Clue 2). Rewriting Clue 2 to focus on loss of training performance, subjective heaviness, and psychological/motivational changes would create proper progressive narrowing. Clues 3-5 work well and follow logical progression. After rewriting Clue 2, the differential funnel becomes: Clue 1 (broad systemic fatigue) → Clue 2 (training intolerance with autonomic signs) → Clue 3 (training error and recovery deficit as mechanism) → Clue 4 (objective autonomic dysfunction, normal muscular exam) → Clue 5 (neuroendocrine markers confirming overtraining).
Clue 1
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

✓ No changes
💡 Establishes fatigue, declining performance, and recurrent infections in an elite endurance athlete over 3 months. Sets context for systemic illness rather than localized injury.

Activity

no_new_info

UPDATED
Unable to sustain previous training paces. Completes sessions but lacks usual power and speed. Feels 'heavy' during running. Takes 48-72 hours to feel recovered between hard sessions versus previous 24-hour recovery. Irritable and unmotivated about training despite previous enthusiasm.
💡 Rephrases Clue 1 findings (elevated resting HR, fatigue, infections, delayed recovery) with minimal new functional information. Describes the same symptoms in different wording rather than adding distinct activity-based limitations.

Clue 4

✓ OK

VO2 max testing shows 10% reduction compared to previous testing. Psychological assessment shows mood disturbance and depression symptoms. Musculoskeletal examination normal.

💡 Objective measurements (resting HR elevation, delayed heart rate recovery, VO2 max decline, mood disturbance) with normal musculoskeletal exam. 613. Frailty Syndrome
Systemic
POOR
Review Notes: Critical flaws: (1) Clue 1 is far too specific — explicitly states 'progressive functional decline' and 'multiple comorbidities' which telegraphs frailty syndrome before the player has guessed; the phrase 'frail' should never appear in early clues. (2) Clue 2 repeats functional decline and adds vague constructs ('reduced life expectancy') that belong in prognosis, not activity limitations. (3) Clue 4 uses non-specific language ('weakness throughout,' 'frail appearance') instead of measurable findings (grip strength, MMT grades, timed tests). (4) Clue 5 is mislabeled — it contains labs and functional scores, not imaging findings; the clue title suggests imaging but delivers something else. (5) No meaningful differential narrowing occurs between clues 1–3; by Clue 1, an astute clinician has already diagnosed frailty syndrome. The puzzle needs severe restructuring: Clue 1 should be vague (e.g., 'increased falls and fatigue'), Clue 2 should specify functional loss (ADL dependence), Clue 3 should introduce comorbidity burden and weight loss, Clue 4 should provide specific exam findings (strength grades, gait speed, cognitive testing), and Clue 5 should show labs confirming malnutrition and anemia.

614. Dementia-Related Mobility Decline
Systemic
POOR
Review Notes: CRITICAL ISSUES: (1) Clue 1 immediately names 'Alzheimer's dementia' and explicitly states 'gait deterioration' — this essentially names the answer and eliminates the differential reasoning entirely. A player cannot guess anything other than dementia-related conditions after reading it. (2) Clues 2–5 are largely REDUNDANT with each other, all describing the same shuffling gait pattern, reduced stride, postural abnormalities, and cognitive involvement from different angles without adding genuinely new discriminatory information. (3) The puzzle collapses as a diagnostic tool — there is no progressive narrowing of a differential. After Clue 1, a clinician knows it's dementia-related mobility decline. Clues 2–5 just elaborate the same features. (4) A proper puzzle would start with only 'progressive gait deterioration and increased falls' in a frail elderly patient (broad differential: Parkinson's, normal pressure hydrocephalus, cervical myelopathy, frailty, stroke, sarcopenia, etc.), then progressively reveal dementia severity, gait pattern specifics, and imaging findings to narrow to dementia-related decline. Rewrite needed from ground up with removal of diagnosis from Clue 1 and genuine information progression across all 5 clues.

UPDATED

no_new_info

615. Knee Osteoarthritis
Knee
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle catastrophically. Clue 1 essentially names the diagnosis by explicitly saying 'chronic knee pain,' 'weight-bearing,' 'morning stiffness,' and functional limitation — the classic OA presentation. Clue 2 then repeats the same functional information verbatim without adding anything new. Clue 3 compounds the problem by literally naming 'degenerative joint disease' and 'osteoarthritic changes,' removing any remaining diagnostic mystery. By Clue 3, there is no differential left. A player with minimal physio knowledge would guess correctly after Clue 1. The puzzle should start much broader (pain in the knee region, reduced mobility) and progressively narrow through activity impact, then history/timeline, then examination findings that distinguish OA from other knee pathologies (meniscal tears, ligament injuries, bursitis, etc.), and finally imaging confirmation. Substantial rewrite needed.

617. Rheumatoid Arthritis
Systemic
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle fundamentally. Clues 1, 2, and 3 are nearly identical — they all describe the same symmetric polyarthritis, morning stiffness, small joint involvement, and systemic symptoms. A player learning Clue 2 learns almost nothing new beyond Clue 1; Clue 3 adds marginally more. The answer is essentially given away by Clue 1 (symmetric polyarthritis + morning stiffness >2 hours + systemic symptoms is pathognomonic for RA). Clues 4 and 5 are strong and appropriately specific, but by then the game is already over. A skilled clinician would guess RA after Clue 1 alone. To fix this: Clue 1 should be much broader (just polyarticular hand pain, worse in morning); Clue 2 should add functional impact and distribution details; Clue 3 should add mechanism/timeline/risk factors; Clue 4 should add rheumatoid-specific signs (nodules, squeeze test); Clue 5 should confirm with serology and imaging.

619. Chronic Pain Syndrome
Whole Body
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a fundamental problem: Clue 1 essentially names or strongly implies the answer by listing the classic diagnostic criteria for Chronic Pain Syndrome (widespread pain, normal investigations, functional disability disproportionate to findings, mood disturbance, social withdrawal). A player seeing only Clue 1 would already guess correctly. Clue 2 then repeats the same information (widespread pain, functional loss, sleep issues) without adding meaningful differentiation. The progressive reveal principle is violated early. Clues 3, 4, and 5 are individually good, but the damage is done — the puzzle is solved at Clue 1. Recommend rewriting Clue 1 to present only complaint symptoms without diagnosis-specific language, and moving psychosocial and functional disproportionality information to later clues where they become discriminatory.
Clue 2

620. Freiberg's Disease
Foot
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major problem: Clues 1 and 2 are heavily redundant. Clue 1 already specifies 'second metatarsal head' with 'weight-bearing pain', and Clue 2 essentially repeats this verbatim ('pain localized to metatarsal head region', 'worse with weight bearing') plus adds swelling. This overlap means the player learns almost nothing new after their first wrong guess. Clue 2 should pivot to functional impact (gait pattern, antalgic features) or examination-observable signs (swelling/gait changes) rather than re-emphasizing location and activity triggers. Clue 3 then repeats the 'gradual progressive course over 2 months' and 'sports participation' context already given in Clue 1. The progressive reveal funnel collapses early: by Clue 2, the player has already heard the diagnosis territory (adolescent metatarsal head osteonecrosis) twice. Clues 3–5 provide solid differentiation once reached, but the early clues waste the player's guesses without advancing the clinical reasoning meaningfully.

621. Shoulder Dislocation
Shoulder
POOR
Review Notes: Major structural failure. Clues 1, 2, and 3 are severely redundant — they all describe the same anterior shoulder dislocation with identical mechanism, positioning, severity, and athlete profile. Clue 1 essentially names the answer ('anterior shoulder dislocation', 'forced external rotation and abduction', 'arm held in protective position', 'immediate severe pain'). A physio student would have the answer after Clue 1 alone. Clues 2 and 3 add almost no new discriminatory information — they rephrase the same clinical picture. The puzzle only becomes genuinely progressive at Clue 4 (examination findings), which should have come much earlier or Clues 1-3 should be rewritten to progressively reveal mechanism → functional limitation → comorbidity/risk factors. Currently fails the funnel principle entirely: clues 1-3 should narrow from broad to specific, but instead they widen laterally (repeating the same facts in different language).

Examination
✓ OK

623. Erb's Palsy
Upper Limb
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle has catastrophic differential narrowing problems. Clue 1 explicitly names 'shoulder dystocia' as the preceding event—a near-pathognomonic feature of Erb's palsy and few other conditions. Clue 2 then describes 'waiter's tip position,' which is virtually diagnostic terminology. By Clue 3, the diagnosis is completely given away: it explicitly states 'brachial plexus injury,' 'C5-C6 nerve roots,' and 'upper trunk injury pattern.' A player reading only Clues 1-3 would have no meaningful differential to work through. Clues 4 and 5 add anatomical detail but no new discriminatory power. The progressive reveal has failed entirely. Clue 1 should describe only neonatal weakness without mentioning mechanism. Clue 2 should describe functional loss without naming the posture. Clue 3 should provide mechanism and timeline only. Clue 4 should provide examination findings that permit regional localization (C5-C6 vs lower trunk). Clue 5 should provide imaging confirmation.

624. Chronic Ankle Instability
Ankle & Foot
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Clue 1 gives away the diagnosis too directly by naming both the mechanism (inversion) and the problem (recurrent instability) in plain language. Clue 2 largely repeats Clue 1 without meaningfully narrowing the differential—proprioceptive deficits are already implied by 'ankle giving way,' not new information. Clue 3 appropriately adds history and timeline. Clues 4 and 5 are solid discriminators. The progressive reveal funnel is broken in the first two clues: by Clue 1, an experienced clinician has already narrowed to chronic ankle instability, making the remaining clues feel confirmatory rather than revelatory. Rewrite Clue 1 to be more symptom-focused and less mechanism-specific, and rewrite Clue 2 to focus on true functional limitations (terrain, task types) rather than repeating the mechanism of instability.
Clue 1

625. Cerebral Palsy
CNS
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle catastrophically. Clues 1, 2, and 3 all explicitly name or strongly imply 'cerebral palsy' through language like 'spastic diplegia,' 'scissor gait,' 'perinatal asphyxia,' 'permanent motor dysfunction,' and 'nonprogressive.' A player has essentially solved the puzzle after Clue 2 at the latest. The differential never narrows—it collapses. Clue 1 should establish only developmental delay and movement abnormality without specifying tone type, lower limb involvement, or gait pattern. Clue 2 should introduce the lower limb-predominant, spastic pattern without naming 'spastic diplegia' or 'scissor gait.' Clue 3 should add perinatal injury timing. Clues 4 and 5 are appropriately specific but arrive after the answer is already obvious. Rewrite Clues 1–3 to broaden the early differential (include hypotonic CP, ataxic CP, dystonic CP, metabolic disorders, spinal cord injury) before the examination and imaging narrow it to spastic diplegia specifically.

626. Exercise-Induced Mood Improvement
General
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. The answer—'Exercise-Induced Mood Improvement'—is explicitly stated in Clue 1, making guessing impossible. All subsequent clues merely rephrase the same information (mood improves with exercise) or provide mechanistic explanations (neurotransmitters, fMRI) that don't narrow a differential. A proper puzzle should present a patient WITH mood/anxiety symptoms (Clue 1), then reveal they specifically improve with exercise participation (Clue 2), then provide history showing other interventions didn't work as well (Clue 3), then confirm fitness and mood metrics have genuinely improved (Clue 4), and finally rule out primary psychiatric disorder (Clue 5). The current version is not a diagnostic puzzle—it's a description of exercise physiology.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easynames_answer
ORIGINAL
A patient reports improved mood and mental clarity following regular exercise sessions. Depression and anxiety symptoms improve with consistent physical activity. Psychological well-being is enhanced post-exercise.
UPDATED
Patient reports feeling low in mood and anxious. Describes lack of motivation and mental fatigue impacting daily function.
💡 Patient experiences mood and mental clarity improvement with exercise; depression/anxiety symptoms improve.
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_infonames_answer
ORIGINAL
Mood enhancement occurs during and after moderate to vigorous aerobic exercise. The patient reports feeling energized and psychologically better following workouts. Regular exercise participation is maintained due to positive mood effects.
UPDATED
Patient finds it difficult to engage in work and social activities. Reports feeling most functional and engaged during and immediately after structured physical activity.
💡 Redundant with Clue 1 — both describe mood improvement during/after exercise and positive mood effects.
Clue 3
History
✗ MAJOR
no_new_infowrong_order
ORIGINAL
Exercise tolerance and motivation improve with consistent training. History of depression or anxiety may improve with exercise programming. Neurotransmitter changes facilitate mood regulation through physical activity.
UPDATED
Six-month history of low mood and anxiety. Previously tried antidepressant medications with modest response. Noticed spontaneous improvement when started attending gym 3× weekly. No other recent life changes or medical events.
💡 Mentions neurotransmitter mechanism, but this is physiological explanation, not clinical history. No new information about onset, prior treatments, or timeline.
Clue 4
Examination
✗ MAJOR
no_new_infotoo_generic
ORIGINAL
On assessment, psychological mood scales demonstrate improvement post-exercise. Cardiovascular fitness improvements correlate with mood enhancement. Endorphin release and monoamine neurotransmitter changes are proposed mechanisms.
UPDATED
Cardiovascular fitness testing shows improved VO₂ max and resting heart rate compared to baseline 3 months ago. Mood questionnaire (PHQ-9) score improved from 16 to 8. Patient demonstrates consistent attendance and progressive training intensity.
💡 References mood scales (subjective measure already known from Clue 1) and cardiovascular fitness. Proposes neurotransmitter mechanisms without objective measurement.
Clue 5
Imaging
✗ MAJOR
no_new_infotoo_hard
ORIGINAL
Functional MRI studies demonstrate enhanced activation in reward centers of the brain post-exercise. Endorphin and serotonin levels increase measurably following physical activity. Neuroimaging confirms the biological basis for exercise-induced mood improvement.
UPDATED
Baseline psychiatric evaluation ruled out bipolar disorder and primary mood disorder requiring pharmacotherapy. Sleep study normal. No abnormalities on basic metabolic or thyroid screening. Exercise-based intervention has produced sustained mood improvement without additional medication or psychotherapy changes.
💡 Functional MRI and neurotransmitter levels are mechanistic explanations, not diagnostic findings. This clue explains *why* the condition exists, not what distinguishes it from other causes of mood improvement (e.g., medication, therapy, lifestyle change, social factors).
627. Adhesive Capsulitis
Shoulder
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Critical problem: Clue 1 essentially diagnoses adhesive capsulitis by naming the answer ('profound loss of motion in all planes', 'pain at rest and with activity', combined with middle-aged diabetic woman). Clue 2 then repeats the capsular pattern finding already heavily implied by Clue 1 ('marked restriction in all planes'). Clue 4 again repeats capsular pattern—this information should have been at Clue 2 level (Activity/functional clue). The progressive reveal funnel collapses early. Rewrite Clue 1 to be genuinely broad (shoulder pain, gradual onset, functional impact only—remove the diagnostic language). Rewrite Clue 2 to focus on WHAT the patient cannot do functionally, not exam findings. Move capsular pattern specificity to Clue 4 where it belongs.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easynames_answer

A middle-aged woman with diabetes presents with shoulder pain and profound loss of motion. Shoulder function is markedly restricted in all planes of movement. Pain is present both at rest and with activity, limiting sleep and daily function.
UPDATED
Middle-aged woman with diabetes presenting with shoulder pain. Onset gradual over recent weeks. Pain present at rest and with activity, disrupting sleep and daily routines.
