Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_infowrong_order
ORIGINAL
All shoulder movements demonstrate significant restriction in both active and passive ranges. External rotation is most severely limited followed by abduction and flexion. The restriction occurs in a capsular pattern indicating joint involvement rather than muscular dysfunction.
UPDATED
Unable to reach overhead or behind back. All shoulder movements restricted. Patient reports difficulty with dressing, grooming, and sleeping on affected side.
💡 Specific movement hierarchy (external rotation > abduction > flexion most limited) and capsular pattern descriptor
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Risk factors include middle age, female gender, and poorly controlled diabetes mellitus. The condition often follows minor shoulder trauma or immobilization. Onset is typically gradual with progressive loss of motion over weeks to months.
✓ No changes

Clue 4
Examination
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
On examination, passive motion reveals the characteristic capsular pattern of restriction. External rotation at 0 degrees abduction is severely limited. Scapulohumeral rhythm is disrupted with compensatory scapular elevation during attempted flexion.
UPDATED
Passive external rotation severely limited (near 0 degrees). Scapulohumeral rhythm abnormal with early scapular elevation. Discomfort at end-range in capsular pattern. No weakness detected.
💡 Capsular pattern ROM hierarchy (ER > ABD > FL), scapular compensation pattern, passive ROM findings
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

✓ No changes
💡 Normal plain radiographs, capsular thickening and enhancement on MR arthrography, loss of axillary pouch
628. Overactive Bladder
Pelvis
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Progressive reveal is generally sound: Clue 1 casts wide net on urinary symptoms; Clue 2 adds functional/behavioural context without much new discriminatory value (could be condensed or rewritten to focus more on incontinence pattern); Clue 3 adds critical temporal and red-flag exclusion data; Clue 4 provides physical exam that excludes neurological and structural causes while hinting at neuromuscular dysfunction; Clue 5 confirms absence of organic pathology. Minor issue: Clue 2 largely restates the severity already implied by Clue 1 (12-14 voids daily naturally limits activities). Consider rewriting Clue 2 to emphasize URGENCY INCONTINENCE as a distinct functional consequence, or shift focus to a different functional limitation (e.g., sexual dysfunction, fluid avoidance strategies) that adds genuinely new information. Overall the puzzle is clinically coherent and appropriate for the answer, but could be tightened.
Clue 2
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
The patient has modified her activities extensively to stay near bathroom facilities and has experienced occasional urgency incontinence when unable to reach the toilet immediately. Social activities and travel are limited by her symptoms, and sleep is disrupted by nocturia.
✓ No changes

Activity

no_new_info

UPDATED
Patient has restricted activities to remain near bathrooms and experiences occasional leakage when unable to toilet immediately. Sleep is disrupted by frequent nighttime voids. Social and travel plans are significantly limited.
💡 Functional impact: bathroom-seeking behaviour, urgency incontinence, social/travel limitations, nocturia-induced sleep disruption. Confirms the urgency is severe enough to alter behaviour and affect quality of life.
Clue 4
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

✓ No changes

Clue 5

✓ No changes

Clue 6

✓ OK
ORIGINAL
✓ No changes

Confirms diagnosis is functional bladder overactivity without organic substrate.
629. Patellar Tendinopathy
Knee
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Primary issue: Clue 1 gives away the answer by explicitly naming 'patellar tendon' and describing the classic jumping-athlete presentation. This is too specific for the opening clue and eliminates most of the differential immediately. Clue 2 then largely repeats Clue 1's activity information without adding meaningful narrowing. Clues 3–5 are appropriately structured and progressive, but the first two clues fail the funnel principle. Rewrite Clue 1 to be symptom-only (pain location and activity trigger, but not 'patellar tendon' anatomy), and rewrite Clue 2 to add temporal/post-activity information (e.g., delayed-onset soreness, morning stiffness) that differentiates from other anterior knee pain.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easynames_answer
ORIGINAL
An athlete reports anterior knee pain specifically at the patellar tendon. Pain is worse with jumping and pivoting activities. Functional limitation occurs with sport-specific movements requiring explosive power.
UPDATED
Young athlete reports anterior knee pain that worsens with jumping and cutting activities. Pain is activity-dependent, onset gradual over weeks to months.
💡 Athlete with anterior knee pain localized to patellar tendon; pain with jumping and pivoting; functional limitation in explosive power activities
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info

UPDATED
Pain during landing from jumps and deceleration activities. Single-leg stance and stairs provoke symptoms. Pain often resolves during activity but returns severely after sport, with stiffness the next morning.
💡 Redundant with Clue 1 — rephrases the same activity triggers (jumping, running, direction changes) and pain behavior (worse at onset, improves with warmup, returns post-activity)
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Onset follows increased training volume or intensity, particularly jumping-based sports. Weakness in hip or core muscles may contribute to abnormal loading patterns. Previous patellar or knee pathology may predispose to tendinopathy.
✓ No changes

Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

✓ No changes

Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Ultrasound imaging reveals thickened patellar tendon with mixed echogenicity and increased vascularity on Doppler. MRI demonstrates signal changes within the tendon substance. Imaging findings correlate with symptom duration and severity.
✓ No changes

630. Axillary Nerve Palsy
Shoulder
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has a critical progressive reveal failure in Clues 1–3. Clue 1 essentially names the answer by explicitly stating 'acute-onset shoulder weakness and inability to initiate arm abduction' combined with 'numbness over lateral shoulder'—this is nearly pathognomonic for axillary nerve palsy and leaves almost no differential reasoning for the player. Clues 2 and 3 then add only elaboration and mechanism (both already implied by Clue 1) rather than new discriminating information. By the time the player reaches the examination findings, they have already locked in the diagnosis. The puzzle should cast a much wider net in Clue 1 (e.g., 'shoulder weakness after trauma' without specifying abduction or lateral nerve distribution), allowing Clue 2 to narrow via functional pattern, Clue 3 to refine via timeline/history, and Clue 4 to clinch via specific motor/sensory patterns. Clues 4 and 5 are well-written but are wasted on an already-solved puzzle.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easy
ORIGINAL
A 35-year-old male presents with acute-onset shoulder weakness and inability to initiate arm abduction following a severe fall with direct trauma to the shoulder region. He reports numbness over the lateral shoulder and upper arm.
UPDATED
A 35-year-old male presents with acute-onset weakness and numbness in the shoulder region following a severe fall with direct shoulder impact.
💡 Acute-onset weakness and inability to abduct arm; numbness in lateral shoulder/upper arm distribution following direct shoulder trauma
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
The patient cannot perform overhead activities or lift the arm away from the body due to profound weakness. He is unable to do any pushing or reaching movements requiring shoulder abduction. Pain is primarily from the associated shoulder injury rather than nerve pain.
UPDATED
Patient cannot initiate any movements requiring shoulder abduction. Passive range of motion is preserved. Weakness is the primary functional limitation rather than pain.
💡 Functional limitation: cannot abduct arm or perform overhead activities; weakness is profound but pain is secondary
Clue 3
History
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
The injury occurred when the patient fell directly onto his shoulder from a standing height and struck a hard surface. No prior neurological symptoms or shoulder pathology existed. The patient seeks immediate evaluation within 48 hours of the injury.
UPDATED
Direct trauma from fall onto shoulder from standing height. No prior neurological symptoms, shoulder pathology, or recurrent dislocations. First neurological symptoms noted immediately post-injury.
💡 Direct impact mechanism from fall; no prior shoulder pathology; acute presentation within 48 hours
Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
On examination, deltoid muscle is atrophic and significantly weakened with shoulder abduction strength 2/5. Sensation is diminished over the lateral shoulder in the regimental badge distribution. Axillary nerve traction test is positive. Rotator cuff strength is intact.
✓ No changes
💡 Deltoid atrophy and profound weakness (2/5); sensory loss in regimental badge pattern; positive axillary nerve traction test; intact rotator cuff—these findings specifically implicate axillary nerve
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Shoulder X-rays rule out fracture or dislocation. MRI shows no rotator cuff tear but reveals signal changes in the axillary nerve course with surrounding soft tissue edema. Electromyography demonstrates denervation potentials in deltoid and axillary nerve conduction slowing.
✓ No changes
💡 Imaging rules out structural fracture/dislocation; MRI shows nerve-specific signal changes and edema; EMG confirms denervation and axillary nerve conduction slowing—definitively confirms axillary nerve injury with denervation
631. Post-Total Shoulder Replacement
Shoulder
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 essentially names the diagnosis ('total shoulder arthroplasty' in early post-operative period). Clues 2 and 4 repeat the same observations (pain, ROM limitation, protective positioning) without adding discriminatory information. After Clue 1, a student knows the answer is a post-operative TSR condition — the remaining clues are just elaboration, not narrowing. The differential should not collapse to one answer until Clue 4 or 5. To fix this: (1) Make Clue 1 describe only pain and restriction WITHOUT mentioning surgery. (2) Clue 2 should hint at functional pattern that suggests recent major surgery (not just any shoulder problem). (3) Clue 3 should explicitly state 'post-surgical' in timeline. (4) Clue 4 should add examination specifics that rule out other post-op complications (e.g., infection, nerve injury, instability). (5) Clue 5 should confirm prosthetic positioning. Currently, the game is too easy after Clue 1.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
names_answertoo_easy
ORIGINAL
A patient in early post-operative period following total shoulder arthroplasty reports pain at surgical site. Shoulder motion is limited by protective guarding and pain. Functional use of the arm is significantly restricted immediately post-surgery.
UPDATED
A patient reports shoulder pain and difficulty moving the arm. Pain is worse with attempted active motion. Onset was acute, following a surgical procedure several weeks ago.
💡 Post-operative shoulder pain and restricted motion in early period
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Pain and stiffness limit active range of motion in all planes. Passive motion is protected in early stages to allow tissue healing. Immobilization is maintained according to surgical protocol.
UPDATED
Patient cannot perform overhead activities or reaching tasks. Cannot lift arm away from body or sleep on affected side. Requires assistance with dressing and grooming. Functional limitation is global rather than movement-specific.
💡 Redundant with Clue 1 — both describe limited ROM and protective guarding. No new functional discrimination.
Clue 3
History
✗ MAJOR
names_answer
ORIGINAL
Total shoulder replacement was performed for severe osteoarthritis or rotator cuff damage. Pre-operative shoulder function was markedly limited by arthropathy. Post-operative rehabilitation follows a predictable timeline with progressive loading.
UPDATED
Patient underwent major shoulder surgery approximately 6 weeks ago for chronic degenerative joint disease. Pre-operative ROM was severely restricted. Recovery has been slower than anticipated; passive stretching causes apprehension.
💡 Reveals surgical history (TSR for OA/RCD) and pre-operative status. This is valuable — it establishes the procedure occurred.
Clue 4
Examination
✗ MAJOR
too_genericno_new_info
ORIGINAL
On examination, pain is elicited with passive motion beyond protected range. Incision appears well-healed without signs of infection. Arm position is supported in protective sling during early post-operative phase.
UPDATED
Passive external rotation is limited to 20°; forward flexion 60° with pain. Apprehension noted at end-range passive motion. No strength testing performed. Rotator cuff integrity not formally assessed. Shoulder girdle musculature appears atrophic.
💡 Confirms incision healing, protective positioning, pain on passive motion. Largely predictable for any post-op shoulder patient — no discriminatory value.
Clue 5
Imaging
⚠ MINOR
no_new_infotoo_generic
ORIGINAL
Post-operative radiographs confirm prosthetic component positioning and alignment. Serial imaging monitors for component loosening or complications. CT may be used if complications are suspected.
UPDATED
Post-operative X-rays show secure component fixation with no signs of loosening or subsidence. No heterotopic ossification noted. Soft tissue shadow consistent with post-operative edema.
💡 Confirms prosthetic position and alignment. Expected finding for TSR — provides little new diagnostic information beyond what Clue 2 already established.

Clue 0 should be more generic (pelvic heaviness/pressure/urinary symptoms only, no bulging), and Clue 1 should introduce the postural/activity pattern as NEW information. Clue 4 is the key discriminator (anterior wall bulging on Valsalva) and appropriately positions cystocele vs. rectocele/uterine prolapse. Clue 5 adds confirmation but is somewhat redundant with Clue 4's clinical findings; suggested rewrite specifies compartment involvement to add distinct value.

634. Diastasis Rectus Abdominis

Clue 2 adds good functional specificity but feels somewhat redundant after Clue 1 already revealed the anatomy. The rewrite for Clue 1 removes the anatomical diagnosis; the rewrite for Clue 2 emphasizes functional limitation patterns and response to intervention, adding new discriminatory information. Clues 3, 4, and 5 are well-structured and appropriately specific for their positions.

635. Pectoralis Major Rupture
Chest
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major problem: Clue 1 is far too specific and essentially names the answer (explicitly states 'acute sharp pain in anterior chest,' 'sudden loss of function,' AND 'chest deformity with loss of pectoral contour' AND strength loss in two specific directions). A physio student reads this and already knows it's pectoralis major rupture — there is no differential left. Clue 2 then merely restates the same findings without adding new information. Clue 3 is strong and adds useful historical context. Clues 4 and 5 are redundant with Clues 1 and 2 because the deformity, weakness, and muscle loss were already stated upfront. The progressive reveal principle is violated: clues should start broad and get progressively narrower, not start by naming the diagnosis and then repeat details. Rewrite Clues 1 and 2 to establish only the complaint and functional impact without the diagnostic giveaway.
Clue 1
Complaint

too_easy

💡 Redundant with Clue 1 — horizontal adduction limitation and loss of arm pushing activities are already stated. Loss of anterior chest bulk rephrases 'visible deformity' from Clue 1.
Clue 3

✓ OK

💡 Palpable gap in pectoral region due to muscle retraction, visible asymmetry, severe weakness on strength testing — these are new objective findings that confirm what Clue 1 described visually but add clinical examination detail.
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK

636. Meniscal Tear
Knee
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major progressive reveal problem: Clues 1 and 2 are far too specific and essentially name the diagnosis. Clue 1 explicitly states 'catching and locking' and 'joint line pain' — classic meniscal tear language. Clue 2 then repeats the same mechanical findings ('catching sensations', 'locking') and adds rotational movements — but this is elaborating on already-given information rather than narrowing a differential. By Clue 3, a player has already essentially solved the puzzle. The funnel should broaden from general knee pain → specific region/movement pattern → history context → examination discrimination → imaging confirmation. Clue 4 naming McMurray and Thessaly tests is premature — these tests are pathognomonic for meniscal tears and shouldn't appear until after Clue 3. Rewrite to make Clues 1–2 genuinely broad (could be ACL, MCL, osteoarthritis, etc.) and shift mechanical findings to Clues 3–4.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easy
ORIGINAL
A patient reports mechanical symptoms in the knee including catching and locking. Pain is localized to the joint line. Swelling develops in the knee, worse with prolonged standing or walking.
UPDATED
Patient reports knee pain with intermittent mechanical symptoms. Pain is localized around the knee joint. Swelling develops gradually, particularly worse after prolonged weight-bearing activities.
💡 Mechanical symptoms (catching, locking), joint line pain, swelling worse with prolonged activity
Clue 2
Activity

no_new_info

Twisting movements and cutting activities trigger catching sensations. Mechanical locking may occur with weight bearing requiring manipulation to unlock. Squatting and kneeling activities are particularly provocative.
UPDATED
Patient unable to perform rotational movements of the knee under load. Squatting and deep flexion with rotation are significantly limited. Occasional episodes of knee 'giving way' or sudden inability to extend fully.
💡 Specific movement patterns that provoke symptoms: twisting, cutting, squatting, kneeling; mechanical locking with weight bearing
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Tear may follow a specific injury with twisting mechanism or develop insidiously in degenerative knees. Previous meniscal pathology or knee injuries increase risk. Degenerative tears are common in middle-aged and older patients.
✓ No changes

Clue 4
Examination
✗ MAJOR
too_easy

McMurray test and Thessaly test reproduce the mechanical symptoms. Joint line tenderness is localized to the meniscal location. Effusion may be present with ballottement demonstrating fluid in the joint.
UPDATED
Joint line palpation reproduces localized tenderness. Knee effusion present. Range of motion limited in flexion, particularly with rotation. Special tests for meniscal irritation are positive.
💡 McMurray test, Thessaly test, joint line tenderness, effusion with ballottement — all highly specific findings for meniscal pathology
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
MRI of the knee demonstrates meniscal tear in the medial or lateral meniscus with classification of tear pattern. T2 signal intensity within the meniscal substance indicates tear. Imaging localizes the tear and guides treatment decisions.

637. Hearing Loss
Ear
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major issue: The puzzle conflates sensorineural hearing loss (age-related presbycusis + noise exposure) with a mixed conductive component (air-bone gaps, cerumen impaction). This is internally inconsistent and confuses the differential. More critically, Clues 1-2 already establish bilateral symmetric high-frequency hearing loss with functional impact; Clues 3-5 merely elaborate without genuinely narrowing diagnosis. After Clue 1 alone, a clinician has already generated presbycusis, noise-induced hearing loss, and mixed loss as top differentials. The subsequent clues should eliminate confounders (cerumen impaction, ototoxic meds, trauma, conductive pathology) and confirm the pattern, but they repeat the same information. Clue 4 mentions cerumen impaction as a 'new' finding, but this should have been in the History or as an Examination red herring that gets ruled out. The sequence lacks meaningful narrowing. Rewritten clues should: (1) remove redundant symptom descriptions; (2) use Examination to explicitly rule in/out conductive causes; (3) use Imaging to confirm the audiometric pattern and eliminate other sensorineural differentials (e.g., retrocochlear, sudden SNHL, Ménière's). The answer 'Hearing Loss' is also too generic—presbycusis or 'mixed hearing loss (age and noise-induced)' would be more clinically precise.

639. Sarcopenia

NEEDS WORK

Clue 4 should focus on examination findings NOT already captured in Clues 1–3: palpable muscle bulk, specific distribution of wasting, neurological screen, joint involvement, balance/proprioception findings. This would meaningfully differentiate sarcopenia from other causes of weakness (e.g., neuropathy, myositis, proximal myopathy, motor neurone disease). As written, Clue 3 already narrows strongly to sarcopenia (elderly, sedentary, inadequate protein, comorbidities); Clue 4 should add discriminatory clinical examination data, not repeat functional limitations. 643. Atlantoaxial Instability

A player reading Clue 1 alone knows the answer is some form of cervical spine instability; by Clue 3 they know it's specifically atlantoaxial instability in a Down syndrome patient. Clues 2 and 3 add almost no new diagnostic information beyond what Clue 1 already revealed. The puzzle violates the fundamental 'funnel' principle—it front-loads diagnosis-specific anatomy and pathophysiology rather than building toward it. 💡 MRI confirms intramuscular hematoma with specific muscle involvement (vastus lateralis and biceps femoris), quantifies hematoma size (6x8 cm), and rules out fracture and compartment syndrome. This is appropriately specific for final confirmation.
646. Post-Total Hip Replacement
Hip
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 explicitly names 'total hip arthroplasty' and describes the immediate post-operative context — this is essentially giving away the answer before the player guesses. Clues 2 and 3 then repeat and elaborate on the same post-operative limitations and surgical context without adding meaningfully new discriminatory information. By Clue 2, a player already knows this is post-operative hip surgery with specific precautions; Clues 3–5 only reinforce and detail what was already established. For a valid Physiodle puzzle, Clue 1 should describe only acute hip pain and mobility loss without mentioning surgery; Clues 2–3 should gradually reveal the post-operative context; Clues 4–5 should confirm prosthetic replacement via imaging and specific examination findings. The current set is too easy and lacks a genuine clinical reasoning funnel.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
names_answertoo_easy
ORIGINAL
A patient in the immediate post-operative period following total hip arthroplasty reports pain at the surgical site. Hip motion is restricted by protective positioning and precautions. Functional mobility is severely limited in early post-operative phase.
UPDATED
A 68-year-old patient presents with severe hip pain and inability to walk independently. Onset was acute following a surgical procedure performed two weeks ago. Pain is worsened by movement and the patient requires assistive devices for mobility.
💡 Post-operative pain and restricted hip motion in immediate post-operative period following total hip replacement
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Weight bearing is progressed according to surgeon protocol with crutches or walker. Hip flexion is restricted to prevent prosthetic dislocation. Abduction pillows or positioning devices maintain protective alignment.
UPDATED
Patient cannot tolerate hip flexion beyond 60–80 degrees and actively avoids adduction across the body. Attempts to cross legs or sit in low chairs trigger sharp pain and muscle guarding. Lying supine is poorly tolerated; side-lying is only comfortable on the operated side with a pillow between knees.
💡 Redundant with Clue 1 — both describe restricted hip flexion, protective positioning, and severe mobility limitations. Clue 2 adds only surgical protocol details (weight bearing progression, abduction pillows) but doesn't meaningfully narrow the differential beyond what Clue 1 already established.
Clue 3
History
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Total hip replacement was performed for advanced osteoarthritis or femoral neck fracture. Pre-operative hip pain and dysfunction significantly impaired mobility. Post-operative rehabilitation involves progressive weight bearing and motion over weeks to months.
UPDATED
Patient underwent surgery 14 days ago. Surgeon issued strict precautions: no hip flexion beyond 90 degrees, no adduction past midline, no internal rotation. Patient is currently weight-bearing as tolerated on both legs. No complications reported (no excessive swelling, no calf pain, no fever). Physiotherapy commenced 48 hours post-operatively.
💡 Diagnosis for which surgery was performed (osteoarthritis or femoral neck fracture) and timeline of pre-operative dysfunction. However, this information is largely predictable from Clues 1 and 2 (post-operative context) and does not significantly narrow the differential — it simply explains the reason for surgery, not the current post-operative state.
Clue 4
Examination
✗ MAJOR
too_genericno_new_info
ORIGINAL
On examination, surgical site shows appropriate wound healing without excessive drainage. Hip motion is limited per post-operative precautions. Muscle guarding is present with pain on end-range motion testing.
UPDATED
Hip abduction and external rotation are preserved; flexion and adduction are markedly limited and guarded. Palpation elicits tenderness over anterior hip and proximal thigh. Trendelenburg sign is present. No signs of infection (temperature normal, wound well-approximated). No evidence of hip instability or apprehension on gentle passive motion testing within precaution limits.
💡 Wound healing status, muscle guarding, and pain on end-range motion. These are generic post-operative findings that could apply to any recent surgical procedure and don't differentiate post-THR from other post-operative hip or lower-limb surgeries.
Clue 5
Imaging
⚠ MINOR
too_generic
ORIGINAL

UPDATED
Anterior-posterior and lateral radiographs show femoral and acetabular components in neutral alignment with no evidence of malpositioning. Acetabular anteversion is 15–20 degrees. Femoral stem is well-fixed with no early subsidence. Hip-to-knee distance and soft tissue contours are symmetric. No periprosthetic radiolucencies or osteolytic lesions.
💡 Post-operative radiographs confirm prosthetic component positioning and alignment. This is highly specific to hip replacement but somewhat generic in confirming 'appropriate' positioning — doesn't describe findings that would distinguish this from a successful THR vs. a complicated THR.

A functional puzzle would describe a post-surgical shoulder patient without naming the surgery, then progressively narrow via functional loss, imaging confirmation of a repaired tendon, and specific ROM/strength deficits. 648. Compartment Syndrome
Limbs
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The clues have a fundamental problem with progressive reveal. Clue 1 explicitly names 'pain out of proportion to clinical findings' — a classic red flag for compartment syndrome that immediately narrows the differential dramatically. Clues 2, 3, and 4 then simply elaborate on features already implied (progressive worsening, crush injury, compartment findings, neurological signs). By Clue 2, an experienced clinician would already be highly suspicious of compartment syndrome. Clues 3 and 4 add objective confirmatory details but don't meaningfully eliminate competing diagnoses. The puzzle becomes too easy too quickly. To improve: (1) Clue 1 should be much broader — just severe leg pain after crush, without the 'disproportionate' language. (2) Clue 2 should reveal the passive stretch pain and resistance to analgesics as the key functional red flag. (3) Clue 3 should establish timeline and swelling progression. (4) Clue 4 should introduce the neurological signs and compartment tenseness that differentiate from simple contusion/hematoma. (5) Clue 5 remains definitive with pressure measurement. This would create a proper funnel from broad (pain after crush) to diagnosis-specific (compartment pressure).
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easy
ORIGINAL
A 28-year-old athlete presents with severe leg pain disproportionate to clinical findings following an acute crush injury to the anterior lower leg during a contact sport. Pain is out of proportion to the apparent soft tissue injury.
UPDATED
A 28-year-old athlete presents with severe leg pain following a crush injury to the lower leg during contact sport. Pain severity seems excessive relative to the visible soft tissue damage.
💡 28-year-old athlete with severe leg pain out of proportion to apparent injury following crush trauma to anterior lower leg
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
too_easy
ORIGINAL
The patient reports progressive pain with passive stretching of the foot, particularly dorsiflexion and plantarflexion, which produces severe discomfort. Active foot movements are increasingly limited. The pain is not controlled by standard analgesics and worsens over hours.
UPDATED
Pain dramatically worsens with passive stretching of the ankle and foot. Active ankle movements are increasingly difficult. Pain continues to escalate despite standard pain relief measures.
💡 Pain with passive ankle movements (dorsiflexion/plantarflexion), progressive worsening, resistant to analgesics, active ROM limited
Clue 3
History
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Injury occurred 4-6 hours prior to presentation from direct blunt trauma during athletic competition. No fracture was evident on initial clinical assessment. The patient reported swelling beginning soon after injury and steadily worsening.
UPDATED
Injury occurred 4-6 hours ago from direct blunt crush during sport. Visible swelling began immediately and has worsened steadily. No fracture was identified on initial assessment. Patient reports the pain trajectory is unusually rapid for a simple contusion.
💡 Injury 4-6 hours prior, direct blunt trauma, no fracture on initial exam, rapid onset swelling that is progressive
Clue 4
Examination
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
On examination, the anterior leg demonstrates marked swelling and tenseness of the compartment. Passive ankle plantarflexion produces severe pain. Foot sensation is diminished in a stocking distribution. Peripheral pulses remain palpable, but pale appearance is noted.
UPDATED
Anterior leg is visibly swollen and firm/tense. Passive ankle plantarflexion elicits severe pain. Foot sensation is diminished in a stocking-like pattern. Pulses are palpable bilaterally, but the skin appears pale. No obvious fracture deformity.
💡 Marked swelling and compartment tenseness, passive plantarflexion pain, diminished sensation (stocking distribution), preserved pulses but pale appearance
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Compartment pressure measurement via needle manometer shows anterior compartment pressure of 65 mmHg at rest, exceeding the critical threshold of 60 mmHg. CT imaging of the leg demonstrates swollen muscles with no fracture. MRI shows edema within the anterior compartment muscles.
✓ No changes
💡 Compartment pressure measurement shows 65 mmHg (exceeds 60 mmHg threshold). CT and MRI show swollen muscles, no fracture, edema in anterior compartment
649. Meniscal Tear
Knee
NEEDS WORK

Clue 2 simply elaborates the exact same mechanical symptoms from Clue 1 (twisting, cutting, squatting, catching, locking) without adding fundamentally new discriminatory information. A player reading Clues 1+2 already knows the answer is almost certainly meniscus. Clue 4 also overlaps with earlier clues by re-confirming the same mechanical findings. The funnel collapses too early. Rewrites shift Clue 1 to vaguer mechanical instability language, Clue 2 to functional movement limitation that doesn't repeat the catch/lock terminology, and Clue 4 to specific test findings that distinguish meniscal tears from other knee pathology (e.g., ligamentous vs meniscal).

651. Radial Tunnel Syndrome
Elbow
POOR

The clues lack a proper clinical funnel — they should start broad (forearm pain, weakness), narrow to region and nerve (proximal forearm, posterior interosseous nerve involvement), then confirm via examination and imaging. Clues 1–2 are also partially redundant, both focusing on grip/fine motor and aggravating factors. Reorder and rewrite clues 1–3 to preserve diagnostic mystery while maintaining clinical coherence.

Neck

Congenital torticollis results from shortening or fibrosis of the sternocleidomastoid muscle. Intrauterine positioning or birth trauma may precipitate muscle injury. The condition is present from birth or develops in early infancy.
UPDATED
Birth history unremarkable or notable for difficult/instrumented delivery or breech presentation. Condition present from birth or noticed within first 2–3 months of life. No recent trauma. Family history not contributory.
💡 Mechanism (intrauterine positioning or birth trauma); timeline (from birth or early infancy); underlying pathology (SCM shortening/fibrosis)
Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
On examination, a tight mass or rope-like sternocleidomastoid muscle is palpable on the affected side. Head tilt toward the affected side with chin rotation away is evident. Facial asymmetry may be present with flattening on the affected side.
✓ No changes
💡 Palpable muscle mass; specific head/chin position pattern; objective asymmetry signs
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Ultrasound of the sternocleidomastoid muscle demonstrates focal enlargement and echogenic appearance consistent with fibrosis. MRI may show muscle pathology and structural changes. Imaging rules out spinal or skeletal abnormalities.
✓ No changes
💡 Ultrasound findings (enlargement, echogenicity/fibrosis); MRI structural detail; exclusion of skeletal/spinal pathology
655. Olecranon Fracture
Elbow
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Clue 1 is too specific — it names 'posterior elbow pain' and immediately states loss of active extension, which is almost pathognomonic for olecranon fracture. A good Clue 1 should avoid 'posterior elbow' language and just say 'elbow pain' broadly. Clue 2 repeats the same functional limitation (cannot extend) without adding new discriminatory information. The progressive reveal stalls between Clues 1–2. After Clue 1 alone, a clinician is already thinking olecranon fracture; after Clue 2, they've learned almost nothing new. Clues 3–5 are appropriately structured and add genuine specificity, but the early funnel is too narrow.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easy
ORIGINAL
A patient reports acute posterior elbow pain and swelling following direct trauma. The elbow cannot be extended actively. Severe pain limits any arm movement.
UPDATED
A patient reports acute onset severe elbow pain and rapid swelling following a direct blow or fall. The arm is held protectively in a flexed position and any attempt to straighten it causes intense pain.
💡 Acute posterior elbow pain, swelling, loss of active extension following direct trauma
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Active elbow extension is severely limited or impossible. Passive extension may be restricted by swelling and severe pain. Arm positioning is guarded with elbow flexed for comfort.
UPDATED
Patient cannot perform any functional activities requiring the arm — cannot push off a chair, cannot use the arm for self-care, cannot support weight through the affected arm.
💡 Redundant with Clue 1 — both describe inability to actively extend elbow and guarded arm positioning
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Fracture typically follows direct blow to the olecranon or fall on flexed elbow. High energy trauma results in displaced fractures. Low energy injuries may result in undisplaced fractures.
✓ No changes
💡 Mechanism of injury (direct blow vs fall), energy level (high vs low), relationship to fracture displacement pattern
Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
On examination, significant swelling and ecchymosis are present over the olecranon. A palpable step-off may be felt at the fracture site. Crepitus may be elicited with movement of fracture fragments.
✓ No changes
💡 Palpable deformity (step-off), crepitus from fragment movement — specific physical signs that indicate fracture
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Radiographs demonstrate olecranon fracture with classification of displacement and comminution. Fracture line is visible with relationship to the joint surface assessed. Imaging guides treatment decisions regarding operative intervention.
✓ No changes
💡 Confirms fracture, assesses displacement/comminution, guides treatment planning
656. Vestibular Neuritis
Vestibular
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Clue 3 is the main problem: it largely reiterates information from Clues 1 and 2 (viral prodrome, acute onset, preserved hearing) without adding substantive new discriminatory history. A strong History clue should introduce timeline nuances, comorbidities, medication use, or prior episodes that further narrow the differential (e.g., distinguish from Menière disease, migraine-associated dizziness, or recurrent vestibular events). Clue 4 is strong and appropriately positions the puzzle near diagnosis. Clue 5 is adequate but could be more specific about what structures the MRI examined. Overall progression is reasonable but Clue 3 weakens the funnel effect.
Clue 1
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
A 42-year-old otherwise healthy adult presents with sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking this morning. No hearing loss reported. Recent upper respiratory viral illness 5 days prior to symptom onset.
✓ No changes
💡 Sudden onset severe vertigo, recent viral illness, no hearing loss. Establishes acute presentation and potential viral trigger.
Clue 2
Activity
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Vertigo is constant and disabling, with dizziness exacerbated by head movements and eye closure. Patient experiences significant nausea with vomiting. Symptoms slightly improve with bed rest and head immobilization.
✓ No changes
💡 Constant disabling vertigo, exacerbated by head movement/eye closure, nausea/vomiting, improves with immobilization. Establishes severity and movement-related exacerbation pattern.
Clue 3
History
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Acute onset following viral prodrome typical for vestibular neuritis. No prior episodes of vertigo. Hearing preserved, ruling out audiovestibular involvement. No recent head trauma or otologic surgery.
UPDATED
No trauma, otalgia, or recent air travel. Patient reports mild imbalance persisting even at rest, but no positional component and no fluctuating symptoms suggestive of Menière disease.
💡 Redundant with Clues 1 and 2. Restates 'acute onset following viral prodrome', 'no prior episodes', 'hearing preserved'. Does not add meaningful new history (no trauma, no surgery) — these are exclusions already implied by the clinical picture, not new discriminatory information.
Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Head impulse test shows positive catch-up saccade indicating unilateral vestibular deficit. Dix-Hallpike maneuver negative excluding BPPV. Caloric testing shows reduced response in affected ear (50% unilateral paresis). Romberg test positive with falls toward affected side.
✓ No changes
💡 Head impulse test positive (unilateral deficit), Dix-Hallpike negative, caloric testing with 50% unilateral paresis, Romberg positive with fall toward affected side. These are specific, discriminatory findings that differentiate vestibular neuritis from BPPV and central causes.
Clue 5
Imaging
⚠ MINOR
too_generic
ORIGINAL
MRI inner ear structures normal excluding neuroma or structural lesion. No enhancement of CN VII or VIII. Audiometry normal. Electrography confirms vestibular asymmetry consistent with peripheral vestibulitis.
UPDATED
MRI brain and internal auditory canal normal with no T2 signal abnormality or enhancement of vestibulocochlear nerve. Excludes demyelinating disease and structural lesions. Findings consistent with peripheral vestibular origin.
💡 MRI normal (excludes neuroma/lesion), no CN enhancement, audiometry normal, electrography confirms asymmetry. Confirms peripheral origin and rules out structural/neoplastic mimics.
657. Post-Spinal Fusion
Spine
POOR
Review Notes: CRITICAL ISSUES: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 literally states 'following spinal fusion', immediately naming the answer. Clues 2, 3, and 4 all rephrase the same post-operative recovery picture without adding meaningfully new information—they are essentially elaborations of what is already clear from Clue 1. The differential narrows to zero after Clue 1, making guesses 2-5 pointless. The puzzle is structured as a description of post-operative spinal fusion recovery rather than a diagnostic challenge. To fix this: (1) Clue 1 must describe ONLY the complaint without mentioning surgery or spinal fusion; (2) Clues 2-5 must introduce distinct clinical information (functional pattern, timeline/mechanism, examination findings, imaging) that gradually reveals the post-operative nature and specific diagnosis. The rewritten clues above attempt to preserve the clinical reality while creating actual diagnostic discrimination.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
names_answertoo_easywrong_order
ORIGINAL
A patient in the post-operative period following spinal fusion reports back or neck pain at surgical site. Spinal mobility is restricted per post-operative precautions. Neurological function is monitored for improvement or complications.
UPDATED
Patient reports persistent back or neck pain with severe restriction in spinal movement. Onset was acute, following a recent surgical procedure. Neurological symptoms (if present pre-operatively) are being monitored for change.
💡 Patient is post-operative following spinal fusion; reports pain at surgical site; spinal mobility is restricted; neurological function is being monitored.
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_inforedundant with clue 1
ORIGINAL
Bending and twisting movements are restricted per surgical protocol to protect fusion site. Weight bearing progression occurs gradually depending on construct. Bed mobility and transfer training are critical early post-operative activities.
UPDATED
Patient cannot independently perform activities involving spinal flexion, extension, or rotation. Sitting tolerance is limited to short periods. Transfers from bed and ambulation require assistance or close supervision due to instability concerns.
💡 Specific functional limitations: cannot bend or twist; weight-bearing is progressing gradually; mobility and transfers require training.
Clue 3
History
✗ MAJOR
no_new_infotoo_generic
ORIGINAL
Spinal fusion was performed for stenosis, spondylolisthesis, or instability. Pre-operative symptoms included pain and/or neurological deficit. Post-operative recovery involves bone fusion at surgical levels over 3-6 months.
UPDATED
Spinal fusion was performed 2-4 weeks ago for a degenerative or structural pathology. Pre-operative symptoms included radiculopathy or claudication-type pain. Patient was immobilized post-operatively with strict motion precautions. No prior spinal surgery.
💡 Spinal fusion was performed (for stenosis, spondylolisthesis, or instability); pre-operative presentation included pain and/or neurological deficit; fusion timeline is 3-6 months.
Clue 4
Examination
✗ MAJOR
no_new_infotoo_generic
ORIGINAL
On examination, post-operative incision appears well-healed without infection signs. Spinal motion is restricted per precautions. Neurological examination monitors for nerve root recovery.
UPDATED
Surgical incision is clean, dry, and well-approximated. Spinal ROM is severely limited in all planes (<10° in most directions). Neurological examination shows persistent lower extremity weakness or sensory changes consistent with nerve root distribution; deep tendon reflexes may be hypoactive.
💡 Incision is well-healed without infection; spinal motion is restricted per precautions; neurological exam shows monitoring for nerve recovery.
Clue 5
Imaging
✗ MAJOR
no_new_infotoo_generic
ORIGINAL
Post-operative X-rays confirm instrumentation position and alignment. Serial imaging monitors for fusion progression and hardware integrity. CT or MRI assesses neural element decompression and hardware position.
UPDATED
Post-operative X-rays show appropriate instrumentation alignment with no obvious hardware failure or migration. Early signs of callus formation are visible at fusion levels. MRI (if performed) confirms adequate neural decompression and absence of epidural hematoma or infection.
💡 X-rays confirm instrumentation position and alignment; serial imaging monitors fusion progression and hardware integrity; CT or MRI assesses decompression and hardware position.
658. Parkinson's Disease
Brain
POOR
Review Notes: Major structural problems: Clue 1 is far too revealing—it explicitly names cardinal features of Parkinson's disease (progressive tremor, stiffness, slowness, postural instability, gait/fine motor decline) and even mentions the pathophysiology by name in Clue 3. A physio student reading Clue 1 would immediately think 'Parkinson's' rather than generating a broad differential. Clue 2 adds almost nothing new—it just elaborates on tremor behavior and stiffness already mentioned in Clue 1. Clue 3 commits the cardinal sin of revealing the neuroanatomical pathology (substantia nigra dopaminergic loss), which should never appear in a complaint/history clue. The progressive reveal is broken: Clues 1–3 essentially converge on a single diagnosis, leaving Clues 4–5 as mere confirmation rather than genuine differentiation. To fix this, Clue 1 must be stripped to simple symptoms without functional specificity or pathology language. Clue 2 should establish functional impact (what can't they do?). Clue 3 should add temporal/epidemiological context without naming pathology. Clues 4–5 should then narrow from a realistic differential (essential tremor, atypical parkinsonian syndromes, medication-induced movement disorder, cerebellar ataxia) down to confirmed Parkinson's disease.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easynames_answer
ORIGINAL
A patient reports progressive tremor, stiffness, and slowness of movement. Motor function gradually declines affecting gait and fine motor control. Postural instability develops with disease progression increasing fall risk.
UPDATED
Older adult reports gradual onset of involuntary shaking at rest and difficulty with fine motor tasks. Movement feels slower and stiffer overall. Recent falls despite no acute injury.
💡 Progressive motor decline with tremor, stiffness, slowness, postural instability, and fall risk.
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Movement is slow with reduced amplitude (bradykinesia). Tremor is worse at rest and improves with purposeful movement. Stiffness limits range of motion and comfortable positioning.
UPDATED
Cannot button clothing or use cutlery with usual dexterity. Walking requires concentration and has become slow and shuffling. Difficulty rising from chairs and turning in bed.
💡 Redundant with Clue 1 — bradykinesia, rest tremor behaviour, and stiffness limiting ROM already stated. No new functional impact beyond what Complaint established.
Clue 3
History
✗ MAJOR
names_answerwrong_order
ORIGINAL
Parkinson's disease typically develops in older adults with gradual progressive course. Family history may be positive. Dopaminergic neuron loss in substantia nigra is the pathological basis.
UPDATED
Symptoms began gradually 18 months ago. No acute injury or recent medication change. One relative diagnosed with similar movement disorder in their 70s. Symptoms have progressively worsened without remission.
💡 Age group (older adults), gradual progressive course, family history possibility, and explicit pathological mechanism (dopaminergic neuron loss in substantia nigra).
Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
On examination, resting tremor is evident at 4-6 Hz frequency. Rigidity is present with cogwheel quality on passive motion testing. Gait demonstrates shuffling pattern with reduced arm swing.
✓ No changes
💡 Specific objective findings: resting tremor frequency (4-6 Hz), cogwheel rigidity on passive testing, shuffling gait with reduced arm swing.
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
MRI of the brain may show midbrain atrophy. PET imaging demonstrates reduced dopamine uptake in striatum confirming diagnosis. DaTscan shows reduced dopaminergic activity in the putamen.
✓ No changes
💡 Neuroimaging findings (midbrain atrophy, reduced dopamine uptake on PET, DaTscan positivity) confirm the diagnosis.
659. Growing Pains
Lower Limb
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle struggles with redundancy between Clues 1 and 2. Both emphasize evening/night timing and symptom resolution, which severely blunts the progressive reveal. Clue 2 should add genuinely new functional or contextual detail (e.g., specific functional limitations, response to typical pain relievers, relationship to activity level during the day) rather than restating timing. The phrase 'activity-related pain may occur' in Clue 2 also muddies the picture and contradicts the rest-pain pattern, which should be clarified. Clues 3, 4, and 5 are reasonably ordered and add distinct information. Overall, the puzzle is strong on clinical accuracy but weak on game design due to clue overlap.
Clue 1
Complaint

too_easy

UPDATED
A school-age child reports intermittent pain in the lower legs. Pain typically emerges in the evening or night and resolves by morning. No history of injury or specific trauma.

Clue 2

💡 Redundant with Clue 1 — timing (evening/night, resolution by morning) already stated; adds only sleep disturbance and 'activity-related pain may occur' (which contradicts the night-pain-at-rest pattern and muddies the differential)
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Growing pains occur during growth spurts in children typically between ages 3-10. Symptoms are benign and self-limited. Family history of growing pains is common.
✓ No changes
💡 Age range refined (3–10 years), benign/self-limited character, family history association. These add meaningful narrowing: benign trajectory and genetic predisposition help rule out fracture, infection, malignancy, and structural pathology.
Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
On examination, no objective findings are present. Lower extremity range of motion is normal. Gait is normal during examination with no limp observed.

Clue 5

⚠ MINOR
too_generic

UPDATED

💡 Imaging not needed; normal if done. Confirms no fracture, bone lesion, or structural pathology. Reinforces clinical diagnosis.

Face
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Main problem: Clue 2 (Activity) is almost entirely redundant with Clue 1 (Complaint). Both describe the same trigger pattern (touch, chewing, talking) and pain onset. Clue 4 (Examination) also has some redundancy—reproducing pain by light touch is already stated in Clue 1. Rewrite Clues 2 and 4 to emphasize NEW clinical findings: Clue 2 should focus on actual functional losses/adaptations (avoidance behaviours, quality of life), and Clue 4 should highlight normal sensory exam (ruling out peripheral neuropathy/structural nerve injury) plus specific anatomical distribution of reproducible trigger points. This will tighten the funnel from a broad 'facial pain with triggers' (Clue 1) → significant quality-of-life impact (Clue 2) → age/MS risk/mechanism (Clue 3) → normal neuro exam but exquisite trigger points (Clue 4) → imaging confirmation (Clue 5).

Clue 2

Clue 3

661. Little League Elbow
Elbow
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The main problems are in Clues 1 and 2. Clue 1 names 'medial elbow pain' during 'throwing' in a 'young baseball pitcher' — this is nearly diagnostic on its own and removes the challenge. Clue 2 then repeats that throwing causes pain and rest relieves it, adding minimal new information. The rewrite for Clue 1 removes the sport-specific language and keeps only the functional complaint (repetitive overhead activity, medial pain, progressive, rest-responsive). Clue 2 rewrite emphasizes functional loss and timeline for recovery, which is new information. Clues 3–5 form a solid clinical reasoning progression: history narrows to apophyseal injury in a young athlete; examination confirms medial-sided pathology with specific valgus/resistance findings; imaging definitively shows apophysitis. The puzzle improves significantly with rewrites to Clues 1 and 2.

POOR

The puzzle needs Clue 1 to be much broader (e.g., 'acute lower limb pain post-fall') and Clue 2 to add meaningful directional or movement-pattern information that distinguishes hip fracture from other post-fall injuries (ankle fracture, knee injury, pelvic fracture, soft tissue injury). Clues 3-5 are reasonable in specificity but arrive too late in a sequence that was already essentially 'solved' after Clue 1.

664. Quadriceps Tendinopathy
Thigh
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: MAJOR ISSUE: Clue 1 is too anatomically specific and telegraphs the diagnosis immediately by naming 'superior patellar pole' and 'palpable swelling at tendon insertion'—this essentially narrows to quadriceps/patellar tendon before any clinical reasoning occurs. A player can guess the answer from Clue 1 alone. Clue 2 adds minimal new information beyond what Clue 1 already established (same activities, same pain pattern). Clues 3-5 are well-structured and provide genuine discrimination, but the foundation is compromised. Rewrite Clue 1 to present only complaint without anatomy, and Clue 2 to define functional loss pattern more distinctly from complaint.

Complaint
✗ MAJOR

UPDATED

665. Cancer Rehabilitation
General
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle has severe structural problems. Clue 1 is already quite obvious ('post-cancer treatment'), Clue 2 adds only elaboration of Clue 1, and Clue 3 completely gives away the diagnosis by naming cancer therapy as the cause. By Clue 3, any player knows the answer is cancer rehabilitation. Clues 4 and 5 then become redundant confirmations. The progressive reveal funnel does not work—it collapses after Clue 2. The puzzle is far too easy. For a robust puzzle, Clue 1 should mention fatigue and exercise intolerance WITHOUT revealing the cause; Clue 2 should suggest cardiopulmonary involvement; Clue 3 should introduce the timeline and prior fitness level without yet naming cancer; Clue 4 should provide exam findings that differentiate cancer-related deconditioning from other cardiopulmonary or systemic etiologies; and Clue 5 should provide imaging/testing that confirms cancer treatment history and its sequelae.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easynames_answer

A patient post-cancer treatment reports pervasive fatigue and deconditioning. Exercise capacity is markedly reduced compared to pre-treatment baseline. Functional limitations impact ability to return to work and normal activities.
UPDATED
Patient reports persistent fatigue and difficulty with sustained physical activities. Reports feeling significantly weaker than before, with reduced tolerance for exertion.
💡 Patient reports fatigue and deconditioning post-cancer treatment with reduced exercise capacity and functional limitations.

UPDATED

💡 Redundant with Clue 1. Simply elaborates that exertion causes dyspnea and fatigue—already stated in Complaint. Does not narrow differential meaningfully; could apply to cardiopulmonary disease, severe deconditioning from any cause, anemia, etc.
Clue 3

✗ MAJOR
too_easynames_answerwrong_order

UPDATED

💡 BREAKTHROUGH: Explicitly names cancer treatment (chemotherapy, radiation, or surgery) as the cause. This is the critical clue that establishes etiology and essentially reveals the answer.

no_new_infotoo_generic

no_new_infowrong_order

666. Jones Fracture
Foot
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle suffers from severe redundancy and premature anatomical specificity. Clue 1 essentially names the answer by specifying 'base of the fifth metatarsal' — a physio student would immediately recognize a Jones fracture. Clue 2 merely rephrases activity limitations already stated in Clue 1. Clue 3 explicitly describes the fracture anatomy and vascular supply, removing any remaining diagnostic uncertainty before examination or imaging. Clue 4 adds almost nothing new. The progressive reveal principle is entirely violated. To fix: (1) Clue 1 should focus only on 'lateral midfoot pain in a runner, acute onset' without specifying metatarsal or metaphyseal anatomy. (2) Clue 2 should narrow to activity pattern and swelling behavior. (3) Clue 3 should introduce the timeline, mechanism (inversion vs. overuse), and slow healing response — suggesting a significant structural injury without naming it. (4) Clue 4 should provide precise palpation and functional tests that differentiate Jones fracture from stress reaction or other midfoot injuries. (5) Clue 5 should confirm the imaging.

669. Vibration White Finger
Hand
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major progressive reveal failure: Clues 1, 2, and 4 are largely repetitive, describing the same symptom sequence (blanching→rewarming pain→color recovery) without advancing the differential diagnosis. Clue 1 nearly names the answer with 'vibration white finger' terminology. Clue 2 adds no functional or temporal information beyond what Clue 1 establishes. Clue 4 redundantly confirms clinical signs already described in Clues 1–2. After Clue 1 alone, a student would reasonably narrow to VWF or similar vascular disorders affecting digits. Clues 2 and 4 fail to narrow further. The puzzle needs restructuring: Clue 1 should be more generic (episodic finger color changes), Clue 2 should focus on functional impact/occupational context, Clue 3 should establish the specific tool/exposure history, Clue 4 should introduce discriminating examination findings (capillary response, arterial insufficiency signs), and Clue 5 should confirm with imaging. Current clue order works, but content redundancy undermines the progressive reveal.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easynames_answer
ORIGINAL
A worker exposed to vibrating tools reports episodic finger blanching and color changes. Fingers become white then red and painful during rewarming. Sensory loss and numbness accompany the color changes.
UPDATED
Worker reports episodic finger blanching and color changes triggered by cold or vibration exposure. Fingers turn white, then red and painful as they rewarm. Numbness and tingling accompany episodes.
💡 Establishes episodic finger blanching with color changes (white→red), pain on rewarming, and accompanying sensory loss. Sets the core symptom pattern.
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Symptoms are triggered by exposure to vibrating equipment and cold exposure. Finger blanching occurs during or shortly after tool use. Rewarming causes pain and restoration of normal color and sensation.
UPDATED
Symptoms worsen with prolonged grip or repetitive hand tasks. Patient avoids cold environments due to severe finger reactions. Occupational exposure is unavoidable given job demands.
💡 Redundant with clue 1. Clue 1 already states symptoms are triggered by vibrating tools and cold, occur during/after tool use, and improve with rewarming. Clue 2 simply restates the same trigger-symptom-recovery sequence.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Vibration white finger develops from chronic exposure to vibrating power tools. Jackhammers, chainsaws, and pneumatic tools are common culprits. Duration and intensity of tool exposure correlate with symptom severity.
✓ No changes
💡 Adds occupational context (type of tools: jackhammers, chainsaws, pneumatic tools) and dose-response relationship (duration and intensity correlate with severity). This narrows to an occupational vascular condition.
Clue 4
Examination
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL

UPDATED
Affected fingers show poor color return after cold provocation test. Grip strength reduced in affected hand. Two-point discrimination diminished in fingertips. Pulse oximetry shows reduced oxygen saturation in affected digits compared to unaffected fingers.
💡 Redundant with clues 1 and 2. Describes visible blanching, numbness, cold sensation, and post-rewarming erythema and pain—all already stated in complaint and activity clues. No new discriminating examination findings provided.
Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Nailfold capillaroscopy demonstrates abnormal capillary patterns characteristic of vascular dysfunction. Thermography shows abnormal temperature distribution in affected fingers. Doppler ultrasound assesses digital artery flow and perfusion.
✓ No changes
💡 Provides vascular-specific imaging: nailfold capillaroscopy findings, thermography abnormalities, and Doppler assessment of digital artery flow. This confirms vascular dysfunction and rules out purely neurological or musculoskeletal causes.
670. Multiple Sclerosis
CNS
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clues 1-2 together already name the diagnosis through pathognomonic features (optic neuritis + progressive myelopathy + relapsing pattern + demyelination imaging = MS). A competent clinician reads Clue 1 and immediately narrows to MS; Clues 2-5 merely confirm rather than progressively differentiate. The puzzle conflates 'being clinically complete' with 'teaching good diagnostic reasoning.' Clue 1 should omit optic neuritis details and cognitive fog (too specific). Clue 2 should omit 'optic neuritis' terminology. Clue 5 contains examination-level specificity (VEP, oligoclonal bands) before imaging details are introduced, violating clue order logic. Rewrite needed: make Clue 1 boarder (non-specific leg weakness + vision loss), Clue 2 functional/regional (lower limb dysfunction + eye problem), Clue 3 relapsing history (key MS discriminator), Clue 4 demyelination signs (hyperreflexia, Babinski, etc.), Clue 5 imaging confirmation.

671. Deep Vein Thrombosis
Lower Limb
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: MAJOR PROBLEMS: (1) Clue 1 is far too specific — it names 'edema', 'pitting edema', 'warmth', and 'constant pain worse with weight bearing', which together almost uniquely point to DVT in a physio context. A student would narrow dramatically after Clue 1 alone. (2) Clues 1 and 2 are heavily redundant — both emphasize weight bearing causing pain, swelling, and functional limitation. Clue 2 adds almost nothing new except 'elevation helps', which is obvious and doesn't differentiate from cellulitis, Baker's cyst rupture, or post-thrombotic syndrome. (3) Clue 3 is strong and adds genuine risk-factor information that wasn't in earlier clues, but by then the diagnosis is already clear. (4) Clue 4 repeats examination signs already heavily implied by Clue 1 ('warmth', 'edema', pain on movement). The mention of Homan's sign and calf pain on dorsiflexion are useful discriminators, but the edema/warmth component is redundant. PROGRESSIVE REVEAL: The sequence collapses early. Clue 1 should be much vaguer (remove 'warm', remove 'edematous' or at least remove 'pitting', remove weight-bearing link). Clue 2 should focus on novel functional impact, not restate symptom exacerbation. The puzzle would be stronger if Clues 1–2 cast a wider differential (could include cellulitis, Baker's cyst, crush injury, compartment syndrome, venous insufficiency), with Clues 3–4 narrowing to DVT specifically.

💡 Sudden onset lower limb swelling and pain with warmth and edema; pain worse with weight bearing

💡 Redundant with Clue 1 — weight bearing exacerbates pain (already stated in Clue 1), elevation helps (marginal new detail), mobility limited by pain/swelling (restates the complaint)

Pain is triggered by running activity with friction from chest wall clothing. Distance running worsens symptoms with longer running duration. Pain improves with rest and adequate wound care.
UPDATED
Pain is specifically aggravated by prolonged running distance and improves with rest. Symptoms worsen in warm weather and with worn or poorly fitting athletic tops.
💡 Redundant with Clue 1. Reiterates that running causes pain, friction from clothing is the trigger, and rest helps. Adds only 'distance running worsens it' but this is already implied by 'during or after running' in Clue 1.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Runner's nipple occurs from repetitive friction between clothing and chest wall. Male runners are more commonly affected than females. Increased running volume and intensity increase symptom risk.
✓ No changes
💡 Introduces mechanism (repetitive friction, clothing-related), demographics (male predominance), and risk factors (increased volume/intensity). This meaningfully narrows from 'chest pain in runners' to friction-based irritation.

⚠ MINOR
no_new_info

💡 Objective findings: visible irritation, scabbing, bleeding, callus, areolar thickening, point tenderness. This is appropriate specificity but partially overlaps with Clue 1 complaint description (bleeding and callus already mentioned).
Clue 5

✗ MAJOR
too_hard

💡 States imaging is not needed and that diagnosis is clinical. This is correct but not useful—it tells the player 'imaging won't help you' rather than providing confirmatory findings. Imaging clue should be definitive or should explain why imaging adds nothing.
673. Posterior Ankle Impingement
Ankle & Foot
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: MAJOR ISSUES: Clue 1 is too anatomically specific ('posterior ankle compartment', 'plantarflexion and inversion') — this immediately narrows to posterior ankle pathology and essentially names the region. A ballet dancer with posterior ankle pain on plantarflexion should trigger 'posterior impingement' in any experienced clinician's mind by Clue 2. Clues 1, 2, and 3 are highly redundant: they all emphasize plantarflexion/dance demands/posterior location without adding distinct new information. The differential is never truly open; by Clue 1, posterior ankle impingement is already the obvious answer. Clue 3 adds no new information beyond what 1+2 already established (intensity, duration, and mechanism are all implied by a ballet dancer with plantarflexion pain). To improve: make Clue 1 much broader (just posterior ankle pain in an athlete), use Clue 2 to functionally characterize (pointe work specifically), use Clue 3 to establish exacerbating factors/timeline/prior history (rules out acute trauma, prior episodes), use Clue 4 to differentiate posterior impingement from posterior ankle OA, FHL tenosynovitis, or Achilles pathology (via palpation, ROM, specific movement patterns), and use Clue 5 to confirm the impingement anatomy on imaging. Currently the puzzle is too easy by Clue 2.

NEEDS WORK

The progressive reveal funnel is broken early. Recommend: (1) Rewrite Clue 1 to describe 'leg pain' without specifying dermatomal features or mentioning numbness/tingling. (2) Rewrite Clue 2 to add distinct functional information about posture/timing. (3) Keep Clue 3 as-is. (4) Rewrite Clue 4 to include specific motor weakness pattern and reflex findings that differentiate nerve root level rather than repeating dermatomal language.

Ankle & Foot
POOR

Rewrite needed to: (1) make Clue 1 broad and symptom-focused only, (2) use Clue 2 to establish functional pattern (not just repeat movement triggers), (3) use Clue 3 to introduce mechanism and history (not pathology naming), (4) use Clue 4 to provide discriminatory exam findings that differentiate from anterior ankle pain of other etiologies (syndesmotic injury, anterior ankle impingement vs OCD, etc.), and (5) reserve Clue 5 for imaging confirmation.

678. Facet Joint Syndrome
Spine
POOR
Review Notes: CRITICAL ISSUES: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clues 1, 2, and 3 all repeat or rephrase the same clinical information — movement-pattern-dependent pain (extension/rotation aggravating, flexion relieving) and degeneration/aging as mechanisms. Clue 1 uses the term 'facet joint region' and Clue 3 uses 'facet joint syndrome' outright, which essentially names the answer before the player has made a single guess. Clue 4 again repeats extension/rotation findings from Clue 1 without adding new discriminatory information. A player reading only Clue 1 already knows the diagnosis is facet-mediated; there is no clinical reasoning funnel — just repetition. The puzzle needs to be restructured: Clue 1 should describe isolated back pain without movement direction or anatomy hints; Clue 2 should narrow to extension-biased movements and functional loss; Clue 3 should add demographic/timeline context; Clue 4 should provide specific examination findings that distinguish from other spine conditions (e.g., negative neural tension tests, preserved flexion ROM, segmental tenderness); Clue 5 should confirm with imaging. As written, this is unsuitable for a Wordle-style game.

679. Vasculitis
Blood Vessels
POOR

Clues 2 and 3 add almost nothing new — they just rephrase Clue 1's information as functional limitation and historical context without true narrowing. Clue 4 repeats the same findings already established. A physio student would diagnose vasculitis from Clue 1 alone and have no reason to read further. The game has no differential funnel and no 'aha' moments. Rewritten clues should: (1) start broadly with systemic illness, (2) narrow to lower limb + joint involvement, (3) add temporal/precipitant history, (4) provide specific exam findings (purpura distribution, abdominal signs), (5) confirm with labs and biopsy.

680. Hip Labral Tear
Hip
NEEDS WORK

Rewrite Clues 1 and 2 to properly funnel: Clue 1 broad (groin pain, insidious onset, some mechanical feel), Clue 2 functional specificity (cutting/pivoting, deep flexion/IR position). This makes the game appropriately progressive.
