723. Neuropathic Pain
Whole Body
POOR
Review Notes: Major structural problem: Clue 1 immediately names 'diabetic neuropathy' and 'neuropathic pain,' eliminating the diagnostic mystery entirely. A physio student reading only Clue 1 already knows the answer. Clues 2–3 largely reinforce what was already established (diabetic patient with neuropathic pain in a stocking distribution). The progressive reveal is essentially flat — each clue adds clinical detail but does not meaningfully narrow a broad differential. The puzzle should start with just the complaint (distal burning pain, worse at night, triggered by touch) without disclosing diabetes or neuropathy explicitly, forcing the player to generate a real differential. Clues 2–3 would then build in diabetes and glycemic control as contextual narrowing. Clue 4 (examination) would clinch the stocking sensory loss. Clue 5 (NCS/EMG) would confirm. As written, this is a teaching case, not a diagnostic puzzle.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easynames_answer
ORIGINAL
A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes for 12 years presents with progressive burning pain in the feet extending into the lower legs, beginning distally and advancing proximally. Pain quality is described as burning, electrical, or 'pins and needles.' Diabetic neuropathy is the primary consideration.
UPDATED
A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes for 12 years presents with progressive pain in the feet and lower legs over the past 8 months. Pain is described as burning, electrical, or 'pins and needles' with a distal-to-proximal pattern.
💡 Establishes diabetic patient with progressive distal burning/electrical pain in feet and lower legs
Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Pain is worse at night and is significantly aggravated by touch or light clothing contact (allodynia). Activities requiring prolonged standing or walking exacerbate symptoms. Cool, moist conditions provide temporary relief by reducing allodynia.
UPDATED
Light touch or clothing contact triggers sharp pain out of proportion to stimulus severity. Symptoms are notably worse at night and wake the patient from sleep. Prolonged standing or walking significantly exacerbates discomfort.
💡 Establishes functional limitation (standing/walking exacerbates); introduces allodynia and temporal pattern (worse at night)
Clue 3
History
⚠ MINOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Glycemic control has been suboptimal with HbA1c ranging 7.8-8.5% over past 2 years. The patient also takes medication for hypertension. No significant trauma to the feet is reported. Symptoms began insidiously 8 months ago.
UPDATED
Glycemic control has been inconsistent (HbA1c 7.8–8.5% over 2 years). Patient is also hypertensive. No preceding injury or trauma to the feet. Symptoms began insidiously without clear trigger.
💡 Adds glycemic control status (suboptimal) and hypertension; confirms insidious 8-month onset; rules out trauma
Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Sharp-dull discrimination is diminished in the feet bilaterally with a stocking distribution. Vibration sense is reduced at the ankles. Monofilament testing (10g) shows loss of sensation in the plantar surface. Temperature sensation is mildly altered. Reflexes are diminished at the ankles. Motor strength is normal.
✓ No changes
💡 Provides objective sensory deficits (sharp-dull, vibration, monofilament, temperature) in stocking distribution; diminished reflexes; normal motor strength
Clue 5
Imaging
✗ MAJOR
incoherent
ORIGINAL
Nerve conduction studies demonstrate slowing of both motor and sensory nerve conduction velocities in lower extremities consistent with demyelination. EMG shows mild denervation changes in distal lower limb muscles. The DN4 neuropathic pain questionnaire score is elevated. Imaging confirms peripheral neuropathy pattern.
UPDATED
Nerve conduction studies show reduced motor and sensory conduction velocities in bilateral lower extremities consistent with demyelination. EMG demonstrates mild denervation in distal foot muscles. DN4 neuropathic pain questionnaire score is elevated, supporting neuropathic origin.
💡 Confirms demyelination pattern on NCS/EMG; DN4 score; definitively establishes peripheral neuropathy diagnosis
724. Cognitive Impairment
Brain
POOR
Review Notes: CRITICAL ISSUES: (1) Clue 1 explicitly names 'cognitive impairment' in the statement 'Early-stage cognitive impairment must be formally assessed' — this gives away the answer immediately. (2) Clues 1 and 2 are heavily redundant: both describe the same memory loss and functional decline without meaningful new discrimination. (3) Clue 2 does not narrow the differential beyond what Clue 1 already established; it elaborates the same complaints rather than shifting perspective to functional impact that would eliminate non-cognitive diagnoses. (4) Clue 3 introduces vascular risk factors and TIA history, which is appropriate NEW information and begins to point toward etiology (vascular vs. neurodegenerative). (5) Clues 4 and 5 are appropriate in specificity but by this point the diagnosis is already essentially given away. The progressive reveal has collapsed by Clue 1. A player should not be able to guess the answer from the complaint alone.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
too_easynames_answer

725. Colles Fracture
Wrist & Hand
POOR
Review Notes: CRITICAL FLAWS: Clue 1 essentially solves the puzzle by stating 'distal radius fracture is the anticipated diagnosis' — this defeats the entire game. The clue also contains examination-level detail ('dinner fork' deformity would belong in Clue 4), making it informationally cluttered. Clues 2 and 3 add only marginal new information — Clue 2 largely rephrases the functional loss already implied by immediate pain and deformity in Clue 1, and Clue 3 adds contextual risk factors without significantly narrowing a differential that's already been named. The progressive reveal principle completely breaks down: Clues 1–3 do not funnel a player through differential reasoning; instead, Clue 1 gives the answer away, and subsequent clues merely elaborate. Clues 4–5 are well-constructed (specific exam findings, clear imaging), but by then the game is over. RECOMMENDATION: Rewrite Clue 1 to present only the complaint (age, FOOSH, immediate pain, swelling) without mechanism detail or diagnostic hint. Restructure Clues 2–3 to provide functional, then historical narrowing that builds toward wrist/hand fracture as a category. This puzzle needs fundamental restructuring to work as a game.

730. Jones Fracture
Foot
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fails the progressive reveal principle catastrophically. 732. Costochondritis
Thoracic
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: The puzzle has significant issues with progressive revelation. Clue 1 essentially names the answer by specifically mentioning 'costochondral junction' and the exact anatomical site — a physio student reading only this would immediately narrow to costochondritis. Clues 2, 3, and 4 largely rephrase or elaborate on information already established rather than introducing genuinely new discriminators. UPDATED
Movements that increase chest wall tension or rib cage excursion aggravate symptoms, particularly heavy lifting and repetitive pushing. Sleeping on the left side is uncomfortable. Symptoms improve with arm support and reduced upper body activity.
💡 Functional limitations and mechanical triggers: deep inspiration, coughing, exertion (lifting, push-ups), and positional aggravation (lying on affected side). Relief with rest and shallow breathing.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

✓ No changes
💡 Temporal link to recent upper respiratory infection with cough (2 months ago); cough resolved but pain persists. Absence of direct trauma. Recurrent nature (prior episode 3 years ago, 6-week duration).

⚠ MINOR
no_new_info

UPDATED

UPDATED

733. Generalised Joint Hypermobility
Systemic
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: This puzzle has CRITICAL ISSUES with the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 essentially names the answer ('hypermobility,' 'double-jointed,' 'systemic connective tissue disorder') — a player reads it and immediately thinks 'hypermobility syndrome/EDS.' Clue 2 adds minimal new info beyond what Clue 1 already established (instability + pain + activity-linked symptoms). Clue 3 reiterates hypermobility features and adds family history, but still doesn't distinguish GJH from EDS or other syndromic forms. Clue 4 (Beighton score) is the first objective discriminator, but by then the answer is already obvious. Clue 5 attempts to narrow further but still leaves EDS in the differential. The puzzle needs: (1) a broader, symptom-focused Clue 1 that doesn't name hypermobility, (2) Clues 2–3 that narrow to the joint region/movement pattern WITHOUT assuming systemic disease, (3) Clue 4 that introduces the Beighton score as the key finding, (4) Clue 5 that rules out syndromic forms (normal imaging, negative genetic testing) to land on primary GJH. Current difficulty is TOO EASY for guessing, yet the clinical story is incoherent because systemic features (family history, bruising, wound healing) are mixed in without clearly distinguishing GJH from EDS.
Clue 2
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
names_answertoo_easy

UPDATED

💡 Lifetime joint instability with frequent dislocations (shoulders, fingers), diffuse joint pain worse with activity, childhood onset of hypermobility ('double-jointed')
Clue 3

no_new_info

UPDATED
Patient cannot sustain repetitive fine motor activities (typing, playing instruments) without pain and apprehension. General exercise tolerance is preserved, but dynamic multi-joint movements (jumping, pivoting, contact sports) consistently provoke instability episodes.
💡 Repetitive motions (typing, instruments) aggravate pain; joint instability during movement creates apprehension; sports participation limited by recurrent injuries and dislocations

UPDATED

734. Biceps Tendinopathy
Shoulder
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Overall structure is sound: Clue 1 establishes broad differential for anterior shoulder pain in an overhead athlete. Clue 2 adds functional context but overlaps with anatomy-specific language already present in Clue 1 (anterior shoulder, resisted motions). Clue 3 effectively narrows via mechanism (repetitive overhead, gradual) and adds rotator cuff context. Clue 4 is the true discriminator with Speed's and Yergason's tests — excellent differentiation. Clue 5 confirms via imaging. Minor redundancy in Clue 2 (functionally it doesn't add much beyond what Clue 1 already implied) and marginal overlap in Clue 5 regarding subacromial involvement. The puzzle is clinically coherent and follows a reasonable diagnostic funnel, but Clue 2 could be tightened to focus purely on functional loss without re-specifying anatomical movement patterns.
Clue 1
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
A 52-year-old recreational tennis player presents with anterior shoulder pain for 3 weeks. Pain began during overhead serve motion. Reports occasional clicking sensation in shoulder during reaching.
✓ No changes
💡 Age, sport context, onset timing (3 weeks, during specific motion), location (anterior shoulder), and symptom character (pain + clicking). Establishes broad differential for anterior shoulder pain in overhead athlete.
Clue 2
Activity
⚠ MINOR
no_new_info
ORIGINAL
Sharp anterior shoulder pain with overhead activities, serving, and throwing motions. Pain exacerbated by resisted elbow flexion and supination. Discomfort improves with rest and ice. Patient experiences pain at night when lying on affected shoulder.
UPDATED
Cannot tolerate overhead throwing or serving. Pain worsens with sustained reaching above shoulder height. Symptoms improve partially with rest and ice, but night pain disturbs sleep, particularly when lying on the affected side.
💡 Functional impact (overhead activities, serving, throwing), directional movement pattern (overhead+resisted flexion/supination), and response to management (improves with rest/ice). Narrows region but clue already specifies 'anterior shoulder' and 'resisted elbow flexion' — this is getting anatomy-specific.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Repetitive overhead sports participation (tennis 4x/week) for past year. Gradual onset suggesting tendinopathy rather than acute tear. No prior shoulder surgery. Underlying rotator cuff dysfunction noted on screening examination.
✓ No changes

Clue 4
Examination
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Speed's test (supinated elbow flexion against resistance) reproduces anterior shoulder pain. Yergason test (supinated forearm with elbow flexion) also positive. Palpable tenderness over bicipital groove. Apprehension test negative. Rotator cuff strength 4+/5 suggesting dynamic instability.
✓ No changes
💡 Specific special tests (Speed's, Yergason's — both positive for biceps), palpation finding (bicipital groove tenderness), negative apprehension test (rules out anterior instability), and rotator cuff weakness (4+/5). This is the key discriminator — Speed's and Yergason's are highly suggestive of biceps tendinopathy specifically.
Clue 5
Imaging
⚠ MINOR
no_new_info

UPDATED
Ultrasound demonstrates biceps tendon thickening (5mm) with hypoechoic echotexture and increased Doppler signal indicating active inflammation. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration of the proximal biceps tendon with intact fibres and no full-thickness tear.
💡 Ultrasound findings (tendon thickening 5mm, hypoechoic signal, increased Doppler vascularity) and MRI confirmation (intrasubstance degeneration, no full tear). These are highly specific imaging markers for biceps tendinopathy. However, the mention of 'subacromial space narrowed' is slightly redundant with Clue 3's 'subacromial impingement' inference.
735. Myositis Ossificans
Whole Body
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major problem: Clue 1 explicitly mentions 'heterotopic ossification must be considered'—this names the diagnosis and collapses the entire differential before any guessing begins. Clue 5 also reinforces heterotopic ossification. The puzzle is essentially given away at the start. Clue 2 largely rephrases ROM information from Clue 1 without adding substantially new clinical data. Clue 3 elaborates on mechanism already stated in Clue 1 (elbow fracture-dislocation) without materially narrowing the differential beyond what was already disclosed. The progressive reveal principle is violated: by Clue 1, a player already knows the answer. Rewrite Clue 1 to withhold the heterotopic ossification hint; rewrite Clue 5 to name the finding explicitly. Clue 3 should add information about timeline and comorbidities that make HO more likely (e.g., high-energy trauma, significant soft tissue injury, post-operative day timing), not just repeat the mechanism.

736. Acromioclavicular Joint Sprain
Shoulder
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major problem: Clue 1 names the anatomical location (acromioclavicular joint) too explicitly and describes the functional pattern clearly enough that experienced clinicians will guess AC sprain immediately. The differential should be broader at clue 1 — shoulder pain after trauma could be rotator cuff injury, labral tear, fracture, or other pathology. Clue 2 adds little beyond what clue 1 already established about movement patterns; both clues focus on the same adduction-based functional limitation. Clue 3 is well-positioned. Clue 4 is overly redundant with clue 1 — tenderness over the AC joint and crossover adduction test pain are already implied by clue 1's statement that pain is 'localized to the superior aspect' and triggered by 'crossing the arm over the body.' Clue 5 is appropriate. Progressive reveal is weak because clues 1 and 2 are too similar, and clue 4 doesn't discriminate well. Rewrite clue 1 to be deliberately vague about anatomy, and rewrite clue 4 to add examination findings that differentiate AC joint injury from other shoulder pathologies (e.g., negative supine external rotation test ruling out posterior labral pathology, or specific clavicular position findings).

737. Wound Dehiscence Post-Surgical
General
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle has critical progressive reveal failures. Clue 1 essentially names the answer by explicitly stating 'partial separation of the incision site' — a direct synonym for wound dehiscence. A player reading only Clue 1 would have one obvious candidate, eliminating the entire game mechanic. Clues 2 and 3 largely rephrase information already given (separation, drainage, timing, risk factors) without adding meaningfully new discriminatory information. Clue 4 is appropriate but arrives too late. The puzzle should start broader (complaint only: acute pain + drainage at surgical site, post-op context) and progressively reveal the separation finding and imaging correlation. The current design violates the fundamental 'progressive reveal' principle.

739. Shoulder Arthroscopy
Shoulder
POOR
Review Notes: CRITICAL FAILURE: This puzzle fundamentally misunderstands the game's purpose. The 'answer' is not a diagnosis but a surgical PROCEDURE ('Shoulder Arthroscopy'). This is not a physiotherapy diagnosis — it's a surgical intervention. Physiodle should ask players to diagnose CONDITIONS (e.g., subacromial impingement, rotator cuff tear, frozen shoulder), not procedures. Additionally, Clue 1 explicitly names the surgery ('arthroscopic subacromial decompression'), which immediately reveals the answer. The entire sequence fails the progressive reveal principle: Clues 1-3 are largely redundant, Clue 4 rephrases ROM already mentioned, and Clue 5 just confirms the surgery was done well. There is no meaningful narrowing funnel. The puzzle needs a complete conceptual rebuild: either (1) ask the player to diagnose the POST-OPERATIVE COMPLICATION (e.g., post-operative stiffness, unresolved impingement, adhesive capsulitis), not the surgery itself, or (2) reframe to diagnose the PRE-OPERATIVE condition (subacromial impingement) without revealing the surgical management in Clue 1.

💡 Post-operative status (2 weeks), persistent pain, limited ROM after subacromial decompression

UPDATED

💡 Specific ROM limitations: abduction 90°, ER restricted, inability to reach overhead or behind back

no_new_inforedundant_with_clue_0wrong_order

740. Thoracic Disc Herniation
Thoracic Spine
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Primary issue: Clue 1 is far too specific. It explicitly names 'thoracic spine' anatomy, mentions 'girdle-like distribution' (a quasi-pathognomonic sign for thoracic myelopathy), and instructs to 'assess myelopathic features' — essentially telegraphing the answer before any guessing. A player reading only Clue 1 would narrow the differential dramatically and have minimal work to do. Clue 3 is also slightly redundant with information already implied by Clue 2 (progressive, worsening nature). The puzzle is well-structured in terms of exam findings (Clue 4) and imaging (Clue 5), but the early clues need to cast a wider net and withhold anatomical specificity until later.
Clue 2

741. Labyrinthitis
Inner Ear
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Major progressive reveal failures: (1) Clue 1 is too specific and names the diagnosis indirectly ('vestibular pathology must be confirmed' + rotatory/horizontal vertigo + post-URI essentially equals labyrinthitis). (2) Clue 2 merely elaborates functional consequences already clear from Clue 1 without narrowing the differential further—both audiomotor vertigo and BPPV cause head-movement intolerance. (3) Clue 3 repeats URI timeline and adds hearing loss, but this information should have been in Clue 1 or 2 to build the story progressively. (4) Clue 4 mixes inconsistent findings: Dix-Hallpike is a BPPV test and should not typically reproduce true nystagmus in labyrinthitis; Weber/Rinne findings (conductive shift) are inconsistent with labyrinthitis (which causes sensorineural loss). (5) The puzzle conflates vestibular and audiological findings without clear clinical coherence. Rewrite needed to: remove 'vestibular pathology must be confirmed' from Clue 1; move hearing loss to Clue 1 or 2 to build differential narrowing; remove Dix-Hallpike from Clue 4 (not consistent with labyrinthitis); correct Weber/Rinne to show sensorineural pattern (Weber away from affected side, not toward it).

748. Muscular Dystrophy
Muscle
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. Clue 1 explicitly names X-linked Duchenne muscular dystrophy—there is no differential diagnosis left to narrow. The game is essentially over before the player guesses. Clues 2–4 are merely elaborations and confirmations of a diagnosis already stated. Clue 5 adds multiple diagnostic tests but the diagnosis was already named in Clue 1, making all subsequent clues redundant. The puzzle needs a complete restructuring: Clue 1 should present only broad functional complaints (frequent falls, difficulty running/climbing) in a 5-year-old without mentioning dystrophy, inheritance, or pathognomonic signs. Clues 2–4 should progressively narrow through activity limitations, family history, and exam findings (weakness pattern, calf enlargement, Gowers sign). Clue 5 should provide confirmatory tests. As written, this is essentially a 'confirm the diagnosis' exercise, not a diagnostic puzzle.

752. Overactive Bladder
Pelvis
NEEDS WORK

Clues 2–4 repeat the same functional/symptom information without adding discriminatory value. The differential should include: medication-induced (diuretic), BPH with outlet obstruction, urinary tract infection, neurogenic bladder, and true OAB. To fix: Clue 1 should be much vaguer ('patient reports bothersome lower urinary tract symptoms'), Clue 2 should describe functional impact (sleep, social limitation), Clue 3 should introduce medication history as a confounding factor, Clue 4 should include urodynamic findings that CONFIRM detrusor overactivity (not just ruled-out causes), and Clue 5 should differentiate OAB from BPH-outlet obstruction or medication effect. Currently, a player can guess OAB from Clue 1 alone.

757. Piriformis Syndrome
Hip
NEEDS WORK

A player reading Clue 1 would immediately know the diagnosis. Clues 3 and 5 are also weak because they add little NEW information beyond what Clues 2 and 4 already established—they mostly elaborate or confirm rather than narrow the differential. The puzzle lacks a true progressive funnel: by Clue 2, an experienced clinician would already suspect piriformis syndrome (sitting-aggravated buttock pain with hip IR reproduction). Clues 3–5 should add concrete differentiators (e.g., specific ROM deficits, neurological findings, or imaging specifics) that rule out mimics like gluteal tendinopathy, sacroiliac joint dysfunction, or L5/S1 radiculopathy. 758. Chronic Exertional Compartment Syndrome
Lower Leg
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Progressive reveal is compromised by Clue 2 being almost entirely redundant with Clue 1. Both describe the same complaint (bilateral anterior shin pain during running, resolves with rest) without meaningfully narrowing the differential. Clue 2 should pivot to FUNCTIONAL LIMITATIONS or MOVEMENT PATTERNS not yet covered (e.g., what activities can/cannot be done, or how the limitation differs from other shin pain syndromes). Clues 3–5 are well-ordered and appropriately specific. After Clue 1 alone, a clinician should be thinking: shin splints, stress fracture, compartment syndrome, tibialis anterior strain, peroneal nerve irritation, exertional posterior tibial syndrome—at least 6+ conditions. Clue 2 as written adds almost nothing; Clue 3 narrows to overuse syndromes. Clue 4 is the true discriminator (compartment pressure abnormality post-exercise). Rewrite Clue 2 to add distinct functional information.
Clue 1
Complaint
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
A runner reports bilateral anterior shin pain that occurs predictably during running activity. Pain develops after consistent running distance and resolves completely with rest. The condition recurs with return to running at similar intensity.
✓ No changes

Clue 2
Activity
✗ MAJOR
no_new_info

UPDATED

Symptoms do not occur with sport-specific drills performed at low speed, only with sustained endurance running.
💡 Redundant with Clue 1. Repeats that pain occurs during running, bilateral/symmetric, resolves within 15-30 min of rest. No new functional limitation or movement pattern information beyond what Clue 1 already stated.
Clue 3
History
✓ OK
ORIGINAL

Clue 4

Clue 5

761. Osteoporotic Vertebral Compression Fracture
Spine
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle has severe progressive reveal problems. Clue 1 essentially names the diagnosis by explicitly stating 'osteoporosis,' 'compression fracture must be ruled out urgently,' plus the complete clinical context (postmenopausal woman, mechanism, localization). A player reading only Clue 1 has already arrived at the answer. Clues 2–5 are primarily elaborations and confirmations rather than new narrowing information. The differential funnel never narrows because the answer is already revealed in the complaint. To fix this, Clue 1 must be rewritten to remove the named diagnosis and imaging-level specificity, presenting only symptom complaint without the clinical history that gives away osteoporosis risk status. The remaining clues then need restructuring to add meaningful new dimensions (functional impact in 2, risk factors/timeline in 3, objective exam findings that exclude mimics in 4, imaging confirmation in 5).

763. Complex Regional Pain Syndrome
Limbs
POOR
Review Notes: Critical failure: Clue 1 explicitly names 'Complex regional pain syndrome type I'—this is a complete giveaway and violates the core game mechanic. The puzzle is unsolvable as intended because the answer is revealed in the opening clue. Additionally, Clues 2, 4, and 5 become redundant once CRPS is named—they merely elaborate on CRPS signs without narrowing a differential that no longer exists. The progressive reveal principle is entirely absent. Clue 1 must be rewritten to describe only symptoms and context (pain, swelling, discoloration, allodynia, post-operative timing) without naming or strongly implying the diagnosis. This would allow Clues 2–5 to meaningfully narrow the differential from a reasonable set of post-operative complications (infection, compartment syndrome, DVT, reflex sympathetic dystrophy, standard post-operative pain syndrome) down to CRPS through functional, historical, and examination findings. The puzzle cannot function in its current form.
Clue 1
Complaint
✗ MAJOR
names_answertoo_easy

💡 Establishes post-operative wrist fracture context, disproportionate pain, burning quality, and allodynia; BUT explicitly names 'Complex regional pain syndrome type I' which gives away the answer immediately.
Clue 2
Activity

no_new_info

UPDATED
Protective posturing of the hand. Any light touch triggers severe pain; active movement attempts provoke intense pain and patient spontaneously avoids use. Patient holds hand immobilized and has withdrawn from rehabilitation participation.
💡 Describes allodynia and protective posturing; demonstrates functional impact with inability to use hand and pain with attempted movement.
Clue 3

✓ OK

no_new_info

Vasomotor instability is evident with color variation. Skin appears shiny and trophic changes include thin, fragile appearance. Allodynia is prominent with severe pain to light touch.
UPDATED
Hand demonstrates marked edema, warmth, and erythema with cyanotic discoloration. Profuse sweating noted. Skin is glossy, thin, and fragile with visible trophic changes. Allodynia confirmed—even light pressure elicits severe pain disproportionate to stimulus.

no_new_info

Shoulder
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Main issue: Clue 1 is far too specific and essentially names the diagnosis ('anterior shoulder instability', 'fear of dislocation', overhead athlete + throwing + apprehension). A physio student reading only Clue 1 would immediately narrow to anterior GH instability—defeating the purpose of a 5-clue game. Clue 1 should present only the complaint (episodic apprehension during throwing) without the mechanism or the answer-adjacent framing. Clue 3 then reveals the dislocation history, which is the key discriminator. Clue 4 has some redundancy with Clue 2 (apprehension test again reproduces apprehension), so minor rewrite adds specificity (ROM measurements, load-and-shift) to distinguish from other instability patterns (posterior, multidirectional). Otherwise the progressive reveal is solid: Complaint → Activity → History (confirms instability type) → Exam (special tests) → Imaging (labral/bone pathology). Rewrite Clue 1 to be genuinely broad (symptom-focused, no anatomy-specific language), and minor tightening of Clue 4 for non-redundancy.

765. Cerebral Palsy - Ataxic
Whole Body
POOR

This violates the progressive reveal principle entirely — a player reading only Clue 1 has essentially solved the puzzle. 766. Post-Laminectomy
Spine
NEEDS WORK
Review Notes: Clue 1 severely undermines the puzzle by naming the answer ('post-laminectomy syndrome must be assessed'). Remove this phrase entirely. Clue 3 is robust and narrows well. The differential after clues 1-3 should realistically include: incomplete decompression, recurrent stenosis, post-operative adhesions, foraminal stenosis at adjacent level, and post-laminectomy syndrome. Clue 4 should narrow this further with specific examination findings; instead it mostly confirms what we already know. Clue 5 does excellent work eliminating anatomical failure. Overall structure is sound but Clue 1 naming is a critical flaw.

Clue 5
Imaging
✓ OK
ORIGINAL
Heel radiographs show fracture of the calcaneus, typically intra-articular with loss of the subtalar joint space. CT of the foot shows the fracture pattern, number of fragments, degree of comminution, and extent of articular involvement. Imaging determines if conservative or surgical treatment is appropriate based on fracture severity and displacement.
✓ No changes
💡 Radiographs and CT confirm fracture location, pattern, comminution, articular involvement, and guide treatment. This is appropriately specific and definitive for calcaneal fracture, though it does not distinguish calcaneal fracture from other heel injuries (e.g., severe plantar fasciitis, stress fracture) as clearly as it could — the imaging is more about guiding treatment than confirming diagnosis at this stage.

Clues 1–4 are heavily repetitive, each restating the same clinical picture (left leg pain, sciatic distribution, neurological deficit, failed recovery post-op) without meaningful narrowing. The differential between failed surgical decompression (scar adhesions, missed disc, wrong level), recurrent herniation, and post-surgical nerve sensitisation is never established or progressively eliminated. By Clue 3, a player already knows: age, post-op status, site, pre-op imaging, and outcome—nothing new is added in Clue 2 or 3 that wasn't already in Clue 1. Clue 4 repeats pain distribution and neurological findings already described. Only Clue 5 adds concrete diagnostic information (MRI confirmation of recurrence vs adequate decompression). This puzzle lacks progressive reveal—it broadcasts the diagnosis immediately and then reiterates the same findings. Rewrite needed: Remove diagnostic speculation from Clue 1. Make Clue 2 focus on functional pattern. Use Clue 3 to establish mechanism/timeline and prior comorbidities. Use Clue 4 to systematically differentiate between surgical failure phenotypes. Use Clue 5 to confirm imaging-based diagnosis.

775. Work-Related Upper Limb Disorder
Upper Limb
POOR
Review Notes: This puzzle fundamentally fails the progressive reveal principle. The answer ('Work-Related Upper Limb Disorder') is essentially named in Clue 1, which eliminates any diagnostic challenge. All five clues repeat the same generic themes (repetitive work, pain, functional limitation, work-related) without meaningfully narrowing from a broad differential. A player reading only Clue 1 already knows the diagnosis is occupational/repetitive strain—Clues 2-4 add almost nothing new. Clue 4 and 5 remain too vague to discriminate between specific conditions (carpal tunnel, lateral epicondylitis, thoracic outlet, de Quervain's, etc.). The puzzle needs a complete overhaul: Clue 1 should describe only symptoms without occupational language; Clue 2 should narrow to a specific body region/movement pattern; Clue 3 should establish the mechanism; Clue 4 should provide examination findings that differentiate between remaining candidates (e.g., Phalen's test positive, Tinel's sign, intrinsic muscle atrophy); Clue 5 should confirm with imaging (e.g., nerve conduction studies showing median nerve compression at carpal tunnel). Currently, this puzzle is unsolvable in the intended way because the answer is given away too early.
